Harbor project access capabilities restrict access to the sensitive data that is used to train and tune models. Model endpoints support authentication through harbor-pull-secret-readonly to control access to container images stored in private registries, and Harbor model retrieval requires certificate-based trust in addition to credential authentication.

Internal service-to-service authentication is addressed at the component level. PAIS authenticates to the OpenTelemetry Collector using a Kubernetes Secret containing Authorization Bearer tokens and custom headers, and the LLM Traces flow uses the same pattern with semi-colon separated headers. The PAIS MCP server supports custom HTTP headers including User-Agent and Authorization headers for API authentication, and remote MCP servers can optionally use static authentication tokens. PAIS integrates with Grafana for metrics visualization using OIDC provider authentication.

For Knowledge Base data sources, PAIS supports authentication to Confluence using either a username and password or a personal access token, configured according to the Confluence administrator's settings. Personal access tokens are generated from user account settings and used for programmatic access to Knowledge Bases and data sources. Microsoft SharePoint data sources require username and password authentication, with support limited to SharePoint On-Premises deployments.

Deep Learning VMs deployed through self-service catalog items in VCF Automation, when configured with the PyTorch NGC software bundle, support optional JupyterLab authentication activation for interactive notebook access.

The underlying user accounts, credential storage, password policies, and OIDC provider administration are owned by VCF SSO or an external identity provider. PAIS configures and enforces authentication using those systems but does not manage authenticator lifecycle itself.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides several mechanisms for managing authenticators across both the administrative plane and the database layer, supporting the secure credential management aspect of this control.

For administrative access to the DSM platform, the system supports two authentication paths: local user accounts authenticated by email ID and password, and LDAPS-integrated accounts authenticated by matching the email domain against a registered LDAPS server. When directory service credentials are non-functional, DSM falls back to local user authentication, maintaining access continuity. Administrators configure and manage these authentication sources through the DSM Admin UI, which also provides a Permissions view for creating, monitoring, and managing configured users. LDAPS integration supports certificate-based validation of the directory service connection, with administrators able to upload directory service server certificates through the Admin UI to establish trusted connections. LDAP groups can be assigned roles within DSM, enabling group-based access control aligned with directory service group membership.

At the database layer, DSM supports directory service authentication for PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, and MinIO databases. For PostgreSQL, host-based authentication (HBA) rules determine how clients are authenticated, using ordered matching rules that evaluate connection type, client address, requested database, and user name. The default HBA configuration includes a final catch-all entry using scram-sha-256, with optional LDAP entries for directory-integrated authentication. This HBA configuration can be modified through the DSM interface. For MySQL databases, LDAPS authentication is available using Active Directory Global Catalog, with support for multi-domain controller deployments via the AD Secure/SSL Global Catalog on port 3269. Directory service authentication for individual database instances can be enabled or disabled through the Enable Directory Service Authentication toggle in the database settings.

For SQL Server databases, DSM uses contained database users, where users authenticate directly against the database and identity information resides within the database. DSM supports Windows Authentication for SQL Server server-level logins through Active Directory integration, enabling Kerberos-based single sign-on for clients joined to the same domain. SQL Server Authentication logins can also be created on all DSM clusters. Administrators configure which user types are permitted per data service policy, selecting between Windows Principal authentication using Active Directory credentials and SQL User authentication using standard SQL Server logins. An internal management login (dsm_mgmt) restricts access to system-level objects, keeping administrative operations within an isolated context.

DSM enforces password policies for local accounts, including complexity requirements, and applies a root password expiry on a three-month rotation cycle. For service-to-service communication, DSM uses authentication secrets (dsm-auth-creds) containing endpoint credentials and root CA certificates, with automatic secret generation available through the Consumption Operator installation. Prometheus metrics forwarding supports configurable authentication options, including no authentication, certificate-only authentication, basic authentication with username and password, or a combination of certificate and basic authentication.

Regarding authentication strength appropriate to data classification, DSM provides the authentication mechanisms that administrators can configure to match organizational security policies, but it does not automatically enforce differentiated authentication strength based on data classification. Organizations must define their data classification scheme and determine which DSM authentication options (local credentials, LDAPS, HBA rules, certificate validation, Kerberos) apply to each tier of sensitivity. DSM contributes the technical controls; the alignment between authentication strength and data classification requires organizational governance to establish and maintain.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides mechanisms to securely manage authenticators across two distinct planes: the Controller management plane (admin access) and the application data plane (end-user client authentication for load-balanced applications).

For the management plane, Avi maintains user accounts in two categories: local users managed directly on the Controller, and remote users authenticated through an external authentication, authorization, and accounting (AAA) server. Avi uses Authentication Profiles to configure how administrators authenticate to the Controller. Authentication Profiles support LDAP for credential validation against a directory service, SAML for federated authentication that delegates the process to a centralized identity provider (IdP), and basic authentication. The recommended approach is federated authentication, which centralizes the authentication process by delegating to an external IdP rather than managing credentials locally on the Controller. SAML profiles verify user identity without storing credentials on untrusted systems, and Avi Controller SAML authentication profiles can be integrated with Workspace ONE Access for catalog-based application access. Admin users can also authenticate using password-less SSH-based login as an alternative to password-based authentication. On every user login, the Auth Mapping Profile evaluates authorization mapping rules and updates the user record with tenant and role pairs drawn from the IdP's assertions.

For the application data plane, Avi acts as a proxy that applies client authentication policies to inbound application traffic. Supported methods include LDAP authentication (checking client credentials against a directory service), SAML single sign-on (enabling federated application access with a single credential set), OAuth and OIDC (where Avi acts as a client and resource server, with the authorization server authenticating clients and validating authorization codes before issuing tokens), and JSON Web Token (JWT) validation. The SSO Policy supports configurable Authentication Rules and Authorization Rules with path-based matching criteria, allowing different authentication requirements to be applied to different application paths. More granular authorization in OAuth/OIDC flows is available through Authorization Policies or DataScript.

Organizations configure which authentication method applies to each virtual service or application based on the sensitivity of the data accessed. Avi's Authentication Profiles and SSO Policies provide the technical enforcement points to apply different authentication strengths across the application portfolio, but the mapping from data classification tier to required authentication method is an organizational governance decision that must be defined and maintained by the organization.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CR 1.9(1)

> Strength of function: Ensure that authentication mechanisms are robust against brute force attacks.  Strength of function enhancement: Specific metrics for resistance to automated login attempts.

**SCF Controls:** IAC-12.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several integration points for Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) and hardware-rooted trust mechanisms that support the protection of authenticators and cryptographic material, though VCF itself does not include a built-in HSM. The platform is designed to work with external HSM devices and hardware security components to strengthen key management, entropy sourcing, and boot integrity.

VCF integrates with external Key Management Servers (KMS) through the KMIP 1.1 protocol, providing the mechanism through which organizations can connect HSM-backed key management infrastructure to the platform's encryption services. The external KMS is the component that would be backed by an HSM to protect the root encryption keys on which VCF relies for vSAN data-at-rest encryption, VM Encryption, and other cryptographic operations. The KMS must support KMIP 1.1 and be capable of acting as a symmetric key foundry and server. VCF supports Standard Key Providers that connect to external KMIP-compliant KMS systems, where the KMS can be backed by HSMs for hardware-protected key storage. VMware vCenter establishes a trusted connection with the KMS by uploading the KMS server certificate and private key, and provisions encryption keys from the key provider. Each VMware ESX host uses the Key Encryption Key (KEK) to encrypt its Data Encryption Keys (DEKs) and stores the encrypted DEKs on disk, but does not store the KEK on disk. When a host reboots, it requests the KEK with the corresponding ID from the KMS and decrypts its DEKs as needed. The vSAN KMS configuration also supports an optional proxy address for environments that require it. When a user performs an encryption task such as creating an encrypted virtual machine, vCenter requests a new key from the default KMS to use as the KEK.

ESX supports the use of external HSM devices that are FIPS 140-3 and EAL4 certified as sources of external entropy data. Starting with vSphere 8.0 Update 1, administrators can add external entropy sources to an ESX host to feed high-quality, hardware-generated randomness into the host's cryptographic subsystem. This capability directly strengthens the generation of keys and other authenticators used across the platform by supplementing the host's own entropy sources.

ESX host hardening guidance includes enabling the Trusted Platform Module (TPM), a hardware-based security component embedded in the physical server. TPM provides a hardware root of trust for secure boot attestation and can store cryptographic keys and measurements in tamper-resistant hardware. When TPM is enabled and configured as part of the ESX hardening baseline, it provides hardware-backed protection for host-level authenticators and boot integrity verification. The ESX hardening guidance covers configuring authentication and authorization settings, enabling Secure Boot, disabling unnecessary services, enabling TPM, and implementing network security measures as part of a layered security posture. TPM modules must run the latest 2.0 firmware when in use, or be disabled in the BIOS if not in use. The physical TPM on ESX hosts also supports sealing policies based on Platform Configuration Register (PCR) measurements, which restrict access to sensitive data stored in the TPM to configurations that match a known-good platform state. Beyond host-level TPM, vSphere supports Virtual Trusted Platform Modules (vTPM) for virtual machines, allowing guest operating systems to use hardware-rooted cryptographic security without requiring a dedicated physical TPM per VM; vTPM keys are not exposed to the guest operating system itself, which reduces the attack surface at the VM level.

VCF also supports hardware memory encryption through confidential vSphere Pods. Confidential vSphere Pods use hardware technology such as AMD SEV-ES to keep guest OS memory encrypted, protecting it against access from the hypervisor. This capability addresses workload confidentiality rather than authenticator storage, but is part of the platform's hardware-rooted security mechanisms.

Organizations deploying VCF in environments that require HSM-protected authenticators should procure and integrate external HSM appliances, configure ESX hosts to use them for entropy and key management, enable TPM on all hosts, and establish trusted connections between vCenter and the KMS infrastructure. The platform provides the integration interfaces, protocol support, and configuration options, but the HSM hardware itself, its lifecycle management, and the policies governing its use are organizational responsibilities.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer supports Hardware Security Module (HSM) integration as a native capability for protecting the cryptographic keys that Avi's TLS termination and certificate operations depend on. The Avi Controller integrates with Thales Luna (formerly SafeNet Luna) HSM appliances, enabling hardware-backed storage and management of SSL certificates and private keys rather than software-based key storage.

The primary configuration mechanism is the HSM Group, configured in the Service Engine Group settings under the Advanced Settings tab in the Avi Controller UI or through the API. An HSM Group defines how Avi Controller and Service Engine nodes reach and authenticate with the external HSM device, including registering the IP addresses of all Controllers and Service Engines authorized to interact with it. The HSM Group is assigned per Service Engine Group and acts as a template of HSM settings applied uniformly to every Service Engine in that group. Mutual certificate authentication is established between the Avi Controller and the HSM during initial registration, so that only authorized nodes can request cryptographic operations from the device.

Avi Service Engines support dedicated network interfaces for HSM communication, separate from their standard management and data-plane interfaces. This interface isolation keeps HSM traffic off the general management network. Avi Controller cluster nodes similarly use dedicated network interfaces labeled HSM for hardware security module communication, and the HSM interface can be moved to a secondary interface if needed. High availability across multiple HSM devices is supported, allowing organizations to add redundant HSM nodes to an HSM Group to reduce single points of failure in key availability.

Avi Load Balancer's FIPS 140-3 compliance posture also incorporates dedicated HSM interfaces on Service Engines for secure cryptographic operations. Supported HSM clients include Thales Luna HSM client 7.3 and Universal Client v 10.4.1 for key management integration.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CR 1.9(a)

> Sub-item (a) of CR 1.9 in IEC 62443-4-2. The licensed standard text is not reproduced at this granularity; refer to the CR 1.9 requirement in the standard.

**SCF Controls:** IAC-10.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF includes the VMware Certificate Authority (VMCA), a built-in internal certificate authority integral with every vSphere deployment that is the foundation for constructing and verifying certification paths within the VCF infrastructure. Components that obtain certificates from VMCA have an inherent chain of trust back to this root. Administrators can replace the VMCA root certificate when organizational policy requires alignment with an enterprise PKI hierarchy, at which point VMCA operates as a subordinate CA, and all certificates it issues chain back to the enterprise root. Replacing the VMCA root certificate triggers a restart of services that use that certificate, and the VMware vCenter Certificate Management API provides a dedicated endpoint for this operation. Management of both VMCA-issued and third-party certificates is supported across the environment.

The VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) platform, which provides Kubernetes cluster provisioning within the VCF consumption layer, extends this VMCA-anchored trust chain to cluster infrastructure. TLS certificates for vSphere Supervisor and VKS cluster components, including the Kubernetes API server, kubelet, etcd, scheduler, and front-proxy, derive from the VMCA certificate. A CA-signed certificate can also be uploaded to replace the default certificate on the Supervisor Kubernetes control plane, supporting organizations that require alignment with an external PKI hierarchy at the Supervisor infrastructure level. VKS Service Cluster certificate authorities have a 9-year lifetime; leaf certificates have a 364-day validity period. Trust configuration for VKS clusters supports specifying additional trusted certificate authorities through the TkgServiceConfiguration additionalTrustedCAs field and the TanzuKubernetesCluster API's corresponding trust configuration, expressed as base64-encoded PEM certificate data, allowing administrators to extend trust to enterprise CA hierarchies within the Kubernetes cluster context. The Kubernetes ClusterTrustBundle API provides a structured mechanism for storing and distributing trust anchors to workloads and cluster components, decoupling trust anchor distribution from the certificate-signing pipeline. The VKS AuthenticationConfiguration Secret also accepts an optional certificate authority field to support JWT-based authentication flows backed by an internal PKI.

VCF SSO supports certificate-based authentication through holder-of-key tokens, where security artifacts embedded in the token are validated against trusted certificate authorities. The VCF SSO client can obtain a holder-of-key token by sending a token request that uses a certificate, and the SSO SDK includes samples demonstrating how solution components use their private key and certificate to acquire holder-of-key tokens. Solution user accounts, which handle service-to-service communication between vCenter, VCF Operations, and other platform components, authenticate using certificates rather than passwords, with the platform validating the certificate chain for each connection. Requests are signed with a user certificate private key through a security signature handler.

Trust store management across VCF components allows administrators to define accepted trust anchors. VCF Automation allows certificates to be added to the trust store through the UI or the VCF Automation API. The vSphere Automation SDKs support creating certificate stores using keytool to import certificates into a truststore with a password, providing a structured approach to managing which root and intermediate certificates are trusted. The dir-cli trustedcert publish command provides a CLI path for adding certificate authority certificates, including intermediate CA certificates, to the VECS TRUSTED_ROOTS store on vCenter. The vSphere Automation API exposes certificate management interfaces including TrustedRootChains, VMCARoot, and TLS certificate operations, allowing programmatic management of certificate trust. The NSX management plane and truststore APIs provide additional certificate management for network infrastructure components, including management of non-service certificates.

VCF Automation provides automated certificate validation capabilities, including an API endpoint that validates certificates and returns a resource with the certificate ID upon successful validation. When establishing trust with remote servers, VCF Automation can verify certificate information for SSL communication and retrieve intermediate and leaf certificates from issuing certificate authorities if the certificate chain is incomplete, allowing administrators to review the full path before making a trust decision and make selective choices about which certificates in a chain to trust. This chain retrieval capability supports the construction of certification paths from an end-entity certificate back to a trusted root. VCF Automation also validates server certificates during LDAPS connections through a Trust Certificate window, applying certificate verification to directory service authentication paths.

VCF Operations for Networks offers configurable certificate validation with two modes: Automatic Acceptance, where the system automatically accepts all detected certificate changes, and Manual Acceptance, where the system presents a notification for an administrator to review and accept certificates individually. Standard certificate validation can be activated in VCF Operations to verify certificates during connection testing, using a standard verification flow that checks the certificate chain against configured trust anchors. When standard certificate validation is activated, the Certificate Revocation List (CRL) tab becomes available, allowing administrators to upload and manage revocation lists for digital certificates that have been revoked by the issuing CA before their scheduled expiration date.

NSX also supports certificate revocation checking. NSX Manager can import certificate revocation lists in PEM format through its API and can enable or disable Certificate Revocation List Distribution Point (CDP) validation checks during certificate import. The NSX API provides endpoints to configure NSX Manager to retrieve CRLs, supporting ongoing revocation validation for network infrastructure certificates.

VCF applies proper certificate validation practices in its own operations. The platform documentation explicitly states that trust-all trust managers, which accept all certificates without validation, are appropriate only for development environments and should not be used in production. Production implementations should use proper certificate support with validated trust paths.

Automated certificate lifecycle management further supports PKI operations. VCF supports automatic renewal of TLS certificates for components using VMCA, Microsoft CA, OpenSSL CA, or self-signed certificates, with renewal triggered 60 days before certificate expiration. The Machine SSL certificate can be renewed for a specified period up to 730 days using the vSphere Automation API, and the auto-renewal toggle in VCF Operations console (Fleet Management, Certificates) allows administrators to activate automatic renewal. By default, the VMCA root certificate has a 10-year lifetime, and all certificates that VMCA signs expire no later than the root certificate; organizations planning to use VMCA long-term should account for root CA rotation before that threshold. VCF Operations provides a Certificates tab for each workload domain that displays certificate expiration status, supporting monitoring of certificate health.

Within VKS clusters, certificate lifecycle management operates through dedicated automation. The ClusterClass certificateRotation parameter governs automated control-plane certificate rotation, and the renewalDaysBeforeExpiry field defaults to 90 days and triggers a rolling rollout of updated certificates before they expire; this value is configurable per ClusterClass to align with organizational PKI policy. Certificate rotation is enabled by default across VKS ClusterClass versions, including builtin-generic-v3.2.0, v3.3.0, and v3.4.0 and later, so automated pre-expiry renewal is active without explicit configuration. Kubelet TLS bootstrapping automates initial certificate distribution and signing for new nodes joining a VKS cluster and can be configured to automatically request renewal when the kubelet certificate approaches expiry, with renewal approved automatically or manually depending on configuration. For application workloads, cert-manager is available as a standard VKS package; it creates TLS certificates for workloads and renews them automatically before expiration. CertificateSigningRequest resources from both the native Kubernetes certificates.k8s.io API group and the cert-manager.io API group are supported for certificate lifecycle management within VKS clusters. Cert-manager is also a prerequisite for several standard VKS addons including Contour, ExternalDNS, Prometheus, and Harbor. Harbor, available as a standard VKS package for container image registry services, supports TLS certificate configuration through cert-manager integration, a custom certificate provided via the tlsCertificate field, or a reference to an existing Kubernetes secret; remote registry certificate verification is configurable through the Verify Remote Cert option in Harbor's registry configuration. VKS clusters can be configured to trust self-signed CA certificates for private registries, and from ClusterClass builtin-generic-v3.2.0 onward, Harbor services with custom certificates are automatically trusted by VKS clusters through deterministic ordering of trusted certificates. The VCF Consumption CLI's imgpkg tool provides --registry-verify-certs and --registry-ca-cert-path flags for validating registry connections against configured trust anchors. Certificate expiration status across VKS control-plane certificates can be monitored using the kubeadm certs check-expiration command. For manual certificate renewal, the kubeadm certs renew command can renew all or individual cluster certificates; in replicated control-plane deployments, it must be executed on each control-plane node. Runtime OCSP or CRL revocation checking is not enforced natively by the Kubernetes API server at authentication time; organizations with requirements for runtime revocation validation integrate external PKI systems at the application or service mesh layer.

Together, these mechanisms construct and verify certification paths to accepted trust anchors across VCF components and check certificate status through CRL import and Certificate Revocation List Distribution Point validation in VCF Operations and NSX. The VKS consumption layer extends these PKI foundations through VMCA-anchored cluster component certificates, ClusterTrustBundle-based trust anchor distribution, and automated rotation at both the platform and workload levels, covering the automated certification path validation capabilities the control asks about.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) contributes to PKI-based authentication through a CA trust bundle management layer that spans its AI service components. PAIS validates certificates through configurable CA trust bundles stored as ConfigMaps in the VCF Automation namespace, with bundles supplied either as certificate files or ConfigMap resources by the administrator of the Harbor registry or content management system that PAIS connects to. The PAISConfiguration custom resource's spec.clientTls.caBundleRefs field references those ConfigMaps for client TLS authentication with OIDC providers and Harbor registries, and PAIS is updated with a new ConfigMap resource when a CA certificate is selected and added from the namespace. Trust bundles are organized per application and rotated through the Service Configuration tab by deleting the bundle containing the old certificate and adding a new bundle with the updated certificate before expiration.

PAIS requires CA trust bundles to be configured for the external entities it connects to: OIDC providers for identity federation, Harbor registries for model artifact retrieval, content management systems serving as Knowledge Base data sources (such as Confluence sites and Microsoft SharePoint On-Premises deployments), and remote MCP servers integrated for specialized data and capabilities. On Deep Learning VMs, Harbor registry CA certificates are copied to the /usr/local/share/ca-certificates directory and added to the system trust store using the update-ca-certificates command.

Certificate validation is enforced through several PAIS authentication paths. The VCF Consumption CLI accepts the --ca-certificate option to validate the VCF Automation endpoint certificate when it is not already in the local truststore, covering namespace login, VKS cluster provisioning with vGPU or GPU passthrough in both connected and disconnected environments, and deployment of completion or embedding model endpoints by using kubectl. The VCF Automation instance certificate must be saved locally on the machine deploying model endpoints or provisioning VKS clusters. The VCF Automation Provider Management UI requires trusted certificate import via Certificate Management > Trusted Certificates and certificate acceptance during the namespace import process for PAIS integration with VMware vCenter, and records a TM_CERT_BASE64 credential captured by running openssl s_client against the VCF Automation FQDN on port 443. PAIS OIDC authentication supports configuring Extra Audiences so the service accepts additional OAuth 2.0 clients during access token validation, and Grafana OIDC authentication for PAIS supports PKCE and refresh tokens. The PAIS ingress self-signed certificate is retrievable from the pais-ingress-default Kubernetes secret for downstream TLS configuration.

The actual certification path construction and certificate status checking at the network layer is performed by the underlying VKS and Kubernetes platform rather than by PAIS directly. The PAIS contribution is the CA trust bundle configuration layer, managed through ConfigMaps, the PAISConfiguration custom resource, and the VCF Automation Provider Management UI, that populates the trust stores consumed by those platform-level PKI validation mechanisms across its AI service components.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend uses PKI-based certificate validation within the Security Services Platform (SSP) to authenticate connections between platform components and to secure communications with external identity providers. The platform maintains a certificate hierarchy that supports automated certification path construction and verification against trusted anchors.

The Security Services Platform maintains its own certificate infrastructure, including Web Proxy, Platform Certificates, and Intermediate CA certificates. A CertWatcher component loads and validates root CA certificates and keys during initialization, maintaining a CA certificate pool and truststore for validating cluster API certificates. The platform monitors certificate status, identifying certificates that are expired, expiring soon, or valid, and generates alarms when certificates require attention. Administrators can view certificate details, replace certificates, generate certificate signing requests, and import certificates signed by a certificate authority of their choice. The Security Services Platform proactively manages its Platform CA certificate lifecycle, raising an alert 67 days before expiration and automatically initiating a certificate refresh 60 days before expiration for all internal entities and Intermediate CAs. A Certificates UI page provides visibility into Certificate Refresh Status and External Rollout details, including Bare Metal server certificate validity status.

For network detection components, NDR Sensors connect to the Security Services Platform using Mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication. PKIX certificate path validation is used to authenticate these connections, and authentication failures are logged with specific error messages indicating PKIX path validation or access authorization issues. This validation occurs automatically during sensor registration and ongoing communication.

The Security Services Platform validates certificates when establishing secure connections to external identity providers. When configured for LDAPS authentication, the platform requires an LDAP server certificate to authenticate directory service users, establishing a verified connection. Certificate import capabilities allow administrators to identify web proxy or ingress certificates signed by a certificate authority of their choice, extending the trust model to external systems.

For deployments using TLS Inspection, vDefend provides additional certificate path validation capabilities at the network layer. TLS Inspection certificate management supports importing or updating trusted CA bundles and a default CRL for server certificate validation during traffic inspection. In 9.1, TLS Inspection supports OCSP stapling options for certificate validation, providing a mechanism for checking certificate revocation status in addition to CRL-based checking. When inspecting encrypted traffic to external services, server certificate validation can be enforced against the configured trusted CA bundle and revocation information. The TLS proxy supports Enterprise issuing CA certificates, where a trusted subordinate CA certificate is signed by an Enterprise issuing CA through a CSR workflow, allowing integration with an organization's existing enterprise PKI infrastructure.

vDefend contributes to this control through its internal certificate path validation, automated certificate chain management for SSP component authentication, PKIX-based authentication for NDR Sensor connections via mTLS, CRL-based revocation checking, and OCSP stapling support for TLS Inspection server certificate validation. Full enterprise PKI integration for administrator authentication relies on the organization's LDAP-based identity infrastructure rather than vDefend alone, and comprehensive certificate path validation for all external connections depends on the broader VCF certificate infrastructure.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) uses PKI-based authentication for site pairing, appliance management, and management-plane communication. PNR uses TLS certificates and private keys to protect network communication and establish authentication with other servers, and treats certificates and keys as part of its core security infrastructure.

The PNR appliance supports SSL/TLS server endpoint certificates that can be either self-signed or custom certificates signed by a certificate authority. The PNR self-signed certificate expires five years from the first boot of the appliance. Custom certificates may be intermediate CA certificates that are not the root of a trust chain, supporting full certification path construction to an accepted trust anchor. Custom SSL/TLS certificates must meet specific requirements to work with PNR, and the current time must be within the certificate's validity period for authentication to succeed.

The PNR Configuration REST API provides endpoints to add certificate authority (CA) certificates and to retrieve installed CA certificates used to validate other servers' certificates. PNR also includes Authentication APIs as part of its REST API offerings. The PNR user interface validates host certificates against subject alternative names during connection to Platform Services Controller hosts. The PNR server establishes authenticated communication channels to remote services by acquiring a holder-of-key SAML token from VCF Single Sign-On (VCF SSO), then sends this token in a cryptographically signed message.

PNR includes certificate status monitoring. PNR raises a certificate expiring event when the certificate expiration date is within the configured number of days, with a default value of 30 days controlled by the localSiteStatus.minCertRemainingTime setting. PNR generates SrmCertificateExpiringEvent when the SSL/TLS certificate for a specified Site Recovery Manager (SRM) Server is approaching expiration, and SrmCertificateExpiredEvent when the certificate has expired. Certificate verification errors are recorded in security-related log files for audit-trail purposes.

PNR supports certificate-based authentication with storage providers through either direct certificate specification or thumbprint verification. During site registration and remote site management, PNR can accept or reject remote VMware vCenter certificates and prompts the administrator before adding a certificate to the trusted store. The Configure Local Sites workflow and the VCF Operations orchestrator plug-in include an option to ignore certificate warnings, which silently adds the certificate to the trusted store; administrators should use this option carefully because it bypasses interactive verification.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) includes several certificate management capabilities that contribute to PKI-based authentication validation. DSM maintains a Trusted Root Certificates store where administrators can add PEM-encoded Root and Intermediate CA certificates for directory service connections, database cluster connections, and connections to external systems such as backup repositories. The store displays the expiration time and date for each stored certificate, giving administrators visibility into certificate lifecycle status. Custom trusted certificates added to DSM must comply with FIPS requirements. Certificate validation behavior is configurable through the advanced-system-config ConfigMap, which supports two validation modes; the "latest" mode is the default for version 9.1 and later.

When clients connect to a DSM database cluster, DSM stores PEM-encoded certificate authorities in the ca.crt property of a Kubernetes Secret. Clients must add this CA certificate to their trusted CA list or operating system trust store before a connection is established. Connections from DSM to the VCF Automation Provider also require certificate validation and trust establishment before the connection becomes active. The Consumption Operator requires a TLS certificate for secure communication between the Consumption Operator and the DSM provider. DSM validates certificate status and requires explicit trust before the connection proceeds.

DSM supports custom certificates for both the Provider VM and for individual database clusters. Administrators configure custom certificates in PEM format, including the full certificate chain with its associated private key and CA certificate. The DSM API provides programmatic capability to configure custom certificates for database clusters, supporting integration with certificate management workflows. Administrators with restrictive certificate policies should replace the default DSM certificate with a CA-issued custom certificate. Custom certificate configuration for the Provider VM can only be performed on a fresh installation where no data services are deployed. If the Provider VM certificate expires, access to the DSM UI, database cluster upgrades or restarts, and cluster metrics may all be affected.

Certificate-based authentication is supported for PostgreSQL databases through host-based authentication (HBA) configuration, which accepts cert as an authentication method alongside SCRAM-SHA-256 and reject options. DSM supports mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication for metrics forwarding by enabling the Client Certificate Authentication toggle and providing the client certificate and private key. For SQL Server databases, DSM supports Custom CA as a Certificate Trust Source option for Active Directory domain connections when the system trust store cannot verify the server certificate.

For backup repository connections, DSM supports Trust On First Use (TOFU) configuration to extract and trust certificates for specific backup locations. DSM also introduced updated cryptographic algorithm requirements for certificates; administrators should verify that certificates comply with these requirements before or after upgrading, as noncompliant certificates can cause validation errors, service startup failures, or TLS handshake issues.

Satisfying the full control intent requires the organization to implement automated certificate renewal and revocation-checking processes. Certificate renewal for the Provider VM and database cluster certificates is a manual administrator responsibility; the documentation explicitly places this obligation on the administrator rather than on DSM itself. DSM does not provide built-in automated mechanisms for OCSP or CRL-based certificate status checking; these must be provided by the organization's PKI infrastructure or by PKI automation tooling configured with an appropriate issuer.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Meets)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides native PKI-based certificate validation through the PKI Profile, a reusable configuration object that implements the full certification path construction and status-checking pipeline required by this control.

The PKI Profile is configured under Security > Client SSL Certificate Validation > PKI Profile in the Avi Controller UI or API. It stores one or more certificate authority (CA) certificates that serve as trust anchors. When a client presents a certificate for authentication, Avi constructs the certification path from the presented certificate through any intermediate CAs to the root trust anchor stored in the PKI Profile. Each link in the intermediate chain must be present in the PKI Profile for the validation to succeed, enforcing complete chain construction to a known anchor.

Certificate status checking is handled through Certificate Revocation List (CRL) support built into the PKI Profile. The profile stores CRLs from issuing certificate authorities, including CRLs covering both end-entity and intermediate certificates. The Enable CRL Check option in the PKI Profile controls full-chain CRL checking for client certificate validation. When enabled, Avi checks the revocation status of the presented certificate and each intermediate against the configured CRLs before allowing the connection to proceed. A separate Ignore Peer Chain setting is available to control whether intermediate certificate validation is required, providing explicit configuration control over path depth.

The PKI Profile is attached to virtual service configurations through the HTTP Application Profile's Authentication tab, where it is referenced alongside mutual TLS settings to require and validate client certificates. The avi.ssl.set_pki_profile() DataScript function allows dynamic assignment of a PKI Profile and client certificate verification mode on a per-connection basis for advanced traffic-handling scenarios. A single PKI Profile object can be shared across multiple HTTP profiles within a tenant, centralizing trust anchor and revocation list management.

In Kubernetes environments, the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) exposes PKI-based authentication through the PKIProfile custom resource definition (CRD). The PKIProfile CRD defines a list of trusted Certificate Authorities (Root and Intermediate) through the required ca_certs field and integrates with RouteBackendExtension to enforce certificate validation for backend TLS connections. The System-Standard SSL Profile works in conjunction with the PKIProfile CRD for certificate validation and trust management across Kubernetes ingress configurations. This extends the same PKI validation pipeline to cloud-native deployment patterns without requiring configuration outside Kubernetes-native tooling.

In VCF deployments, the PKI Profile is available as a custom resource through the IaaS Service Console, where it manages the list of trusted authorities permitted to sign client certificates. This supports Zero Trust security patterns between client and server across the VCF-integrated load balancing layer.

For NSX and vCenter cloud integrations, the Avi Controller provides a Truststore PKI Profile setting under System Settings on the Access tab to enable certificate verification and supports CA certificate import for client SSL certificate validation in those cloud contexts.

### Control CR 1.9(b)

> Sub-item (b) of CR 1.9 in IEC 62443-4-2. The licensed standard text is not reproduced at this granularity; refer to the CR 1.9 requirement in the standard.

**SCF Controls:** IAC-10.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF includes the VMware Certificate Authority (VMCA), a built-in internal certificate authority integral with every vSphere deployment that is the foundation for constructing and verifying certification paths within the VCF infrastructure. Components that obtain certificates from VMCA have an inherent chain of trust back to this root. Administrators can replace the VMCA root certificate when organizational policy requires alignment with an enterprise PKI hierarchy, at which point VMCA operates as a subordinate CA, and all certificates it issues chain back to the enterprise root. Replacing the VMCA root certificate triggers a restart of services that use that certificate, and the VMware vCenter Certificate Management API provides a dedicated endpoint for this operation. Management of both VMCA-issued and third-party certificates is supported across the environment.

The VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) platform, which provides Kubernetes cluster provisioning within the VCF consumption layer, extends this VMCA-anchored trust chain to cluster infrastructure. TLS certificates for vSphere Supervisor and VKS cluster components, including the Kubernetes API server, kubelet, etcd, scheduler, and front-proxy, derive from the VMCA certificate. A CA-signed certificate can also be uploaded to replace the default certificate on the Supervisor Kubernetes control plane, supporting organizations that require alignment with an external PKI hierarchy at the Supervisor infrastructure level. VKS Service Cluster certificate authorities have a 9-year lifetime; leaf certificates have a 364-day validity period. Trust configuration for VKS clusters supports specifying additional trusted certificate authorities through the TkgServiceConfiguration additionalTrustedCAs field and the TanzuKubernetesCluster API's corresponding trust configuration, expressed as base64-encoded PEM certificate data, allowing administrators to extend trust to enterprise CA hierarchies within the Kubernetes cluster context. The Kubernetes ClusterTrustBundle API provides a structured mechanism for storing and distributing trust anchors to workloads and cluster components, decoupling trust anchor distribution from the certificate-signing pipeline. The VKS AuthenticationConfiguration Secret also accepts an optional certificate authority field to support JWT-based authentication flows backed by an internal PKI.

VCF SSO supports certificate-based authentication through holder-of-key tokens, where security artifacts embedded in the token are validated against trusted certificate authorities. The VCF SSO client can obtain a holder-of-key token by sending a token request that uses a certificate, and the SSO SDK includes samples demonstrating how solution components use their private key and certificate to acquire holder-of-key tokens. Solution user accounts, which handle service-to-service communication between vCenter, VCF Operations, and other platform components, authenticate using certificates rather than passwords, with the platform validating the certificate chain for each connection. Requests are signed with a user certificate private key through a security signature handler.

Trust store management across VCF components allows administrators to define accepted trust anchors. VCF Automation allows certificates to be added to the trust store through the UI or the VCF Automation API. The vSphere Automation SDKs support creating certificate stores using keytool to import certificates into a truststore with a password, providing a structured approach to managing which root and intermediate certificates are trusted. The dir-cli trustedcert publish command provides a CLI path for adding certificate authority certificates, including intermediate CA certificates, to the VECS TRUSTED_ROOTS store on vCenter. The vSphere Automation API exposes certificate management interfaces including TrustedRootChains, VMCARoot, and TLS certificate operations, allowing programmatic management of certificate trust. The NSX management plane and truststore APIs provide additional certificate management for network infrastructure components, including management of non-service certificates.

VCF Automation provides automated certificate validation capabilities, including an API endpoint that validates certificates and returns a resource with the certificate ID upon successful validation. When establishing trust with remote servers, VCF Automation can verify certificate information for SSL communication and retrieve intermediate and leaf certificates from issuing certificate authorities if the certificate chain is incomplete, allowing administrators to review the full path before making a trust decision and make selective choices about which certificates in a chain to trust. This chain retrieval capability supports the construction of certification paths from an end-entity certificate back to a trusted root. VCF Automation also validates server certificates during LDAPS connections through a Trust Certificate window, applying certificate verification to directory service authentication paths.

VCF Operations for Networks offers configurable certificate validation with two modes: Automatic Acceptance, where the system automatically accepts all detected certificate changes, and Manual Acceptance, where the system presents a notification for an administrator to review and accept certificates individually. Standard certificate validation can be activated in VCF Operations to verify certificates during connection testing, using a standard verification flow that checks the certificate chain against configured trust anchors. When standard certificate validation is activated, the Certificate Revocation List (CRL) tab becomes available, allowing administrators to upload and manage revocation lists for digital certificates that have been revoked by the issuing CA before their scheduled expiration date.

NSX also supports certificate revocation checking. NSX Manager can import certificate revocation lists in PEM format through its API and can enable or disable Certificate Revocation List Distribution Point (CDP) validation checks during certificate import. The NSX API provides endpoints to configure NSX Manager to retrieve CRLs, supporting ongoing revocation validation for network infrastructure certificates.

VCF applies proper certificate validation practices in its own operations. The platform documentation explicitly states that trust-all trust managers, which accept all certificates without validation, are appropriate only for development environments and should not be used in production. Production implementations should use proper certificate support with validated trust paths.

Automated certificate lifecycle management further supports PKI operations. VCF supports automatic renewal of TLS certificates for components using VMCA, Microsoft CA, OpenSSL CA, or self-signed certificates, with renewal triggered 60 days before certificate expiration. The Machine SSL certificate can be renewed for a specified period up to 730 days using the vSphere Automation API, and the auto-renewal toggle in VCF Operations console (Fleet Management, Certificates) allows administrators to activate automatic renewal. By default, the VMCA root certificate has a 10-year lifetime, and all certificates that VMCA signs expire no later than the root certificate; organizations planning to use VMCA long-term should account for root CA rotation before that threshold. VCF Operations provides a Certificates tab for each workload domain that displays certificate expiration status, supporting monitoring of certificate health.

Within VKS clusters, certificate lifecycle management operates through dedicated automation. The ClusterClass certificateRotation parameter governs automated control-plane certificate rotation, and the renewalDaysBeforeExpiry field defaults to 90 days and triggers a rolling rollout of updated certificates before they expire; this value is configurable per ClusterClass to align with organizational PKI policy. Certificate rotation is enabled by default across VKS ClusterClass versions, including builtin-generic-v3.2.0, v3.3.0, and v3.4.0 and later, so automated pre-expiry renewal is active without explicit configuration. Kubelet TLS bootstrapping automates initial certificate distribution and signing for new nodes joining a VKS cluster and can be configured to automatically request renewal when the kubelet certificate approaches expiry, with renewal approved automatically or manually depending on configuration. For application workloads, cert-manager is available as a standard VKS package; it creates TLS certificates for workloads and renews them automatically before expiration. CertificateSigningRequest resources from both the native Kubernetes certificates.k8s.io API group and the cert-manager.io API group are supported for certificate lifecycle management within VKS clusters. Cert-manager is also a prerequisite for several standard VKS addons including Contour, ExternalDNS, Prometheus, and Harbor. Harbor, available as a standard VKS package for container image registry services, supports TLS certificate configuration through cert-manager integration, a custom certificate provided via the tlsCertificate field, or a reference to an existing Kubernetes secret; remote registry certificate verification is configurable through the Verify Remote Cert option in Harbor's registry configuration. VKS clusters can be configured to trust self-signed CA certificates for private registries, and from ClusterClass builtin-generic-v3.2.0 onward, Harbor services with custom certificates are automatically trusted by VKS clusters through deterministic ordering of trusted certificates. The VCF Consumption CLI's imgpkg tool provides --registry-verify-certs and --registry-ca-cert-path flags for validating registry connections against configured trust anchors. Certificate expiration status across VKS control-plane certificates can be monitored using the kubeadm certs check-expiration command. For manual certificate renewal, the kubeadm certs renew command can renew all or individual cluster certificates; in replicated control-plane deployments, it must be executed on each control-plane node. Runtime OCSP or CRL revocation checking is not enforced natively by the Kubernetes API server at authentication time; organizations with requirements for runtime revocation validation integrate external PKI systems at the application or service mesh layer.

Together, these mechanisms construct and verify certification paths to accepted trust anchors across VCF components and check certificate status through CRL import and Certificate Revocation List Distribution Point validation in VCF Operations and NSX. The VKS consumption layer extends these PKI foundations through VMCA-anchored cluster component certificates, ClusterTrustBundle-based trust anchor distribution, and automated rotation at both the platform and workload levels, covering the automated certification path validation capabilities the control asks about.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) contributes to PKI-based authentication through a CA trust bundle management layer that spans its AI service components. PAIS validates certificates through configurable CA trust bundles stored as ConfigMaps in the VCF Automation namespace, with bundles supplied either as certificate files or ConfigMap resources by the administrator of the Harbor registry or content management system that PAIS connects to. The PAISConfiguration custom resource's spec.clientTls.caBundleRefs field references those ConfigMaps for client TLS authentication with OIDC providers and Harbor registries, and PAIS is updated with a new ConfigMap resource when a CA certificate is selected and added from the namespace. Trust bundles are organized per application and rotated through the Service Configuration tab by deleting the bundle containing the old certificate and adding a new bundle with the updated certificate before expiration.

PAIS requires CA trust bundles to be configured for the external entities it connects to: OIDC providers for identity federation, Harbor registries for model artifact retrieval, content management systems serving as Knowledge Base data sources (such as Confluence sites and Microsoft SharePoint On-Premises deployments), and remote MCP servers integrated for specialized data and capabilities. On Deep Learning VMs, Harbor registry CA certificates are copied to the /usr/local/share/ca-certificates directory and added to the system trust store using the update-ca-certificates command.

Certificate validation is enforced through several PAIS authentication paths. The VCF Consumption CLI accepts the --ca-certificate option to validate the VCF Automation endpoint certificate when it is not already in the local truststore, covering namespace login, VKS cluster provisioning with vGPU or GPU passthrough in both connected and disconnected environments, and deployment of completion or embedding model endpoints by using kubectl. The VCF Automation instance certificate must be saved locally on the machine deploying model endpoints or provisioning VKS clusters. The VCF Automation Provider Management UI requires trusted certificate import via Certificate Management > Trusted Certificates and certificate acceptance during the namespace import process for PAIS integration with VMware vCenter, and records a TM_CERT_BASE64 credential captured by running openssl s_client against the VCF Automation FQDN on port 443. PAIS OIDC authentication supports configuring Extra Audiences so the service accepts additional OAuth 2.0 clients during access token validation, and Grafana OIDC authentication for PAIS supports PKCE and refresh tokens. The PAIS ingress self-signed certificate is retrievable from the pais-ingress-default Kubernetes secret for downstream TLS configuration.

The actual certification path construction and certificate status checking at the network layer is performed by the underlying VKS and Kubernetes platform rather than by PAIS directly. The PAIS contribution is the CA trust bundle configuration layer, managed through ConfigMaps, the PAISConfiguration custom resource, and the VCF Automation Provider Management UI, that populates the trust stores consumed by those platform-level PKI validation mechanisms across its AI service components.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend uses PKI-based certificate validation within the Security Services Platform (SSP) to authenticate connections between platform components and to secure communications with external identity providers. The platform maintains a certificate hierarchy that supports automated certification path construction and verification against trusted anchors.

The Security Services Platform maintains its own certificate infrastructure, including Web Proxy, Platform Certificates, and Intermediate CA certificates. A CertWatcher component loads and validates root CA certificates and keys during initialization, maintaining a CA certificate pool and truststore for validating cluster API certificates. The platform monitors certificate status, identifying certificates that are expired, expiring soon, or valid, and generates alarms when certificates require attention. Administrators can view certificate details, replace certificates, generate certificate signing requests, and import certificates signed by a certificate authority of their choice. The Security Services Platform proactively manages its Platform CA certificate lifecycle, raising an alert 67 days before expiration and automatically initiating a certificate refresh 60 days before expiration for all internal entities and Intermediate CAs. A Certificates UI page provides visibility into Certificate Refresh Status and External Rollout details, including Bare Metal server certificate validity status.

For network detection components, NDR Sensors connect to the Security Services Platform using Mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication. PKIX certificate path validation is used to authenticate these connections, and authentication failures are logged with specific error messages indicating PKIX path validation or access authorization issues. This validation occurs automatically during sensor registration and ongoing communication.

The Security Services Platform validates certificates when establishing secure connections to external identity providers. When configured for LDAPS authentication, the platform requires an LDAP server certificate to authenticate directory service users, establishing a verified connection. Certificate import capabilities allow administrators to identify web proxy or ingress certificates signed by a certificate authority of their choice, extending the trust model to external systems.

For deployments using TLS Inspection, vDefend provides additional certificate path validation capabilities at the network layer. TLS Inspection certificate management supports importing or updating trusted CA bundles and a default CRL for server certificate validation during traffic inspection. In 9.1, TLS Inspection supports OCSP stapling options for certificate validation, providing a mechanism for checking certificate revocation status in addition to CRL-based checking. When inspecting encrypted traffic to external services, server certificate validation can be enforced against the configured trusted CA bundle and revocation information. The TLS proxy supports Enterprise issuing CA certificates, where a trusted subordinate CA certificate is signed by an Enterprise issuing CA through a CSR workflow, allowing integration with an organization's existing enterprise PKI infrastructure.

vDefend contributes to this control through its internal certificate path validation, automated certificate chain management for SSP component authentication, PKIX-based authentication for NDR Sensor connections via mTLS, CRL-based revocation checking, and OCSP stapling support for TLS Inspection server certificate validation. Full enterprise PKI integration for administrator authentication relies on the organization's LDAP-based identity infrastructure rather than vDefend alone, and comprehensive certificate path validation for all external connections depends on the broader VCF certificate infrastructure.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) uses PKI-based authentication for site pairing, appliance management, and management-plane communication. PNR uses TLS certificates and private keys to protect network communication and establish authentication with other servers, and treats certificates and keys as part of its core security infrastructure.

The PNR appliance supports SSL/TLS server endpoint certificates that can be either self-signed or custom certificates signed by a certificate authority. The PNR self-signed certificate expires five years from the first boot of the appliance. Custom certificates may be intermediate CA certificates that are not the root of a trust chain, supporting full certification path construction to an accepted trust anchor. Custom SSL/TLS certificates must meet specific requirements to work with PNR, and the current time must be within the certificate's validity period for authentication to succeed.

The PNR Configuration REST API provides endpoints to add certificate authority (CA) certificates and to retrieve installed CA certificates used to validate other servers' certificates. PNR also includes Authentication APIs as part of its REST API offerings. The PNR user interface validates host certificates against subject alternative names during connection to Platform Services Controller hosts. The PNR server establishes authenticated communication channels to remote services by acquiring a holder-of-key SAML token from VCF Single Sign-On (VCF SSO), then sends this token in a cryptographically signed message.

PNR includes certificate status monitoring. PNR raises a certificate expiring event when the certificate expiration date is within the configured number of days, with a default value of 30 days controlled by the localSiteStatus.minCertRemainingTime setting. PNR generates SrmCertificateExpiringEvent when the SSL/TLS certificate for a specified Site Recovery Manager (SRM) Server is approaching expiration, and SrmCertificateExpiredEvent when the certificate has expired. Certificate verification errors are recorded in security-related log files for audit-trail purposes.

PNR supports certificate-based authentication with storage providers through either direct certificate specification or thumbprint verification. During site registration and remote site management, PNR can accept or reject remote VMware vCenter certificates and prompts the administrator before adding a certificate to the trusted store. The Configure Local Sites workflow and the VCF Operations orchestrator plug-in include an option to ignore certificate warnings, which silently adds the certificate to the trusted store; administrators should use this option carefully because it bypasses interactive verification.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) includes several certificate management capabilities that contribute to PKI-based authentication validation. DSM maintains a Trusted Root Certificates store where administrators can add PEM-encoded Root and Intermediate CA certificates for directory service connections, database cluster connections, and connections to external systems such as backup repositories. The store displays the expiration time and date for each stored certificate, giving administrators visibility into certificate lifecycle status. Custom trusted certificates added to DSM must comply with FIPS requirements. Certificate validation behavior is configurable through the advanced-system-config ConfigMap, which supports two validation modes; the "latest" mode is the default for version 9.1 and later.

When clients connect to a DSM database cluster, DSM stores PEM-encoded certificate authorities in the ca.crt property of a Kubernetes Secret. Clients must add this CA certificate to their trusted CA list or operating system trust store before a connection is established. Connections from DSM to the VCF Automation Provider also require certificate validation and trust establishment before the connection becomes active. The Consumption Operator requires a TLS certificate for secure communication between the Consumption Operator and the DSM provider. DSM validates certificate status and requires explicit trust before the connection proceeds.

DSM supports custom certificates for both the Provider VM and for individual database clusters. Administrators configure custom certificates in PEM format, including the full certificate chain with its associated private key and CA certificate. The DSM API provides programmatic capability to configure custom certificates for database clusters, supporting integration with certificate management workflows. Administrators with restrictive certificate policies should replace the default DSM certificate with a CA-issued custom certificate. Custom certificate configuration for the Provider VM can only be performed on a fresh installation where no data services are deployed. If the Provider VM certificate expires, access to the DSM UI, database cluster upgrades or restarts, and cluster metrics may all be affected.

Certificate-based authentication is supported for PostgreSQL databases through host-based authentication (HBA) configuration, which accepts cert as an authentication method alongside SCRAM-SHA-256 and reject options. DSM supports mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication for metrics forwarding by enabling the Client Certificate Authentication toggle and providing the client certificate and private key. For SQL Server databases, DSM supports Custom CA as a Certificate Trust Source option for Active Directory domain connections when the system trust store cannot verify the server certificate.

For backup repository connections, DSM supports Trust On First Use (TOFU) configuration to extract and trust certificates for specific backup locations. DSM also introduced updated cryptographic algorithm requirements for certificates; administrators should verify that certificates comply with these requirements before or after upgrading, as noncompliant certificates can cause validation errors, service startup failures, or TLS handshake issues.

Satisfying the full control intent requires the organization to implement automated certificate renewal and revocation-checking processes. Certificate renewal for the Provider VM and database cluster certificates is a manual administrator responsibility; the documentation explicitly places this obligation on the administrator rather than on DSM itself. DSM does not provide built-in automated mechanisms for OCSP or CRL-based certificate status checking; these must be provided by the organization's PKI infrastructure or by PKI automation tooling configured with an appropriate issuer.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Meets)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides native PKI-based certificate validation through the PKI Profile, a reusable configuration object that implements the full certification path construction and status-checking pipeline required by this control.

The PKI Profile is configured under Security > Client SSL Certificate Validation > PKI Profile in the Avi Controller UI or API. It stores one or more certificate authority (CA) certificates that serve as trust anchors. When a client presents a certificate for authentication, Avi constructs the certification path from the presented certificate through any intermediate CAs to the root trust anchor stored in the PKI Profile. Each link in the intermediate chain must be present in the PKI Profile for the validation to succeed, enforcing complete chain construction to a known anchor.

Certificate status checking is handled through Certificate Revocation List (CRL) support built into the PKI Profile. The profile stores CRLs from issuing certificate authorities, including CRLs covering both end-entity and intermediate certificates. The Enable CRL Check option in the PKI Profile controls full-chain CRL checking for client certificate validation. When enabled, Avi checks the revocation status of the presented certificate and each intermediate against the configured CRLs before allowing the connection to proceed. A separate Ignore Peer Chain setting is available to control whether intermediate certificate validation is required, providing explicit configuration control over path depth.

The PKI Profile is attached to virtual service configurations through the HTTP Application Profile's Authentication tab, where it is referenced alongside mutual TLS settings to require and validate client certificates. The avi.ssl.set_pki_profile() DataScript function allows dynamic assignment of a PKI Profile and client certificate verification mode on a per-connection basis for advanced traffic-handling scenarios. A single PKI Profile object can be shared across multiple HTTP profiles within a tenant, centralizing trust anchor and revocation list management.

In Kubernetes environments, the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) exposes PKI-based authentication through the PKIProfile custom resource definition (CRD). The PKIProfile CRD defines a list of trusted Certificate Authorities (Root and Intermediate) through the required ca_certs field and integrates with RouteBackendExtension to enforce certificate validation for backend TLS connections. The System-Standard SSL Profile works in conjunction with the PKIProfile CRD for certificate validation and trust management across Kubernetes ingress configurations. This extends the same PKI validation pipeline to cloud-native deployment patterns without requiring configuration outside Kubernetes-native tooling.

In VCF deployments, the PKI Profile is available as a custom resource through the IaaS Service Console, where it manages the list of trusted authorities permitted to sign client certificates. This supports Zero Trust security patterns between client and server across the VCF-integrated load balancing layer.

For NSX and vCenter cloud integrations, the Avi Controller provides a Truststore PKI Profile setting under System Settings on the Access tab to enable certificate verification and supports CA certificate import for client SSL certificate validation in those cloud contexts.

### Control CR 1.9(c)

> Sub-item (c) of CR 1.9 in IEC 62443-4-2. The licensed standard text is not reproduced at this granularity; refer to the CR 1.9 requirement in the standard.

**SCF Controls:** IAC-10.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF includes the VMware Certificate Authority (VMCA), a built-in internal certificate authority integral with every vSphere deployment that is the foundation for constructing and verifying certification paths within the VCF infrastructure. Components that obtain certificates from VMCA have an inherent chain of trust back to this root. Administrators can replace the VMCA root certificate when organizational policy requires alignment with an enterprise PKI hierarchy, at which point VMCA operates as a subordinate CA, and all certificates it issues chain back to the enterprise root. Replacing the VMCA root certificate triggers a restart of services that use that certificate, and the VMware vCenter Certificate Management API provides a dedicated endpoint for this operation. Management of both VMCA-issued and third-party certificates is supported across the environment.

The VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) platform, which provides Kubernetes cluster provisioning within the VCF consumption layer, extends this VMCA-anchored trust chain to cluster infrastructure. TLS certificates for vSphere Supervisor and VKS cluster components, including the Kubernetes API server, kubelet, etcd, scheduler, and front-proxy, derive from the VMCA certificate. A CA-signed certificate can also be uploaded to replace the default certificate on the Supervisor Kubernetes control plane, supporting organizations that require alignment with an external PKI hierarchy at the Supervisor infrastructure level. VKS Service Cluster certificate authorities have a 9-year lifetime; leaf certificates have a 364-day validity period. Trust configuration for VKS clusters supports specifying additional trusted certificate authorities through the TkgServiceConfiguration additionalTrustedCAs field and the TanzuKubernetesCluster API's corresponding trust configuration, expressed as base64-encoded PEM certificate data, allowing administrators to extend trust to enterprise CA hierarchies within the Kubernetes cluster context. The Kubernetes ClusterTrustBundle API provides a structured mechanism for storing and distributing trust anchors to workloads and cluster components, decoupling trust anchor distribution from the certificate-signing pipeline. The VKS AuthenticationConfiguration Secret also accepts an optional certificate authority field to support JWT-based authentication flows backed by an internal PKI.

VCF SSO supports certificate-based authentication through holder-of-key tokens, where security artifacts embedded in the token are validated against trusted certificate authorities. The VCF SSO client can obtain a holder-of-key token by sending a token request that uses a certificate, and the SSO SDK includes samples demonstrating how solution components use their private key and certificate to acquire holder-of-key tokens. Solution user accounts, which handle service-to-service communication between vCenter, VCF Operations, and other platform components, authenticate using certificates rather than passwords, with the platform validating the certificate chain for each connection. Requests are signed with a user certificate private key through a security signature handler.

Trust store management across VCF components allows administrators to define accepted trust anchors. VCF Automation allows certificates to be added to the trust store through the UI or the VCF Automation API. The vSphere Automation SDKs support creating certificate stores using keytool to import certificates into a truststore with a password, providing a structured approach to managing which root and intermediate certificates are trusted. The dir-cli trustedcert publish command provides a CLI path for adding certificate authority certificates, including intermediate CA certificates, to the VECS TRUSTED_ROOTS store on vCenter. The vSphere Automation API exposes certificate management interfaces including TrustedRootChains, VMCARoot, and TLS certificate operations, allowing programmatic management of certificate trust. The NSX management plane and truststore APIs provide additional certificate management for network infrastructure components, including management of non-service certificates.

VCF Automation provides automated certificate validation capabilities, including an API endpoint that validates certificates and returns a resource with the certificate ID upon successful validation. When establishing trust with remote servers, VCF Automation can verify certificate information for SSL communication and retrieve intermediate and leaf certificates from issuing certificate authorities if the certificate chain is incomplete, allowing administrators to review the full path before making a trust decision and make selective choices about which certificates in a chain to trust. This chain retrieval capability supports the construction of certification paths from an end-entity certificate back to a trusted root. VCF Automation also validates server certificates during LDAPS connections through a Trust Certificate window, applying certificate verification to directory service authentication paths.

VCF Operations for Networks offers configurable certificate validation with two modes: Automatic Acceptance, where the system automatically accepts all detected certificate changes, and Manual Acceptance, where the system presents a notification for an administrator to review and accept certificates individually.
