Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CR 1.12

> System use notification: Display a notification message before establishing user identification.

**SCF Controls:** SEA-18, SEA-18.1, SEA-18.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides configurable logon banner and system use notification capabilities across its management interfaces, allowing organizations to display approved notification messages before granting access.

NSX Manager supports configuring a security banner or message of the day. A separate login user agreement banner can be activated to display at every login. When enabled, only users with Enterprise Admin roles can modify the banner content, restricting unauthorized changes to the notification text. This banner can be configured with organization-specific language covering acceptable use policies, monitoring notices, and legal disclaimers. NSX login user agreement banner configuration is recommended for organizations with security compliance requirements. In federated environments, each Global Manager and Local Manager maintains its own login user agreement banner configuration, allowing site-specific messaging.

VCF Operations for Networks provides configurable login message banners through both the web UI and the CLI. Administrators can configure custom message text through the System Configuration settings, where login banners can be enabled or deactivated via a toggle button on the Settings page. When login banners are enabled, VCF Operations for Networks supports requiring users to click an explicit consent check box to indicate acceptance of terms and conditions before proceeding, providing a documented acknowledgment mechanism. A separate user consent form can also be configured with a title and content message that requires users to agree before logging in. The set-banner CLI command configures both the SSH pre-login banner for the support user and the post-login banner for the console user, with support for quoted text and newline characters for multi-line messages.

VCF Operations (the monitoring and analytics platform) can display a login message when users access the login page, with the option to require explicit user consent for terms, conditions, or mandatory notices before proceeding.

VCF Automation for VM Apps supports enabling a Notice and Consent Banner through administrator configuration in Workspace ONE Access. This is configured through the Workspace ONE Access administrative console by setting the CertificateAuthAdapter with an Enable Consent Form before Authentication setting. The banner informs users of monitoring and data inspection policies before they access the automation interface.

VMware ESX supports configurable SSH connection banner messages that display when users connect via SSH. A separate welcome message can also be configured for display to users accessing ESX hosts. These settings are exposed as host system properties that can be monitored and enforced through VCF Operations.

vSphere Supervisor cluster configuration supports a login banner message displayed to users upon authentication, configurable through the cluster setup parameters.

These banner capabilities span VCF management components at the network, compute, operations, automation, and Kubernetes layers. Administrators should configure banners on each management interface with the organization's approved notification text and enable consent requirements where available.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Meets)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides configurable Login Banner and Message of the Day mechanisms that apply to all administrative access paths: the Controller web UI, CLI, and SSH. The Login Banner is displayed before authentication completes, meaning it is presented to all users attempting access regardless of whether their credentials succeed. The Message of the Day is displayed after a successful login through any of these interfaces. Both messages are configured in Administration > System Settings and take effect on the next login session.

The Login Banner text is fully customizable and can contain any organizational-approved system use notification, including statements about authorized use, monitoring, and consent. Together, these two mechanisms satisfy the requirement for a pre-login system use notification across all supported administrative interfaces of Avi Load Balancer, with no dependency on the underlying VCF platform beyond network access to the Controller.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CR 1.14

> Strength of symmetric key-based authentication: Capability to manage symmetric keys securely.  Symmetric key protection: Hardware-based protection for symmetric keys.

**SCF Controls:** CRY-09.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides integrated symmetric key management capabilities through its Key Management Server (KMS) infrastructure and the VMware vCenter CryptoManager interface. VCF requires that the KMS support the Key Management Interoperability Protocol (KMIP) 1.1 standard and that it be capable of acting as a symmetric key foundry and server. The KMS must also appear in the VMware Compatibility Guide for Key Management Servers before it can be used in a VCF environment. Alternatively, the vSphere Native Key Provider can manage symmetric keys without requiring an external KMS and can coexist with existing key server infrastructure. This architecture centralizes symmetric key production and lifecycle management through a standards-based protocol, with vCenter as the orchestration layer between VCF components and the key provider.

VCF uses a key hierarchy for encryption operations. Key Encryption Keys (KEK) wrap Disk Encryption Keys (DEK), with DEKs using XTS-AES-256. VMware ESX hosts use the KEK to encrypt their internal keys and store only the encrypted internal keys on disk; the KEK itself is not persisted locally. Internal encryption keys are kept in memory only during operation, with only the encrypted data stored on disk. This separation of key tiers limits the exposure of key material at rest.

vSAN data-at-rest encryption relies on KMS integration for all symmetric key operations. Enabling vSAN encryption requires selecting a KMS cluster during configuration, establishing a trust relationship with the KMS by uploading certificate and key files, and confirming that the KMS is healthy before encryption can proceed. Administrators should deploy KMS servers on infrastructure separate from the vSAN datastore being encrypted and should use highly available KMS clusters to distribute the keys that protect vSAN datastores. Some KMS vendors support credential-based isolation of encryption keys, allowing administrators to specify a username and password during vSAN encryption configuration for key partitioning. An optional proxy port configuration is also available for KMS connectivity. vSAN also supports the vSphere Native Key Provider for encrypting virtual machines in vSAN clusters.

vSAN data-in-transit encryption uses symmetric keys that are generated dynamically and shared between hosts to encrypt inter-host traffic. The rekey interval for data-in-transit encryption is configurable, with a default of 1,440 minutes (24 hours) and a supported range of 30 minutes to 10,080 minutes (7 days). For data-at-rest encryption, vSAN supports generating new Key Encryption Keys (KEK) and new Data Encryption Keys (DEK), with the capability to re-encrypt all stored data using new keys when existing keys expire or become compromised. Both shallow recryption (replacing only KEKs via CryptoSpecShallowRecrypt) and deep recryption (replacing all KEKs and DEKs via CryptoSpecDeepRecrypt) are available through the API. vSAN OSA and vSAN ESA both support these key rotation options using AES-256 bit encryption. AES-NI hardware acceleration significantly improves encryption performance, and administrators should enable AES-NI in the host BIOS.

The vCenter CryptoManager managed object provides programmatic control over cryptographic key operations in vSphere, including integration with the KMS for key creation, retrieval, and lifecycle management. This API supports both vSAN encryption and virtual machine encryption workflows, where symmetric keys are managed through the same KMS infrastructure. The vSphere Web Services API also provides registerKmipServer() to register a KMIP key management server with vCenter's cryptography manager.

VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters running on the vSphere Supervisor extend symmetric key management to the Kubernetes control plane through the Kubernetes KMS provider framework. The kube-apiserver supports etcd encryption-at-rest through an EncryptionConfiguration resource that specifies a KMS provider type with a Unix domain socket endpoint connecting to an external key management system. This framework uses an envelope encryption scheme: data stored in etcd is encrypted with a DEK, which is itself encrypted by a KEK held in the external KMS, keeping the root of trust outside the cluster boundary. Kubernetes recommends the KMS provider over storing raw encryption keys inline in the EncryptionConfiguration file for this reason. KMS v2, stable from Kubernetes 1.29, uses the KmsV2Kdf feature gate to generate single-use DEKs from a secret seed combined with random data via a key derivation function, reducing call volume to the external KMS while maintaining per-object cryptographic isolation. KMS plugin implementations are required to verify the key_id of any wrapped key before attempting decryption, which supports key versioning and rotation workflows. Key rotation requires an administrator to explicitly trigger re-encryption of stored secrets after a KEK rotation; VKS provides the technical hooks for this process. For environments requiring FIPS-validated cryptographic operations at the Kubernetes layer, VKS supports TanzuKubernetesRelease (TKR) images with FIPS cryptographic module support, such as the vmware.3-fips.1-tkg.1 release variants, which configure Kubernetes node binaries to use a FIPS-validated module for symmetric operations within the cluster. The external KMS must independently be a FIPS-validated product or hardware security module.

VCF components use FIPS-validated cryptographic modules across the stack. VCF supports FIPS 140-2 requirements for cryptographic modules. On the vCenter appliance, VMware's Boring Crypto Module version 6.0 provides FIPS-validated support for AES, ECDSA, HMAC, RSA, and SHA2 variants; the VMware OpenSSL FIPS Object Module (versions 2.0.20-vmw and 3.0) also covers DRBG, DSA, SHS, and Triple-DES algorithms. NSX is configured to use FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules by default, with validation performed through the Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). NSX Manager uses FIPS 140 approved algorithms for authentication to cryptographic modules and generates unique session identifiers using a FIPS 140 approved random number generator. VCF Automation uses the BouncyCastle FIPS 2.0.0 cryptographic module, which is compliant with FIPS 140-3 (Certificate #4743). VCF Operations for Networks uses the BC-FJA (Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API) version 2.0.0 as its validated cryptographic module and can restrict cryptographic operations to FIPS-validated modules by enabling FIPS mode for external connections. VCF Operations for Networks also uses FIPS-compliant cryptographic algorithms for internal connections. VCF Operations orchestrator uses a FIPS-compliant AES with a 256-bit cipher key for encryption of strings.

VCF Operations supports a FIPS Security Mode that, when enabled, applies additional algorithm and cipher prerequisites across the platform. This mode restricts the cryptographic algorithms and ciphers available to those that meet FIPS validation requirements, including restricting key exchange algorithms to FIPS-approved options. This provides a platform-wide enforcement mechanism for FIPS-compliant symmetric key usage.

Two limitations apply in FIPS-sensitive environments. Self-signed certificates and private keys generated using OpenSSL are not FIPS-compatible; administrators should use FIPS-compatible key generation methods such as pyOpenSSL for PKCS#8 format. While NSX supports additional elliptic curve key types for certificates, non-FIPS-approved key types should not be used in environments requiring only FIPS-approved cryptographic keys.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) contributes to symmetric cryptographic key management through its requirements for consistent key management infrastructure across protected and recovery sites.

PNR and vSphere Replication require that either a KMIP-compatible Key Management Server (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider be available at both the protected and recovery sites to protect and recover encrypted virtual machines. The KMS cluster must be registered with the same name on both sites. When using the vSphere Native Key Provider, it must be manually configured on both the local and remote sites. The vSphere Native Key Provider enables encryption-related functionality for virtual machines and virtual disks without requiring an external KMS.

PNR establishes a trusted connection between VMware vCenter and the KMS from which vCenter retrieves keys as needed to protect virtual machine disks and other files. This cross-site KMS consistency is a prerequisite for encrypted VM failover and failback through recovery plans.

The generation and lifecycle management of symmetric keys, including the key hierarchy, FIPS validation of cryptographic modules, key rotation scheduling, and TPM-based key persistence, are capabilities of the VCF platform. PNR's contribution is the enforcement of consistent KMS deployment across recovery topologies, which is a necessary condition for encrypted workload protection and recovery to function.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

This control requires mechanisms for producing and managing symmetric cryptographic keys using FIPS-compliant key management technology and processes. VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) applies symmetric encryption across several operational contexts and exposes FIPS-related configuration options, but relies on the broader VCF platform and external tooling to satisfy the full scope of this requirement.

In DSM, the product enforces FIPS 140-3 compliant cryptographic standards and validates certificates during upload, rejecting unsupported algorithms or parameters. DSM specifically rejects the Ed25519 elliptic curve algorithm because it is not FIPS 140-3 compliant. Accepted key algorithms for custom certificates include ECDSA P-256, ECDSA P-384, and RSA keys with a minimum size of 2048 bits. This enforcement is documented in "Using Trusted Certificates in Data Services Manager > Configuring Custom Certificates for Data Services Manager > Supported Cryptographic Algorithms."

For MySQL databases managed by DSM, the ssl_fips_mode setting activates FIPS mode on the client side, restricting cipher negotiation to FIPS-approved algorithms. This setting is documented in "Databases in VMware Data Services Manager > Using MySQL in Data Services Manager > Configuration."

DSM also applies symmetric encryption in its user management subsystem. User passwords are stored encrypted using PGCrypto symmetric encryption with dedicated encryption keys, as documented in "Administering Data Services Manager > Managing LDAP Users in Data Services Manager." PostgreSQL databases managed by DSM include the pgcrypto extension version 1.3, which provides cryptographic functions available to database workloads.

The backup subsystem uses AES-256 to encrypt backup data written to S3-compatible storage, consistent with FIPS-approved algorithm requirements.

What DSM does not provide is a standalone FIPS-validated key management module or integration with an external key management service (KMS). The production, lifecycle management, rotation, and revocation of symmetric keys at the organizational level requires an external FIPS-validated KMS or hardware security module (HSM) operating at the infrastructure layer. DSM consumes keys and applies FIPS-approved symmetric algorithms, but the key generation and management controls for those keys are the responsibility of the organization and the underlying platform. Auditors should verify that the VCF infrastructure supporting DSM is configured with an appropriate FIPS-validated key provider, and that the organization has key management processes that cover the DSM-managed symmetric keys used for backups and internal encryption.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) supports a FIPS 140-3 compliance mode that uses OpenSSL FIPS Provider version 3.1.2 for all cryptographic operations. The OpenSSL FIPS Object Module v3.1.2 has been validated at FIPS 140-3 Security Level 1 and awarded Certificate #4985 by the Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). When FIPS mode is enabled on the Avi Controller and Service Engines, only cryptographic algorithms approved under FIPS 140-3 are used. FIPS 140-3 requires stricter handling of keys, authentication data, and random number generation than its predecessor, and aligns cryptographic module standards with international standards ISO/IEC 19790:2012 and ISO/IEC 24759:2017. This covers the symmetric encryption operations involved in TLS termination, re-encryption between Service Engines and backend servers, and internal control-plane communication. FIPS mode is configured at deployment time for Controller cluster deployments.

SSL/TLS Profile Templates in Avi give administrators control over the cipher suites and protocol versions used by virtual services. These profiles specify a prioritized cipher list and accepted protocol versions per virtual service and are managed under Templates > Security > SSL/TLS Profile in the Avi Controller UI. Configuring a FIPS-aligned SSL/TLS Profile and applying it to virtual services restricts symmetric key negotiation and cipher use across all load-balanced application traffic to FIPS-approved algorithms.

Post-quantum cryptography (PQC)-based Key Encapsulation Mechanisms and Signature Algorithms are not supported in Avi deployments running in FIPS mode.

For organizations deploying Avi in public cloud environments, Avi integrates with cloud-native key management services. In AWS, Avi's Cloud Connector integrates with AWS KMS for Service Engine disk and SQS queue encryption, supporting customer-managed keys with configurable IAM and key policies; the Avi Controller can list KMS encryption keys in the Avi UI and supports S3 and EBS encryption. In Google Cloud Platform, Avi supports Google Cloud KMS for Service Engine disk and image encryption, GCS bucket encryption, and GCS object encryption, using separate KMS keys for each resource type.

The Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) 2.1 adds support for configuring both RSA and ECC signed certificates simultaneously on a single virtual service through the alt.crt and alt.key fields in the default TLS secret, enabling certificate algorithm flexibility for Kubernetes ingress workloads.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control CR 1.14(1)

> Strength of symmetric key-based authentication: Capability to manage symmetric keys securely.  Symmetric key protection: Hardware-based protection for symmetric keys.

**SCF Controls:** IAC-12.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several integration points for Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) and hardware-rooted trust mechanisms that support the protection of authenticators and cryptographic material, though VCF itself does not include a built-in HSM. The platform is designed to work with external HSM devices and hardware security components to strengthen key management, entropy sourcing, and boot integrity.

VCF integrates with external Key Management Servers (KMS) through the KMIP 1.1 protocol, providing the mechanism through which organizations can connect HSM-backed key management infrastructure to the platform's encryption services. The external KMS is the component that would be backed by an HSM to protect the root encryption keys on which VCF relies for vSAN data-at-rest encryption, VM Encryption, and other cryptographic operations. The KMS must support KMIP 1.1 and be capable of acting as a symmetric key foundry and server. VCF supports Standard Key Providers that connect to external KMIP-compliant KMS systems, where the KMS can be backed by HSMs for hardware-protected key storage. VMware vCenter establishes a trusted connection with the KMS by uploading the KMS server certificate and private key, and provisions encryption keys from the key provider. Each VMware ESX host uses the Key Encryption Key (KEK) to encrypt its Data Encryption Keys (DEKs) and stores the encrypted DEKs on disk, but does not store the KEK on disk. When a host reboots, it requests the KEK with the corresponding ID from the KMS and decrypts its DEKs as needed. The vSAN KMS configuration also supports an optional proxy address for environments that require it. When a user performs an encryption task such as creating an encrypted virtual machine, vCenter requests a new key from the default KMS to use as the KEK.

ESX supports the use of external HSM devices that are FIPS 140-3 and EAL4 certified as sources of external entropy data. Starting with vSphere 8.0 Update 1, administrators can add external entropy sources to an ESX host to feed high-quality, hardware-generated randomness into the host's cryptographic subsystem. This capability directly strengthens the generation of keys and other authenticators used across the platform by supplementing the host's own entropy sources.

ESX host hardening guidance includes enabling the Trusted Platform Module (TPM), a hardware-based security component embedded in the physical server. TPM provides a hardware root of trust for secure boot attestation and can store cryptographic keys and measurements in tamper-resistant hardware. When TPM is enabled and configured as part of the ESX hardening baseline, it provides hardware-backed protection for host-level authenticators and boot integrity verification. The ESX hardening guidance covers configuring authentication and authorization settings, enabling Secure Boot, disabling unnecessary services, enabling TPM, and implementing network security measures as part of a layered security posture. TPM modules must run the latest 2.0 firmware when in use, or be disabled in the BIOS if not in use. The physical TPM on ESX hosts also supports sealing policies based on Platform Configuration Register (PCR) measurements, which restrict access to sensitive data stored in the TPM to configurations that match a known-good platform state. Beyond host-level TPM, vSphere supports Virtual Trusted Platform Modules (vTPM) for virtual machines, allowing guest operating systems to use hardware-rooted cryptographic security without requiring a dedicated physical TPM per VM; vTPM keys are not exposed to the guest operating system itself, which reduces the attack surface at the VM level.

VCF also supports hardware memory encryption through confidential vSphere Pods. Confidential vSphere Pods use hardware technology such as AMD SEV-ES to keep guest OS memory encrypted, protecting it against access from the hypervisor. This capability addresses workload confidentiality rather than authenticator storage, but is part of the platform's hardware-rooted security mechanisms.

Organizations deploying VCF in environments that require HSM-protected authenticators should procure and integrate external HSM appliances, configure ESX hosts to use them for entropy and key management, enable TPM on all hosts, and establish trusted connections between vCenter and the KMS infrastructure. The platform provides the integration interfaces, protocol support, and configuration options, but the HSM hardware itself, its lifecycle management, and the policies governing its use are organizational responsibilities.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer supports Hardware Security Module (HSM) integration as a native capability for protecting the cryptographic keys that Avi's TLS termination and certificate operations depend on. The Avi Controller integrates with Thales Luna (formerly SafeNet Luna) HSM appliances, enabling hardware-backed storage and management of SSL certificates and private keys rather than software-based key storage.

The primary configuration mechanism is the HSM Group, configured in the Service Engine Group settings under the Advanced Settings tab in the Avi Controller UI or through the API. An HSM Group defines how Avi Controller and Service Engine nodes reach and authenticate with the external HSM device, including registering the IP addresses of all Controllers and Service Engines authorized to interact with it. The HSM Group is assigned per Service Engine Group and acts as a template of HSM settings applied uniformly to every Service Engine in that group. Mutual certificate authentication is established between the Avi Controller and the HSM during initial registration, so that only authorized nodes can request cryptographic operations from the device.

Avi Service Engines support dedicated network interfaces for HSM communication, separate from their standard management and data-plane interfaces. This interface isolation keeps HSM traffic off the general management network. Avi Controller cluster nodes similarly use dedicated network interfaces labeled HSM for hardware security module communication, and the HSM interface can be moved to a secondary interface if needed. High availability across multiple HSM devices is supported, allowing organizations to add redundant HSM nodes to an HSM Group to reduce single points of failure in key availability.

Avi Load Balancer's FIPS 140-3 compliance posture also incorporates dedicated HSM interfaces on Service Engines for secure cryptographic operations. Supported HSM clients include Thales Luna HSM client 7.3 and Universal Client v 10.4.1 for key management integration.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CR 1.14(a)

> Sub-item (a) of CR 1.14 in IEC 62443-4-2. The licensed standard text is not reproduced at this granularity; refer to the CR 1.14 requirement in the standard.

**SCF Controls:** CRY-09.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides integrated symmetric key management capabilities through its Key Management Server (KMS) infrastructure and the VMware vCenter CryptoManager interface. VCF requires that the KMS support the Key Management Interoperability Protocol (KMIP) 1.1 standard and that it be capable of acting as a symmetric key foundry and server. The KMS must also appear in the VMware Compatibility Guide for Key Management Servers before it can be used in a VCF environment. Alternatively, the vSphere Native Key Provider can manage symmetric keys without requiring an external KMS and can coexist with existing key server infrastructure. This architecture centralizes symmetric key production and lifecycle management through a standards-based protocol, with vCenter as the orchestration layer between VCF components and the key provider.

VCF uses a key hierarchy for encryption operations. Key Encryption Keys (KEK) wrap Disk Encryption Keys (DEK), with DEKs using XTS-AES-256. VMware ESX hosts use the KEK to encrypt their internal keys and store only the encrypted internal keys on disk; the KEK itself is not persisted locally. Internal encryption keys are kept in memory only during operation, with only the encrypted data stored on disk. This separation of key tiers limits the exposure of key material at rest.

vSAN data-at-rest encryption relies on KMS integration for all symmetric key operations. Enabling vSAN encryption requires selecting a KMS cluster during configuration, establishing a trust relationship with the KMS by uploading certificate and key files, and confirming that the KMS is healthy before encryption can proceed. Administrators should deploy KMS servers on infrastructure separate from the vSAN datastore being encrypted and should use highly available KMS clusters to distribute the keys that protect vSAN datastores. Some KMS vendors support credential-based isolation of encryption keys, allowing administrators to specify a username and password during vSAN encryption configuration for key partitioning. An optional proxy port configuration is also available for KMS connectivity. vSAN also supports the vSphere Native Key Provider for encrypting virtual machines in vSAN clusters.

vSAN data-in-transit encryption uses symmetric keys that are generated dynamically and shared between hosts to encrypt inter-host traffic. The rekey interval for data-in-transit encryption is configurable, with a default of 1,440 minutes (24 hours) and a supported range of 30 minutes to 10,080 minutes (7 days). For data-at-rest encryption, vSAN supports generating new Key Encryption Keys (KEK) and new Data Encryption Keys (DEK), with the capability to re-encrypt all stored data using new keys when existing keys expire or become compromised. Both shallow recryption (replacing only KEKs via CryptoSpecShallowRecrypt) and deep recryption (replacing all KEKs and DEKs via CryptoSpecDeepRecrypt) are available through the API. vSAN OSA and vSAN ESA both support these key rotation options using AES-256 bit encryption. AES-NI hardware acceleration significantly improves encryption performance, and administrators should enable AES-NI in the host BIOS.

The vCenter CryptoManager managed object provides programmatic control over cryptographic key operations in vSphere, including integration with the KMS for key creation, retrieval, and lifecycle management. This API supports both vSAN encryption and virtual machine encryption workflows, where symmetric keys are managed through the same KMS infrastructure. The vSphere Web Services API also provides registerKmipServer() to register a KMIP key management server with vCenter's cryptography manager.

VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters running on the vSphere Supervisor extend symmetric key management to the Kubernetes control plane through the Kubernetes KMS provider framework. The kube-apiserver supports etcd encryption-at-rest through an EncryptionConfiguration resource that specifies a KMS provider type with a Unix domain socket endpoint connecting to an external key management system. This framework uses an envelope encryption scheme: data stored in etcd is encrypted with a DEK, which is itself encrypted by a KEK held in the external KMS, keeping the root of trust outside the cluster boundary. Kubernetes recommends the KMS provider over storing raw encryption keys inline in the EncryptionConfiguration file for this reason. KMS v2, stable from Kubernetes 1.29, uses the KmsV2Kdf feature gate to generate single-use DEKs from a secret seed combined with random data via a key derivation function, reducing call volume to the external KMS while maintaining per-object cryptographic isolation. KMS plugin implementations are required to verify the key_id of any wrapped key before attempting decryption, which supports key versioning and rotation workflows. Key rotation requires an administrator to explicitly trigger re-encryption of stored secrets after a KEK rotation; VKS provides the technical hooks for this process. For environments requiring FIPS-validated cryptographic operations at the Kubernetes layer, VKS supports TanzuKubernetesRelease (TKR) images with FIPS cryptographic module support, such as the vmware.3-fips.1-tkg.1 release variants, which configure Kubernetes node binaries to use a FIPS-validated module for symmetric operations within the cluster. The external KMS must independently be a FIPS-validated product or hardware security module.

VCF components use FIPS-validated cryptographic modules across the stack. VCF supports FIPS 140-2 requirements for cryptographic modules. On the vCenter appliance, VMware's Boring Crypto Module version 6.0 provides FIPS-validated support for AES, ECDSA, HMAC, RSA, and SHA2 variants; the VMware OpenSSL FIPS Object Module (versions 2.0.20-vmw and 3.0) also covers DRBG, DSA, SHS, and Triple-DES algorithms. NSX is configured to use FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules by default, with validation performed through the Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). NSX Manager uses FIPS 140 approved algorithms for authentication to cryptographic modules and generates unique session identifiers using a FIPS 140 approved random number generator. VCF Automation uses the BouncyCastle FIPS 2.0.0 cryptographic module, which is compliant with FIPS 140-3 (Certificate #4743). VCF Operations for Networks uses the BC-FJA (Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API) version 2.0.0 as its validated cryptographic module and can restrict cryptographic operations to FIPS-validated modules by enabling FIPS mode for external connections. VCF Operations for Networks also uses FIPS-compliant cryptographic algorithms for internal connections. VCF Operations orchestrator uses a FIPS-compliant AES with a 256-bit cipher key for encryption of strings.

VCF Operations supports a FIPS Security Mode that, when enabled, applies additional algorithm and cipher prerequisites across the platform. This mode restricts the cryptographic algorithms and ciphers available to those that meet FIPS validation requirements, including restricting key exchange algorithms to FIPS-approved options. This provides a platform-wide enforcement mechanism for FIPS-compliant symmetric key usage.

Two limitations apply in FIPS-sensitive environments. Self-signed certificates and private keys generated using OpenSSL are not FIPS-compatible; administrators should use FIPS-compatible key generation methods such as pyOpenSSL for PKCS#8 format. While NSX supports additional elliptic curve key types for certificates, non-FIPS-approved key types should not be used in environments requiring only FIPS-approved cryptographic keys.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) contributes to symmetric cryptographic key management through its requirements for consistent key management infrastructure across protected and recovery sites.

PNR and vSphere Replication require that either a KMIP-compatible Key Management Server (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider be available at both the protected and recovery sites to protect and recover encrypted virtual machines. The KMS cluster must be registered with the same name on both sites. When using the vSphere Native Key Provider, it must be manually configured on both the local and remote sites. The vSphere Native Key Provider enables encryption-related functionality for virtual machines and virtual disks without requiring an external KMS.

PNR establishes a trusted connection between VMware vCenter and the KMS from which vCenter retrieves keys as needed to protect virtual machine disks and other files. This cross-site KMS consistency is a prerequisite for encrypted VM failover and failback through recovery plans.

The generation and lifecycle management of symmetric keys, including the key hierarchy, FIPS validation of cryptographic modules, key rotation scheduling, and TPM-based key persistence, are capabilities of the VCF platform. PNR's contribution is the enforcement of consistent KMS deployment across recovery topologies, which is a necessary condition for encrypted workload protection and recovery to function.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

This control requires mechanisms for producing and managing symmetric cryptographic keys using FIPS-compliant key management technology and processes. VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) applies symmetric encryption across several operational contexts and exposes FIPS-related configuration options, but relies on the broader VCF platform and external tooling to satisfy the full scope of this requirement.

In DSM, the product enforces FIPS 140-3 compliant cryptographic standards and validates certificates during upload, rejecting unsupported algorithms or parameters. DSM specifically rejects the Ed25519 elliptic curve algorithm because it is not FIPS 140-3 compliant. Accepted key algorithms for custom certificates include ECDSA P-256, ECDSA P-384, and RSA keys with a minimum size of 2048 bits. This enforcement is documented in "Using Trusted Certificates in Data Services Manager > Configuring Custom Certificates for Data Services Manager > Supported Cryptographic Algorithms."

For MySQL databases managed by DSM, the ssl_fips_mode setting activates FIPS mode on the client side, restricting cipher negotiation to FIPS-approved algorithms. This setting is documented in "Databases in VMware Data Services Manager > Using MySQL in Data Services Manager > Configuration."

DSM also applies symmetric encryption in its user management subsystem. User passwords are stored encrypted using PGCrypto symmetric encryption with dedicated encryption keys, as documented in "Administering Data Services Manager > Managing LDAP Users in Data Services Manager." PostgreSQL databases managed by DSM include the pgcrypto extension version 1.3, which provides cryptographic functions available to database workloads.

The backup subsystem uses AES-256 to encrypt backup data written to S3-compatible storage, consistent with FIPS-approved algorithm requirements.

What DSM does not provide is a standalone FIPS-validated key management module or integration with an external key management service (KMS). The production, lifecycle management, rotation, and revocation of symmetric keys at the organizational level requires an external FIPS-validated KMS or hardware security module (HSM) operating at the infrastructure layer. DSM consumes keys and applies FIPS-approved symmetric algorithms, but the key generation and management controls for those keys are the responsibility of the organization and the underlying platform. Auditors should verify that the VCF infrastructure supporting DSM is configured with an appropriate FIPS-validated key provider, and that the organization has key management processes that cover the DSM-managed symmetric keys used for backups and internal encryption.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) supports a FIPS 140-3 compliance mode that uses OpenSSL FIPS Provider version 3.1.2 for all cryptographic operations. The OpenSSL FIPS Object Module v3.1.2 has been validated at FIPS 140-3 Security Level 1 and awarded Certificate #4985 by the Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). When FIPS mode is enabled on the Avi Controller and Service Engines, only cryptographic algorithms approved under FIPS 140-3 are used. FIPS 140-3 requires stricter handling of keys, authentication data, and random number generation than its predecessor, and aligns cryptographic module standards with international standards ISO/IEC 19790:2012 and ISO/IEC 24759:2017. This covers the symmetric encryption operations involved in TLS termination, re-encryption between Service Engines and backend servers, and internal control-plane communication. FIPS mode is configured at deployment time for Controller cluster deployments.

SSL/TLS Profile Templates in Avi give administrators control over the cipher suites and protocol versions used by virtual services. These profiles specify a prioritized cipher list and accepted protocol versions per virtual service and are managed under Templates > Security > SSL/TLS Profile in the Avi Controller UI. Configuring a FIPS-aligned SSL/TLS Profile and applying it to virtual services restricts symmetric key negotiation and cipher use across all load-balanced application traffic to FIPS-approved algorithms.

Post-quantum cryptography (PQC)-based Key Encapsulation Mechanisms and Signature Algorithms are not supported in Avi deployments running in FIPS mode.

For organizations deploying Avi in public cloud environments, Avi integrates with cloud-native key management services. In AWS, Avi's Cloud Connector integrates with AWS KMS for Service Engine disk and SQS queue encryption, supporting customer-managed keys with configurable IAM and key policies; the Avi Controller can list KMS encryption keys in the Avi UI and supports S3 and EBS encryption. In Google Cloud Platform, Avi supports Google Cloud KMS for Service Engine disk and image encryption, GCS bucket encryption, and GCS object encryption, using separate KMS keys for each resource type.

The Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) 2.1 adds support for configuring both RSA and ECC signed certificates simultaneously on a single virtual service through the alt.crt and alt.key fields in the default TLS secret, enabling certificate algorithm flexibility for Kubernetes ingress workloads.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control CR 1.14(b)

> Sub-item (b) of CR 1.14 in IEC 62443-4-2. The licensed standard text is not reproduced at this granularity; refer to the CR 1.14 requirement in the standard.

**SCF Controls:** CRY-09.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides integrated symmetric key management capabilities through its Key Management Server (KMS) infrastructure and the VMware vCenter CryptoManager interface. VCF requires that the KMS support the Key Management Interoperability Protocol (KMIP) 1.1 standard and that it be capable of acting as a symmetric key foundry and server. The KMS must also appear in the VMware Compatibility Guide for Key Management Servers before it can be used in a VCF environment. Alternatively, the vSphere Native Key Provider can manage symmetric keys without requiring an external KMS and can coexist with existing key server infrastructure. This architecture centralizes symmetric key production and lifecycle management through a standards-based protocol, with vCenter as the orchestration layer between VCF components and the key provider.

VCF uses a key hierarchy for encryption operations. Key Encryption Keys (KEK) wrap Disk Encryption Keys (DEK), with DEKs using XTS-AES-256. VMware ESX hosts use the KEK to encrypt their internal keys and store only the encrypted internal keys on disk; the KEK itself is not persisted locally. Internal encryption keys are kept in memory only during operation, with only the encrypted data stored on disk. This separation of key tiers limits the exposure of key material at rest.

vSAN data-at-rest encryption relies on KMS integration for all symmetric key operations. Enabling vSAN encryption requires selecting a KMS cluster during configuration, establishing a trust relationship with the KMS by uploading certificate and key files, and confirming that the KMS is healthy before encryption can proceed. Administrators should deploy KMS servers on infrastructure separate from the vSAN datastore being encrypted and should use highly available KMS clusters to distribute the keys that protect vSAN datastores. Some KMS vendors support credential-based isolation of encryption keys, allowing administrators to specify a username and password during vSAN encryption configuration for key partitioning. An optional proxy port configuration is also available for KMS connectivity. vSAN also supports the vSphere Native Key Provider for encrypting virtual machines in vSAN clusters.

vSAN data-in-transit encryption uses symmetric keys that are generated dynamically and shared between hosts to encrypt inter-host traffic. The rekey interval for data-in-transit encryption is configurable, with a default of 1,440 minutes (24 hours) and a supported range of 30 minutes to 10,080 minutes (7 days). For data-at-rest encryption, vSAN supports generating new Key Encryption Keys (KEK) and new Data Encryption Keys (DEK), with the capability to re-encrypt all stored data using new keys when existing keys expire or become compromised. Both shallow recryption (replacing only KEKs via CryptoSpecShallowRecrypt) and deep recryption (replacing all KEKs and DEKs via CryptoSpecDeepRecrypt) are available through the API. vSAN OSA and vSAN ESA both support these key rotation options using AES-256 bit encryption. AES-NI hardware acceleration significantly improves encryption performance, and administrators should enable AES-NI in the host BIOS.

The vCenter CryptoManager managed object provides programmatic control over cryptographic key operations in vSphere, including integration with the KMS for key creation, retrieval, and lifecycle management. This API supports both vSAN encryption and virtual machine encryption workflows, where symmetric keys are managed through the same KMS infrastructure. The vSphere Web Services API also provides registerKmipServer() to register a KMIP key management server with vCenter's cryptography manager.

VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters running on the vSphere Supervisor extend symmetric key management to the Kubernetes control plane through the Kubernetes KMS provider framework. The kube-apiserver supports etcd encryption-at-rest through an EncryptionConfiguration resource that specifies a KMS provider type with a Unix domain socket endpoint connecting to an external key management system. This framework uses an envelope encryption scheme: data stored in etcd is encrypted with a DEK, which is itself encrypted by a KEK held in the external KMS, keeping the root of trust outside the cluster boundary. Kubernetes recommends the KMS provider over storing raw encryption keys inline in the EncryptionConfiguration file for this reason. KMS v2, stable from Kubernetes 1.29, uses the KmsV2Kdf feature gate to generate single-use DEKs from a secret seed combined with random data via a key derivation function, reducing call volume to the external KMS while maintaining per-object cryptographic isolation. KMS plugin implementations are required to verify the key_id of any wrapped key before attempting decryption, which supports key versioning and rotation workflows. Key rotation requires an administrator to explicitly trigger re-encryption of stored secrets after a KEK rotation; VKS provides the technical hooks for this process. For environments requiring FIPS-validated cryptographic operations at the Kubernetes layer, VKS supports TanzuKubernetesRelease (TKR) images with FIPS cryptographic module support, such as the vmware.3-fips.1-tkg.1 release variants, which configure Kubernetes node binaries to use a FIPS-validated module for symmetric operations within the cluster. The external KMS must independently be a FIPS-validated product or hardware security module.

VCF components use FIPS-validated cryptographic modules across the stack. VCF supports FIPS 140-2 requirements for cryptographic modules. On the vCenter appliance, VMware's Boring Crypto Module version 6.0 provides FIPS-validated support for AES, ECDSA, HMAC, RSA, and SHA2 variants; the VMware OpenSSL FIPS Object Module (versions 2.0.20-vmw and 3.0) also covers DRBG, DSA, SHS, and Triple-DES algorithms. NSX is configured to use FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules by default, with validation performed through the Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). NSX Manager uses FIPS 140 approved algorithms for authentication to cryptographic modules and generates unique session identifiers using a FIPS 140 approved random number generator. VCF Automation uses the BouncyCastle FIPS 2.0.0 cryptographic module, which is compliant with FIPS 140-3 (Certificate #4743). VCF Operations for Networks uses the BC-FJA (Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API) version 2.0.0 as its validated cryptographic module and can restrict cryptographic operations to FIPS-validated modules by enabling FIPS mode for external connections. VCF Operations for Networks also uses FIPS-compliant cryptographic algorithms for internal connections. VCF Operations orchestrator uses a FIPS-compliant AES with a 256-bit cipher key for encryption of strings.

VCF Operations supports a FIPS Security Mode that, when enabled, applies additional algorithm and cipher prerequisites across the platform. This mode restricts the cryptographic algorithms and ciphers available to those that meet FIPS validation requirements, including restricting key exchange algorithms to FIPS-approved options. This provides a platform-wide enforcement mechanism for FIPS-compliant symmetric key usage.

Two limitations apply in FIPS-sensitive environments. Self-signed certificates and private keys generated using OpenSSL are not FIPS-compatible; administrators should use FIPS-compatible key generation methods such as pyOpenSSL for PKCS#8 format. While NSX supports additional elliptic curve key types for certificates, non-FIPS-approved key types should not be used in environments requiring only FIPS-approved cryptographic keys.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) contributes to symmetric cryptographic key management through its requirements for consistent key management infrastructure across protected and recovery sites.

PNR and vSphere Replication require that either a KMIP-compatible Key Management Server (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider be available at both the protected and recovery sites to protect and recover encrypted virtual machines. The KMS cluster must be registered with the same name on both sites. When using the vSphere Native Key Provider, it must be manually configured on both the local and remote sites. The vSphere Native Key Provider enables encryption-related functionality for virtual machines and virtual disks without requiring an external KMS.

PNR establishes a trusted connection between VMware vCenter and the KMS from which vCenter retrieves keys as needed to protect virtual machine disks and other files. This cross-site KMS consistency is a prerequisite for encrypted VM failover and failback through recovery plans.

The generation and lifecycle management of symmetric keys, including the key hierarchy, FIPS validation of cryptographic modules, key rotation scheduling, and TPM-based key persistence, are capabilities of the VCF platform. PNR's contribution is the enforcement of consistent KMS deployment across recovery topologies, which is a necessary condition for encrypted workload protection and recovery to function.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

This control requires mechanisms for producing and managing symmetric cryptographic keys using FIPS-compliant key management technology and processes. VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) applies symmetric encryption across several operational contexts and exposes FIPS-related configuration options, but relies on the broader VCF platform and external tooling to satisfy the full scope of this requirement.

In DSM, the product enforces FIPS 140-3 compliant cryptographic standards and validates certificates during upload, rejecting unsupported algorithms or parameters. DSM specifically rejects the Ed25519 elliptic curve algorithm because it is not FIPS 140-3 compliant. Accepted key algorithms for custom certificates include ECDSA P-256, ECDSA P-384, and RSA keys with a minimum size of 2048 bits. This enforcement is documented in "Using Trusted Certificates in Data Services Manager > Configuring Custom Certificates for Data Services Manager > Supported Cryptographic Algorithms."

For MySQL databases managed by DSM, the ssl_fips_mode setting activates FIPS mode on the client side, restricting cipher negotiation to FIPS-approved algorithms. This setting is documented in "Databases in VMware Data Services Manager > Using MySQL in Data Services Manager > Configuration."

DSM also applies symmetric encryption in its user management subsystem. User passwords are stored encrypted using PGCrypto symmetric encryption with dedicated encryption keys, as documented in "Administering Data Services Manager > Managing LDAP Users in Data Services Manager." The backup subsystem uses AES-256 to encrypt backup data written to S3-compatible storage, consistent with FIPS-approved algorithm requirements.

What DSM does not provide is a standalone FIPS-validated key management module or integration with an external key management service (KMS). The production, lifecycle management, rotation, and revocation of symmetric keys at the organizational level requires an external FIPS-validated KMS or hardware security module (HSM) operating at the infrastructure layer. DSM consumes keys and applies FIPS-approved symmetric algorithms, but the key generation and management controls for those keys are the responsibility of the organization and the underlying platform. Auditors should verify that the VCF infrastructure supporting DSM is configured with an appropriate FIPS-validated key provider, and that the organization has key management processes that cover the DSM-managed symmetric keys used for backups and internal encryption.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) supports a FIPS 140-3 compliance mode that uses OpenSSL FIPS Provider version 3.1.2 for all cryptographic operations. The OpenSSL FIPS Object Module v3.1.2 has been validated at FIPS 140-3 Security Level 1 and awarded Certificate #4985 by the Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). When FIPS mode is enabled on the Avi Controller and Service Engines, only cryptographic algorithms approved under FIPS 140-3 are used. FIPS 140-3 requires stricter handling of keys, authentication data, and random number generation than its predecessor, and aligns cryptographic module standards with international standards ISO/IEC 19790:2012 and ISO/IEC 24759:2017. This covers the symmetric encryption operations involved in TLS termination, re-encryption between Service Engines and backend servers, and internal control-plane communication. FIPS mode is configured at deployment time for Controller cluster deployments.

SSL/TLS Profile Templates in Avi give administrators control over the cipher suites and protocol versions used by virtual services. These profiles specify a prioritized cipher list and accepted protocol versions per virtual service and are managed under Templates > Security > SSL/TLS Profile in the Avi Controller UI. Configuring a FIPS-aligned SSL/TLS Profile and applying it to virtual services restricts symmetric key negotiation and cipher use across all load-balanced application traffic to FIPS-approved algorithms.

Post-quantum cryptography (PQC)-based Key Encapsulation Mechanisms and Signature Algorithms are not supported in Avi deployments running in FIPS mode.

For organizations deploying Avi in public cloud environments, Avi integrates with cloud-native key management services. In AWS, Avi's Cloud Connector integrates with AWS KMS for Service Engine disk and SQS queue encryption, supporting customer-managed keys with configurable IAM and key policies; the Avi Controller can list KMS encryption keys in the Avi UI and supports S3 and EBS encryption. In Google Cloud Platform, Avi supports Google Cloud KMS for Service Engine disk and image encryption, GCS bucket encryption, and GCS object encryption, using separate KMS keys for each resource type.

The Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) 2.1 adds support for configuring both RSA and ECC signed certificates simultaneously on a single virtual service through the alt.crt and alt.key fields in the default TLS secret, enabling certificate algorithm flexibility for Kubernetes ingress workloads.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control CR 1.14(c)

> Sub-item (c) of CR 1.14 in IEC 62443-4-2. The licensed standard text is not reproduced at this granularity; refer to the CR 1.14 requirement in the standard.

**SCF Controls:** CRY-09.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides integrated symmetric key management capabilities through its Key Management Server (KMS) infrastructure and the VMware vCenter CryptoManager interface. VCF requires that the KMS support the Key Management Interoperability Protocol (KMIP) 1.1 standard and that it be capable of acting as a symmetric key foundry and server. The KMS must also appear in the VMware Compatibility Guide for Key Management Servers before it can be used in a VCF environment. Alternatively, the vSphere Native Key Provider can manage symmetric keys without requiring an external KMS and can coexist with existing key server infrastructure. This architecture centralizes symmetric key production and lifecycle management through a standards-based protocol, with vCenter as the orchestration layer between VCF components and the key provider.

VCF uses a key hierarchy for encryption operations. Key Encryption Keys (KEK) wrap Disk Encryption Keys (DEK), with DEKs using XTS-AES-256. VMware ESX hosts use the KEK to encrypt their internal keys and store only the encrypted internal keys on disk; the KEK itself is not persisted locally. Internal encryption keys are kept in memory only during operation, with only the encrypted data stored on disk. This separation of key tiers limits the exposure of key material at rest.

vSAN data-at-rest encryption relies on KMS integration for all symmetric key operations. Enabling vSAN encryption requires selecting a KMS cluster during configuration, establishing a trust relationship with the KMS by uploading certificate and key files, and confirming that the KMS is healthy before encryption can proceed. Administrators should deploy KMS servers on infrastructure separate from the vSAN datastore being encrypted and should use highly available KMS clusters to distribute the keys that protect vSAN datastores. Some KMS vendors support credential-based isolation of encryption keys, allowing administrators to specify a username and password during vSAN encryption configuration for key partitioning. An optional proxy port configuration is also available for KMS connectivity. vSAN also supports the vSphere Native Key Provider for encrypting virtual machines in vSAN clusters.

vSAN data-in-transit encryption uses symmetric keys that are generated dynamically and shared between hosts to encrypt inter-host traffic. The rekey interval for data-in-transit encryption is configurable, with a default of 1,440 minutes (24 hours) and a supported range of 30 minutes to 10,080 minutes (7 days). For data-at-rest encryption, vSAN supports generating new Key Encryption Keys (KEK) and new Data Encryption Keys (DEK), with the capability to re-encrypt all stored data using new keys when existing keys expire or become compromised. Both shallow recryption (replacing only KEKs via CryptoSpecShallowRecrypt) and deep recryption (replacing all KEKs and DEKs via CryptoSpecDeepRecrypt) are available through the API. vSAN OSA and vSAN ESA both support these key rotation options using AES-256 bit encryption. AES-NI hardware acceleration significantly improves encryption performance, and administrators should enable AES-NI in the host BIOS.

The vCenter CryptoManager managed object provides programmatic control over cryptographic key operations in vSphere, including integration with the KMS for key creation, retrieval, and lifecycle management. This API supports both vSAN encryption and virtual machine encryption workflows, where symmetric keys are managed through the same KMS infrastructure. The vSphere Web Services API also provides registerKmipServer() to register a KMIP key management server with vCenter's cryptography manager.

VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters running on the vSphere Supervisor extend symmetric key management to the Kubernetes control plane through the Kubernetes KMS provider framework. The kube-apiserver supports etcd encryption-at-rest through an EncryptionConfiguration resource that specifies a KMS provider type with a Unix domain socket endpoint connecting to an external key management system. This framework uses an envelope encryption scheme: data stored in etcd is encrypted with a DEK, which is itself encrypted by a KEK held in the external KMS, keeping the root of trust outside the cluster boundary. Kubernetes recommends the KMS provider over storing raw encryption keys inline in the EncryptionConfiguration file for this reason. KMS v2, stable from Kubernetes 1.29, uses the KmsV2Kdf feature gate to generate single-use DEKs from a secret seed combined with random data via a key derivation function, reducing call volume to the external KMS while maintaining per-object cryptographic isolation. KMS plugin implementations are required to verify the key_id of any wrapped key before attempting decryption, which supports key versioning and rotation workflows. Key rotation requires an administrator to explicitly trigger re-encryption of stored secrets after a KEK rotation; VKS provides the technical hooks for this process. For environments requiring FIPS-validated cryptographic operations at the Kubernetes layer, VKS supports TanzuKubernetesRelease (TKR) images with FIPS cryptographic module support, such as the vmware.3-fips.1-tkg.1 release variants, which configure Kubernetes node binaries to use a FIPS-validated module for symmetric operations within the cluster. The external KMS must independently be a FIPS-validated product or hardware security module.

VCF components use FIPS-validated cryptographic modules across the stack. VCF supports FIPS 140-2 requirements for cryptographic modules. On the vCenter appliance, VMware's Boring Crypto Module version 6.0 provides FIPS-validated support for AES, ECDSA, HMAC, RSA, and SHA2 variants; the VMware OpenSSL FIPS Object Module (versions 2.0.20-vmw and 3.0) also covers DRBG, DSA, SHS, and Triple-DES algorithms. NSX is configured to use FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules by default, with validation performed through the Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). NSX Manager uses FIPS 140 approved algorithms for authentication to cryptographic modules and generates unique session identifiers using a FIPS 140 approved random number generator. VCF Automation uses the BouncyCastle FIPS 2.0.0 cryptographic module, which is compliant with FIPS 140-3 (Certificate #4743). VCF Operations for Networks uses the BC-FJA (Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API) version 2.0.0 as its validated cryptographic module and can restrict cryptographic operations to FIPS-validated modules by enabling FIPS mode for external connections. VCF Operations for Networks also uses FIPS-compliant cryptographic algorithms for internal connections. VCF Operations orchestrator uses a FIPS-compliant AES with a 256-bit cipher key for encryption of strings.

VCF Operations supports a FIPS Security Mode that, when enabled, applies additional algorithm and cipher prerequisites across the platform. This mode restricts the cryptographic algorithms and ciphers available to those that meet FIPS validation requirements, including restricting key exchange algorithms to FIPS-approved options. This provides a platform-wide enforcement mechanism for FIPS-compliant symmetric key usage.

Two limitations apply in FIPS-sensitive environments. Self-signed certificates and private keys generated using OpenSSL are not FIPS-compatible; administrators should use FIPS-compatible key generation methods such as pyOpenSSL for PKCS#8 format. While NSX supports additional elliptic curve key types for certificates, non-FIPS-approved key types should not be used in environments requiring only FIPS-approved cryptographic keys.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) contributes to symmetric cryptographic key management through its requirements for consistent key management infrastructure across protected and recovery sites.

PNR and vSphere Replication require that either a KMIP-compatible Key Management Server (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider be available at both the protected and recovery sites to protect and recover encrypted virtual machines. The KMS cluster must be registered with the same name on both sites. When using the vSphere Native Key Provider, it must be manually configured on both the local and remote sites. The vSphere Native Key Provider enables encryption-related functionality for virtual machines and virtual disks without requiring an external KMS.

PNR establishes a trusted connection between VMware vCenter and the KMS from which vCenter retrieves keys as needed to protect virtual machine disks and other files. This cross-site KMS consistency is a prerequisite for encrypted VM failover and failback through recovery plans.

The generation and lifecycle management of symmetric keys, including the key hierarchy, FIPS validation of cryptographic modules, key rotation scheduling, and TPM-based key persistence, are capabilities of the VCF platform. PNR's contribution is the enforcement of consistent KMS deployment across recovery topologies, which is a necessary condition for encrypted workload protection and recovery to function.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

This control requires mechanisms for producing and managing symmetric cryptographic keys using FIPS-compliant key management technology and processes. VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) applies symmetric encryption across several operational contexts and exposes FIPS-related configuration options, but relies on the broader VCF platform and external tooling to satisfy the full scope of this requirement.

In DSM, the product enforces FIPS 140-3 compliant cryptographic standards and validates certificates during upload, rejecting unsupported algorithms or parameters. DSM specifically rejects the Ed25519 elliptic curve algorithm because it is not FIPS 140-3 compliant. Accepted key algorithms for custom certificates include ECDSA P-256, ECDSA P-384, and RSA keys with a minimum size of 2048 bits. This enforcement is documented in "Using Trusted Certificates in Data Services Manager > Configuring Custom Certificates for Data Services Manager > Supported Cryptographic Algorithms."

For MySQL databases managed by DSM, the ssl_fips_mode setting activates FIPS mode on the client side, restricting cipher negotiation to FIPS-approved algorithms. This setting is documented in "Databases in VMware Data Services Manager > Using MySQL in Data Services Manager > Configuration."

DSM also applies symmetric encryption in its user management subsystem. User passwords are stored encrypted using PGCrypto symmetric encryption with dedicated encryption keys, as documented in "Administering Data Services Manager > Managing LDAP Users in Data Services Manager." PostgreSQL databases managed by DSM include the pgcrypto extension version 1.3, which provides cryptographic functions available to database workloads.

The backup subsystem uses AES-256 to encrypt backup data written to S3-compatible storage, consistent with FIPS-approved algorithm requirements.

What DSM does not provide is a standalone FIPS-validated key management module or integration with an external key management service (KMS). The production, lifecycle management, rotation, and revocation of symmetric keys at the organizational level requires an external FIPS-validated KMS or hardware security module (HSM) operating at the infrastructure layer. DSM consumes keys and applies FIPS-approved symmetric algorithms, but the key generation and management controls for those keys are the responsibility of the organization and the underlying platform. Auditors should verify that the VCF infrastructure supporting DSM is configured with an appropriate FIPS-validated key provider, and that the organization has key management processes that cover the DSM-managed symmetric keys used for backups and internal encryption.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) supports a FIPS 140-3 compliance mode that uses OpenSSL FIPS Provider version 3.1.2 for all cryptographic operations. The OpenSSL FIPS Object Module v3.1.2 has been validated at FIPS 140-3 Security Level 1 and awarded Certificate #4985 by the Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). When FIPS mode is enabled on the Avi Controller and Service Engines, only cryptographic algorithms approved under FIPS 140-3 are used. FIPS 140-3 requires stricter handling of keys, authentication data, and random number generation than its predecessor, and aligns cryptographic module standards with international standards ISO/IEC 19790:2012 and ISO/IEC 24759:2017. This covers the symmetric encryption operations involved in TLS termination, re-encryption between Service Engines and backend servers, and internal control-plane communication. FIPS mode is configured at deployment time for Controller cluster deployments.

SSL/TLS Profile Templates in Avi give administrators control over the cipher suites and protocol versions used by virtual services. These profiles specify a prioritized cipher list and accepted protocol versions per virtual service and are managed under Templates > Security > SSL/TLS Profile in the Avi Controller UI. Configuring a FIPS-aligned SSL/TLS Profile and applying it to virtual services restricts symmetric key negotiation and cipher use across all load-balanced application traffic to FIPS-approved algorithms.

Post-quantum cryptography (PQC)-based Key Encapsulation Mechanisms and Signature Algorithms are not supported in Avi deployments running in FIPS mode.

For organizations deploying Avi in public cloud environments, Avi integrates with cloud-native key management services. In AWS, Avi's Cloud Connector integrates with AWS KMS for Service Engine disk and SQS queue encryption, supporting customer-managed keys with configurable IAM and key policies; the Avi Controller can list KMS encryption keys in the Avi UI and supports S3 and EBS encryption. In Google Cloud Platform, Avi supports Google Cloud KMS for Service Engine disk and image encryption, GCS bucket encryption, and GCS object encryption, using separate KMS keys for each resource type.

The Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) 2.1 adds support for configuring both RSA and ECC signed certificates simultaneously on a single virtual service through the alt.crt and alt.key fields in the default TLS secret, enabling certificate algorithm flexibility for Kubernetes ingress workloads.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

VCF components use FIPS-validated cryptographic modules across the stack. VCF supports FIPS 140-2 requirements for cryptographic modules. On the vCenter appliance, VMware's Boring Crypto Module version 6.0 provides FIPS-validated support for AES, ECDSA, HMAC, RSA, and SHA2 variants; the VMware OpenSSL FIPS Object Module (versions 2.0.20-vmw and 3.0) also covers DRBG, DSA, SHS, and Triple-DES algorithms. VCF Operations for Networks uses the BC-FJA (Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API) version 2.0.0 as its validated cryptographic module and can restrict cryptographic operations to FIPS-validated modules by enabling FIPS mode for external connections. VCF Operations for Networks also uses FIPS-compliant cryptographic algorithms for internal connections. VCF Operations orchestrator uses a FIPS-compliant AES with a 256-bit cipher key for encryption of strings.

VCF Operations supports a FIPS Security Mode that, when enabled, applies additional algorithm and cipher prerequisites across the platform. This mode restricts the cryptographic algorithms and ciphers available to those that meet FIPS validation requirements, including restricting key exchange algorithms to FIPS-approved options. This provides a platform-wide enforcement mechanism for FIPS-compliant symmetric key usage.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control CR 2.1

> Authorization enforcement: Enforce assigned privileges to all users and software processes.  Limit use of privileged accounts: Restrict the use of privileged accounts to only necessary tasks.  Mapping of users to roles: Support the mapping of users to specific authorized roles.  Supervisor override: Capability for a supervisor to override certain locks/blocks.  Dual control: Require two authorized individuals to perform certain sensitive actions.  Response to audit processing failures: Capability to protect audit record generation in case of failure.  Timestamps: Capability to include timestamps in audit records.  Time synchronization: Capability to synchronize the component clock with an NTP source.  Time synchronization protection: Protect the time synchronization process from tampering.  Non-repudiation: Capability to provide evidence that a user performed a specific action.  Non-repudiation for digital signatures: Use of digital signatures for non-repudiation of origin/receipt.  Physical tamper resistance and detection: Capability to detect physical tampering of the component.

**SCF Controls:** IAC-08

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF enforces role-based access control across all major platform components, restricting access to technology assets, applications, services, and data based on assigned roles with defined permissions. RBAC is applied consistently throughout the stack, covering compute management, networking, storage, operations management, automation services, and Kubernetes workloads.

VMware vCenter provides fine-grained authorization through a permissions model that associates roles with users or groups on objects in the vCenter inventory hierarchy. Roles are sets of privileges, and vCenter defines three authorization levels: operator (read-only access to configuration, the most restricted level), administrator (read and write access to configuration but cannot manage user accounts, intended for users who alter vCenter configuration or exercise control functions), and super administrator (full control). When a predefined role is modified, the updated privilege set applies immediately to all users assigned that role. New roles are created with three default system-defined privileges (System.Anonymous, System.Read, System.View), and administrators add specific privileges to match the intended access level. vCenter also supports creation of custom roles with granular privilege assignment and permission propagation to vSphere objects in the inventory hierarchy.

NSX implements RBAC by assigning users predefined roles with specific permissions. The platform includes 15 built-in roles: Enterprise Admin, Admin, Network Admin, Network Operator, Security Admin, Security Operator, Cloud Admin, Cloud Operator, Cloud Project Admin, Load Balancer Admin, Load Balancer Operator, VPN Admin, GI Platform Admin, NSX Platform Admin, and Support Bundle Collector. These roles follow the principle of least privilege, with each role carrying only the permissions needed for its functional area. NSX RBAC applies across multiple operational domains including inventory, plan and troubleshoot, and system management functions. NSX also supports project-scoped RBAC, where Network Admin, Network Operator, Security Admin, and Security Operator roles assigned within a project have permissions limited to that project's scope rather than the entire NSX system. This project-scoped model extends to VPCs and subnets within the NSX networking hierarchy. For organizations that need role definitions beyond the built-in set, NSX allows creation of custom roles tailored to specific operational requirements. Only an Enterprise Administrator can assign the role management permission to a custom role. NSX Federation extends RBAC to multi-site deployments, applying the same access control model across federated NSX instances. Principal identities, which are used for service-to-service authentication local to NSX, also support RBAC role assignment, so automated integrations operate under the same access control model as human users.

VCF Operations enforces RBAC through the assignment of service roles to users and groups. Administrators configure role-based permissions through the Roles tab under Infrastructure Operations, where individual permissions can be selected or deselected to define what each role is authorized to do. The System Administrator role includes rights covering approvals, audits, certificates, datastores, edge clusters, integrations, LDAP settings, namespaces, proxy rules, system settings, tokens, and truststore operations. Administrative operations such as password management require authentication with a user assigned the ADMIN role. VCF Operations also defines its own roles within vCenter, with privileges named using a convention that appends the role to the product name (such as VCF Operations ContentAdmin Role and VCF Operations PowerUser Role). The PowerUser role has privileges to perform the actions of the Administrator role except for user management and cluster management. A further restricted PowerUserMinusRemediation role excludes remediation actions in addition to those limitations. A vCenter administrator assigns these roles to users, and VCF Operations maps vCenter users to the appropriate role.

vSAN supports RBAC and permissions management for authentication and authorization of storage operations. The vSAN Security Configuration Guide provides guidance on configuring these role-based controls for storage infrastructure.

VCF Automation governs access through multiple RBAC mechanisms at different scopes. At the organization level, rights bundles assign specific API permissions to roles including Organization Administrator, Organization Auditor, and Organization User. Group membership and role assignment are managed through the Access Control interface, and organization administrators can edit role assignments for groups at any time. At the project level, VCF Automation defines three distinct roles: administrators, members, and viewers, providing tiered access to project resources and operations. VCF Automation also governs blueprints, catalogs, content libraries, and custom entities through its role model. The Assembler Administrator role has read and write access to the entire user interface and API resources, while other roles have progressively narrower permissions.

For Kubernetes workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), VCF enforces RBAC through two complementary control layers. At the vSphere layer, vSphere Namespaces assign three permission roles: Owner (full control including namespace deletion), Can Edit (provisioning and operating workloads), and Can View (read-only access).
