Organizations must define the threshold percentages, write the Prometheus alerting rules, and configure Alertmanager routing and receivers; the platform provides the instrumentation and delivery infrastructure but does not ship pre-configured rules for event log storage thresholds.

Workload logs from vSphere Namespaces can be streamed to a remote Syslog server by applying the LogAgentConfiguration custom resource to a namespace; each log message includes metadata fields such as namespace name, pod UID, pod name, container name, and VMware ESX host name, preserving context for capacity monitoring at the receiving system. Fluent Bit, available as a VKS standard package, can forward logs from cluster workloads to Syslog, HTTP, Elasticsearch, Kafka, and Splunk destinations, enabling integration with external log storage and monitoring systems that perform their own capacity alerting. When configured for audit log collection, Fluent Bit reads kube-apiserver audit logs in JSON format from /var/log/kubernetes/kube-apiserver.log, with a configurable memory buffer limit of 50 MB per instance. The Kubernetes audit log backend supports configurable truncation through the audit-log-truncate-enabled flag and the audit-log-truncate-max-event-size parameter, which limit individual event sizes and batch sizes sent to the underlying backend and allow administrators to control the rate of audit log storage consumption. Audit event truncation is disabled by default and must be explicitly enabled via API server configuration before the size parameters take effect.

VCF Operations for Networks includes disk utilization alerts viewable on the platform dashboard, the collector dashboard, or in the Infrastructure and Support > Infrastructure and Updates page. These alerts surface storage pressure on the network monitoring components that collect and store network flow data and logs.

VCF Operations also publishes Capacity Analytics metrics for disk space, calculating capacity remaining in GB as the maximum point between usable capacity and projected utilization between now and three days into the future. This forward-looking analysis gives administrators advance warning before storage is actually exhausted, rather than only alerting after the fact. Datastore capacity metric trends can be checked to identify spikes or abnormal growth in storage usage. VCF Automation provides an Alerts page that surfaces capacity, performance, and availability issues, enabling cloud administrators to react proactively before resources are exhausted.

When capacity thresholds are reached and alerts fire, VCF provides multiple automated notification channels to reach appropriate personnel. vCenter can send SNMP traps and email notifications when alarms are triggered, with configurable recipient addresses. vCenter's SNMP agent supports v1 and v2c traps as well as v3 traps with all security levels. The vCenter SNMP agent also supports notification filtering, allowing administrators to suppress specific notifications that should not reach their SNMP management software. VCF PowerCLI provides the New-AlarmAction command to create SNMP alarm actions for alarm definitions programmatically, enabling infrastructure-as-code approaches to alarm notification configuration. VCF Operations can send alert notifications via SNMP trap to up to four SNMP trap destinations, with filtering available when defining notifications. These notification mechanisms allow organizations to route capacity alerts to the appropriate teams through their existing monitoring and incident management infrastructure.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) generates alarms when allocated resources exceed predetermined threshold values, alerting administrators to capacity conditions that may affect analytics and detection services. A separate alarm mechanism in the Distributed Firewall Security Overview Dashboard triggers and remains active when dropped events are detected, notifying administrators of data loss conditions at the event collection layer.

For organizations implementing threshold-based log storage capacity alerting at organization-defined percentages, vDefend provides multiple log forwarding integration points. VMware vDefend Distributed IDS/IPS can be configured to send IDS events to external syslog consumers via the ids_events_to_syslog setting (disabled by default). vDefend appliances emit RFC 5424-compliant syslog messages, and VMware ESX hosts emit RFC 3164-compliant messages, making vDefend log streams compatible with standard syslog infrastructure and SIEM platforms. SSP SIEM integration sends Network Detection and Response (NDR) campaign and event logs to the connected SIEM as they occur, including logs generated when new detections are created, when existing detections are updated, and when new campaigns are identified. Identity Firewall Event Log Sources can be integrated with VCF Log Management for centralized log collection and monitoring.

Native configurable alerting to designated personnel at an organization-defined percentage of log storage capacity is not a documented vDefend feature; this function is provided by an external monitoring platform or SIEM that ingests vDefend log streams and implements capacity-based threshold alerting.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

Protection and Recovery (PNR) monitors free disk space on the appliance host and raises a Warning event, logged as LowDiskSpaceEvent, when disk space falls below a configured threshold. PNR alarms can be configured to send email notifications, SNMP traps, or run scripts on the VMware vCenter host when threshold conditions are met. Configuring PNR alarms to deliver email notifications requires that Mail settings be configured in vCenter. PNR appliance registration requires that an administrator email address be provided, establishing the notification path for disk space and other system events.

PNR log storage is managed through a configurable file count limit. The maxFileNum parameter controls the maximum number of log files retained by the PNR Server, with a default of 20 files. When log files reach their configured size, older files are compressed to keep storage consumption bounded.

The PNR Appliance supports forwarding log files to a remote syslog server for centralized log analysis. PNR also supports forwarding events to VCF Log Management version 9.0 and above. In Live Cyber Recovery (LCR) deployments, the Clean Room Orchestrator can forward events to a Microsoft Azure Log Analytics Workspace using Data Collection Rules and Data Collection Endpoints, extending event visibility to external log management systems.

Percentage-based log storage capacity alerting with organization-configurable thresholds depends on the broader VCF platform monitoring stack. VCF Operations provides an advanced alert system for monitored objects that includes health alerts for protection groups, recovery plans, cloud file systems, recovery SDDCs, and protected sites.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) monitors storage capacity on the data disk and generates global alerts when usage reaches threshold values. A WARNING alert fires when storage on the data disk reaches 70% usage, and a CRITICAL alert fires when it reaches 90%. In DSM, both thresholds are exposed as named settings (data-disk-warning-threshold and data-disk-critical-threshold) in the Configure the Storage on a Data Disk section, giving administrators a documented location to review and adjust these values when sizing storage. The alerts are designed to notify administrators of capacity conditions that may affect backup and WAL archival operations, both of which depend on available storage to function correctly. The alert system operates automatically without administrator intervention and is visible through the Global Alerts Reference in the DSM interface.

DSM uses a three-level severity model for alerts: OK, WARNING, and CRITICAL. The DATA_DISK_HEALTH_ALERT type is associated with disk usage thresholds and is surfaced in the database monitoring view under Database Alerts and Metrics. When this alert fires, administrators should increase the data disk size to prevent data service outage or read-only mode conditions. DSM also documents the ability to change the storage amount directly when disk warning or critical alerts are observed, allowing remediation within DSM itself without external tooling.

For alert delivery, DSM supports webhook-based notifications that can route alert events to email recipients or to external applications such as Slack or ServiceNow. This allows organizations to connect DSM storage capacity alerts to their existing incident management or on-call workflows. Configuring these notification targets is an administrative step performed by the DSM Provider administrator.

The organization is responsible for defining which personnel receive these alerts and for configuring the notification delivery targets. Organizations that require alerting at thresholds other than DSM's defaults should verify whether the named threshold settings meet their requirements, or supplement DSM's built-in alerts with external monitoring tools that observe DSM disk metrics.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) includes an automated disk utilization alerting mechanism through its configurable metric-based alert framework. The Alert feature processes system events and metrics against organization-defined threshold rules and generates alerts that initiate configured notification actions. When disk usage on a Service Engine exceeds a configured threshold percentage, the Controller generates an alert and delivers it to appropriate personnel. The threshold percentage is organization-defined, allowing teams to set the level at which alerting fires.

The Avi Controller supports multiple alert notification destinations: email, syslog, SNMP v2c and SNMPv3 traps, ControlScript automation, and Splunk. These destinations are configured in the Operations > Notifications page. The Syslog-Audit-Persistence template supports streaming audit events to external syslog servers, integrating Avi alerts into centralized log management systems. SNMP notifications support both v2c and v3 protocols, with optional TLS encryption and strict certificate verification for secure delivery to SNMP-based monitoring infrastructure. Multiple SNMP servers can be configured simultaneously, and the Avi Controller cluster leader issues SNMP trap notifications based on system events.

Service Engine log storage is configurable through SE runtime properties. The debug-logs-capacity is calculated as (SE Total Disk minus Maximum Storage not allocated for logs on SE) divided by 100. Client log capacity is derived from total SE disk space minus the debug log allocation, with further adjustments based on configured minimum storage and SE RAM. Increasing disk resources on Controllers and Service Engines extends the volume of logs that can be stored and the overall retention duration.

For deployments using the Avi Kubernetes Operator for VCF clusters, the AKO persistentVolumeClaimSpec.storage parameter allocates a default of 2Gi of storage for the log Persistent Volume Claim, which can be adjusted at deployment time to accommodate larger log volumes.

To activate disk utilization alerting, administrators must configure a metric-based alert with an appropriate threshold and assign at least one notification destination with the relevant recipient addresses, trap server endpoints, or syslog server details in the Operations > Notifications page. Avi system component debug logs and Event Logs are not forwarded to remote syslog servers through OS-level syslog configuration; they require Avi-specific alert action configuration to reach external logging systems. These organizational configuration steps are required before automated alerting is active.

### Control CR 2.9(a)

> Sub-item (a) of CR 2.9 in IEC 62443-4-2. The licensed standard text is not reproduced at this granularity; refer to the CR 2.9 requirement in the standard.

**SCF Controls:** MON-04

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides multiple layers of log storage capacity management across its component products, covering local log retention controls, centralized log aggregation with scalable architecture, proactive capacity monitoring with forward-looking projections, and Kubernetes-native container log bounding for workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS).

At the VMware ESX host level, administrators can configure log file retention and rotation policies. The log.keepOld parameter controls how many log files are retained, and when that limit is reached, the oldest file is automatically deleted. VMware recommends a log file size of 500 KB per file for host logs. The syslog directory location is configurable through the Syslog.global.logDir parameter, allowing administrators to direct log output to a storage volume with sufficient capacity. These same retention controls apply to virtual machine log files, where the number of retained log files can be limited through the log.keepOld setting. Per-folder log size limits prevent any single log source from consuming disproportionate storage, and logging levels can be adjusted per log folder to balance detail against storage consumption. ESX also exposes dedicated audit record storage parameters: the Syslog.global.auditRecord.storageCapacity parameter defines the size of the local audit record storage directory in MiB, and this capacity can be set or adjusted at any time using the esxcli system auditrecords local set --size command. VMware vCenter provides equivalent log rotation controls, with the ability to limit the number of retained log files through the same log.keepOld parameter.

For VKS clusters, the kubelet exposes two key parameters for container log management that can be configured through ClusterClass variables during cluster provisioning. The containerLogMaxSizeMiB setting defines the maximum size of a container log file before rotation, with a default of 10 MiB. The containerLogMaxFiles setting controls how many rotated log files are retained per container, with a valid range of 1 to 100 and a default of 5; the oldest files are removed automatically when the limit is exceeded during rotation. Together these parameters establish a predictable ceiling on per-node storage consumption from container logs. The Kubernetes API server's audit log backend provides complementary controls: the audit-log-truncate-enabled flag activates event and batch truncation, and the audit-log-truncate-max-event-size parameter (default 10,240 bytes) limits the size of each individual audit event sent to the backend. The --audit-log-batch-max-size flag bounds the number of events per batch. For environments where audit completeness is a compliance requirement, administrators should size the --audit-log-batch-buffer-size appropriately, as events are dropped rather than queued indefinitely if the buffer overflows. The audit policy configured for the API server directly affects overall storage consumption: selecting appropriate verbosity levels (None, Metadata, Request, or RequestResponse) for different resource types controls the rate at which audit records accumulate, and restricting verbose logging to high-value operations such as secret access or privileged resource modifications reduces the overall storage footprint. The Kubernetes apiserver_audit_event_total Prometheus metric tracks the total number of audit events exported, providing a mechanism to monitor audit throughput and detect developing capacity pressure before events begin to drop. A companion metric, apiserver_audit_level_total, provides a per-request counter partitioned by the audit level assigned by the active policy, giving administrators visibility into audit volume by verbosity tier to inform storage sizing and policy tuning decisions.

Namespace-level storage governance for VKS workloads is available through LimitRange and ResourceQuota objects. LimitRange allows cluster administrators to set per-PersistentVolumeClaim storage request ceilings, while ResourceQuota caps total storage consumption per namespace, enabling cluster-wide storage budget planning and preventing any single project from exhausting its allotment. For persistent log-related storage in standard packages, the Harbor container registry job log PVC defaults to 1 GB (configured via persistence.persistentVolumeClaim.jobLog.size) and can be expanded to 5 GB or higher after verifying available disk space; the Harbor main registry storage PVC defaults to 10 GB and can be increased to 20 GB or higher. The Alertmanager standard package PVC defaults to 2 GiB.

For centralized log management, VCF Log Management is the primary log aggregation platform. It collects syslog feeds from ESX hosts and pulls tasks, events, and alerts from vCenter instances. The platform supports a High Availability deployment model with multiple instances behind an internal load balancer, and it provides both scale-up and scale-out capabilities to accommodate growing log volumes. The deployment model supports a configurable retention notification threshold and the ability to add disk nodes, giving administrators direct control over when they are alerted about storage consumption and the ability to expand capacity without service interruption. Administrators can configure log retention policies by log type and source, controlling both log file size and the number of retained log files.

VCF Operations provides configurable data retention settings that allow administrators to tune how long historical data is kept, directly managing the storage space consumed by the system. Support log properties can be edited to limit retained log size and set logging levels for individual log file folders, providing granular control over which logs consume the most storage. The orchestrator scripting log size can also be defined in megabytes through a dedicated configuration parameter. VCF Operations retains recent task data for actions for 14 days, after which the data is purged from the system.

For proactive capacity monitoring, VCF Operations collects disk log metrics including used, total, and available space for node logs. The platform publishes a Capacity Analytics metric for disk space usage that provides the remaining capacity in GB, calculated as the maximum point between usable capacity and projected utilization over the next three days. This forward-looking projection allows administrators to identify and address storage shortfalls before they affect log collection. Capacity metrics can also be calculated for datastore objects, providing visibility into the underlying storage that hosts log data. VCF Operations generates disk capacity alerts when utilization reaches 80% (Critical) or 90% (Immediate) severity levels, providing automated notification when log storage is approaching exhaustion. VCF Operations also includes a Kubernetes Capacity dashboard that displays Kubernetes workload resource usage against configured requests and limits, providing unified visibility into K8s storage consumption for capacity planning.

Syslog forwarding to external targets provides an additional safeguard for log storage capacity. ESX hosts can be configured to forward syslog messages to a remote log server, offloading log storage from local host volumes to dedicated infrastructure with managed capacity. vCenter supports forwarding to up to three remote syslog servers using TCP, UDP, TLS, or RELP protocols. For VKS clusters, the Fluent Bit standard package can forward container and system logs to Syslog, HTTP, Elasticsearch, Kafka, or Splunk targets, offloading log storage from cluster node volumes to dedicated log infrastructure. When Fluent Bit is configured to collect kube-apiserver audit logs from VKS cluster nodes, the audit log stream operates with a 50 MB in-memory buffer limit, bounding the local storage footprint for audit events that are pending forwarding. On the vSphere Supervisor control plane, kube-apiserver audit logs are collected by Fluent Bit from /var/log/vmware/audit/kube-apiserver.log and stored locally at /var/log/vmware/fluentbit/flb_audit_apiserver before any external forwarding is applied; directing these logs to an external target reduces the storage load on the Supervisor control plane nodes. Workloads running in vSphere Namespaces can stream their logs to a remote Syslog server by applying a LogAgentConfiguration Custom Resource to the namespace; log messages are enriched with namespace name, pod UID, pod name, container name, and ESX host name for traceability. By forwarding logs to external SIEM or log management systems, organizations maintain a secondary copy of log data that is independent of local storage constraints, so that even if local storage is exhausted, the forwarded copies remain intact. Administrators should note that ESX hosts deployed with Auto Deploy store logs on a RAM disk, which provides limited local log capacity; for those deployments, configuring remote syslog forwarding is particularly important.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) includes several mechanisms for managing the storage capacity used by its observability and logging components. The most direct is the PAISConfiguration custom resource (CR), which governs PAIS observability. Within the CR, the metrics-retention property sets the data retention period for Prometheus metrics, and the Prometheus metrics database is backed by a Kubernetes Persistent Volume whose size is inferred from this retention setting. Operators configure this alongside a storage class via observability.prometheusRuntime when activating PAIS observability, giving them direct control over how much storage is allocated to the metrics backend and how long data is retained before being pruned. The metrics retention period has a default of 14 days, which establishes a predictable upper bound on the metrics volume an operator must size storage for.

For LLM request tracing, PAIS supports forwarding LLM traces to an OpenTelemetry Collector by uncommenting the spec.observability.llmTraces section of the PAISConfiguration CR. This externalizes trace storage to whichever observability platform the organization operates, so trace volume does not accumulate within the PAIS cluster itself. PAIS Controller metrics are also exposed at a Prometheus endpoint on the pais-controller-manager service for collection by external monitoring systems, which keeps long-term metric history out of the PAIS namespace.

Deep Learning VMs write logs to well-known paths: JupyterLab authentication events and cloud-init provisioning output go to /var/log/dl.log, and vGPU and NVIDIA data center GPU driver installation events go to /var/log/gpu-install.log. The Triton Inference Server on Deep Learning VMs generates inference request logs and performance metrics that can be collected for audit and compliance review. The vLLM inference server on model endpoints supports a VLLM_LOGGING_LEVEL environment variable that controls logging verbosity, allowing operators to reduce log volume by selecting an appropriate level for production workloads. Model endpoints also have a configurable tempoMountSize on the /tmp mount point that defaults to 1Gi, which bounds ephemeral working storage on each endpoint.

Capacity for the supporting Kubernetes storage on VKS worker nodes is sized through PAIS-provided defaults. VKS worker node pools provisioned for PAIS use kubelet-dir and containerd-dir storage volumes with a default capacity of 100Gi each, mounted to vSAN storage policies. Data Indexing and Retrieval worker nodes use a storage policy configured through the spec.worker.storageClassName section of the PAISConfiguration YAML. For environments that host a private Harbor registry for AI container images, PAIS guidance recommends allocating registry storage space to accommodate at least three versions of each container image planned for deployment, so that registry growth is anticipated rather than reactive.

The foundational storage layer, including vSAN storage policies, namespace storage limits, and platform-level capacity monitoring, is managed through VCF rather than PAIS itself.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend (vDefend) provides several log storage capacity management mechanisms for its security event data.

At the Distributed Firewall (DFW) layer, packet logging uses bounded log rotation: the DFWPKTLOGS.log file rotates at a 50 MB per-file limit, and the system retains a maximum of 10 log files at a time. This bounds DFW packet log storage on transport nodes while retaining sufficient history for troubleshooting and forensic analysis.

vDefend IDS/IPS includes an automated protective mechanism that handles high-volume event scenarios. When incoming IDS/IPS events and PCAP data would otherwise exhaust memory, the mechanism selectively drops non-critical events and PCAPs to maintain system stability. Administrators can observe event volumes through the IDS/IPS Security Dashboard and Security Overview Dashboard.

IDS/IPS event data can be forwarded to external syslog consumers by enabling the ids_events_to_syslog setting under IDS/IPS Settings. Exporting events externally offloads local storage consumption so that log accumulation on transport nodes is bounded by the rotation policy rather than event volume. Remote syslog must be configured on the ESX host to receive IDS/IPS events exported directly from ESX hosts. vDefend components write logs to /var/log, with syslog messages conforming to RFC 5424 on vDefend appliances and RFC 3164 on ESX hosts.

Guest Introspection Host Agent (MUX) logs are written to /var/log/syslog.log on ESX hosts, with each log message including a timestamp, ContextMUX identifier, and thread ID.

The Security Services Platform (SSP), which hosts Security Intelligence and Network Detection and Response analytics, has documented storage sizing baselines for capacity planning. Each SSP controller node requires 225 GB of storage and each worker node also requires 225 GB. Storage requirements scale with worker node count, adding approximately 155 GB per additional worker node. For a maximum configuration of 10 worker nodes (14 total nodes), the recommended persistent volume claim is 3,545 GB. These sizing baselines enable administrators to allocate storage capacity appropriately at deployment time.

vDefend logs can be forwarded to VCF Log Management by configuring it as a syslog server. VCF Log Management provides a centralized log management framework and supports querying log data by time range through Interactive Analytics.

These mechanisms address event log storage capacity for vDefend's security event data. Broader log storage governance across the VCF platform, including infrastructure-wide capacity monitoring and centralized retention policies, is handled by VCF Log Management and VCF Operations.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides configurable log storage capacity management through file size limits, file count limits, and per-component logging level controls that help prevent log storage exhaustion.

PNR Server log files are managed through the vmware-dr.xml configuration file. The <maxFileSize> parameter controls the maximum size of individual log files, with a default of 10,485,760 bytes (10 MB). The <maxFileNum> parameter controls the maximum number of log files retained, with a default of 20 files. PNR also raises a low disk space event when available disk space on the server falls below a configurable threshold, controlled by the localSiteStatus.minDiskSpace parameter with a default of 100 MB. This event can trigger alarms that alert administrators before log storage is exhausted.

Configurable logging levels per component allow administrators to reduce log verbosity when log files are growing too quickly, or increase it temporarily for troubleshooting. Named per-component settings configurable through the Reconfigure Protection and Recovery Settings interface include logManager.Storage, logManager.StorageProvider, logManager.Licensing, and logManager.VvolProvider, each with a default value of verbose. This allows log output from storage, storage provider, licensing, and Virtual Volumes subsystems to be tuned independently.

The PNR Appliance supports forwarding log files to a remote syslog server, allowing log volume to be offloaded to centralized log management infrastructure. In environments using the Live Cyber Recovery (LCR) clean room, PNR can forward events from the clean room to VCF Log Management (version 9.0 or above), Microsoft Sentinel, or Splunk Cloud.

vSphere Replication, which is part of PNR, supports event logging and allows definition of alarms for each event. vSphere Replication maintains dedicated audit logs at /opt/vmware/support/logs/hms/hms-audit.log, with broker logs at /opt/vmware/support/logs/hbr on the Appliance and at /var/run/log on ESX hosts.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides configurable log storage settings and two-tier capacity alerting that support proactive management of event log storage.

DSM Provider logging includes configurable log storage settings accessible through the Provider interface under Storage Settings. Administrators configure the Provider Log Repo URL to direct logs to an S3-compatible object storage repository and bucket; the Bucket Name and optional Region settings complete the S3 object store configuration. Gateway audit log files are stored locally on the Provider VM at `/var/log/tdm/provider/audit/` using filenames in the format `gateway-audit-timestamp.log`, and are archived periodically to an S3 subpath named `gateway audit` when a provider log repository is configured. The Provider VM ships with a default storage configuration of three disks totaling 595 GB (80 GB, 15 GB, and 500 GB), which can be sized for the anticipated log and backup volume at deployment time.

DSM includes two-tier capacity alerting to notify administrators before storage is exhausted. The `data-disk-warning-threshold` setting governs a WARNING global alert when data disk usage reaches 70%; the `data-disk-critical-threshold` setting governs a CRITICAL global alert at 90%. When either threshold is breached, administrators can increase the storage allocation using the `storage-amount` setting. These alerts are visible to DSM administrators and prompt timely remediation before log capacity is exceeded.

For SQL Server databases, DSM exposes the `errorLog.numErrorLogs` custom engine setting, which controls how many error log files are retained before recycling. Configuring this setting allows administrators to manage database-engine-level log storage consumption directly.

S3-compatible storage sizing for DSM depends on the volume of data backups and the configured retention policy. Organizations must assess their log generation rates, retention requirements, and backup volumes to right-size the S3 repository. DSM does not automatically provision or expand storage capacity, so organizational processes for periodic capacity review and storage expansion remain necessary to fully satisfy this control.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides configurable log storage capacity at both the Controller and Service Engine layers, giving organizations the mechanisms to allocate and proactively manage event log storage for application delivery traffic.

At the Service Engine layer, disk capacity for logs is calculated dynamically based on total disk size, CPU cores, RAM, and the configurable maximum-storage-not-allocated-for-logs setting, adjustable through SE runtime properties. These values drive two derived pools: client-logs-capacity (total disk minus debug logs capacity, with adjustments based on configured minimum storage allocated for logs and SE RAM) and debug-logs-capacity (calculated as the SE total disk minus the maximum storage not allocated for logs, divided by 100). A minimally sized Service Engine with two vCPUs, 2 GB of RAM, and 10 GB of storage can hold approximately 8 million log entries. When storage is exhausted, the oldest Significant Log entries are phased out on a per-Service Engine basis and their disk space freed automatically, keeping the Service Engine operational without requiring operator intervention.

Increasing disk capacity on a Service Engine directly extends both the volume of logs retained and the total retention duration. Avi documentation recommends provisioning Controllers and Service Engines with larger storage allocations in environments that require extended log retention. Adding CPU, memory, and disk resources to Service Engines and Controllers scales log throughput and retention proportionally.

For deployments using the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) within VCF, the AKO persistentVolumeClaimSpec.storage parameter allocates 2 GiB of storage for the log Persistent Volume Claim by default, providing a configurable log storage allocation for Kubernetes-based workloads.

For log retention beyond what local disk supports, the Avi Controller's Syslog-Audit-Persistence feature streams alerts and audit events to external syslog servers. The Controller also supports sending audit records for trusted channel initiation, termination, and failures to Events, Syslog, and Splunk destinations. Offloading to an external syslog or Splunk infrastructure decouples event log retention from local Service Engine disk allocation and supports longer retention windows managed at the logging tier. Note that Syslog notification actions push alert events to syslog servers but do not directly export virtual service logs; virtual service logs must be pulled from an external logging system through the API or scripted to be pushed from the Controller.

### Control CR 2.9(b)

> Sub-item (b) of CR 2.9 in IEC 62443-4-2. The licensed standard text is not reproduced at this granularity; refer to the CR 2.9 requirement in the standard.

**SCF Controls:** MON-04

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides multiple layers of log storage capacity management across its component products, covering local log retention controls, centralized log aggregation with scalable architecture, proactive capacity monitoring with forward-looking projections, and Kubernetes-native container log bounding for workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS).

At the VMware ESX host level, administrators can configure log file retention and rotation policies. The log.keepOld parameter controls how many log files are retained, and when that limit is reached, the oldest file is automatically deleted. VMware recommends a log file size of 500 KB per file for host logs. The syslog directory location is configurable through the Syslog.global.logDir parameter, allowing administrators to direct log output to a storage volume with sufficient capacity. These same retention controls apply to virtual machine log files, where the number of retained log files can be limited through the log.keepOld setting. Per-folder log size limits prevent any single log source from consuming disproportionate storage, and logging levels can be adjusted per log folder to balance detail against storage consumption. ESX also exposes dedicated audit record storage parameters: the Syslog.global.auditRecord.storageCapacity parameter defines the size of the local audit record storage directory in MiB, and this capacity can be set or adjusted at any time using the esxcli system auditrecords local set --size command. VMware vCenter provides equivalent log rotation controls, with the ability to limit the number of retained log files through the same log.keepOld parameter.

For VKS clusters, the kubelet exposes two key parameters for container log management that can be configured through ClusterClass variables during cluster provisioning. The containerLogMaxSizeMiB setting defines the maximum size of a container log file before rotation, with a default of 10 MiB. The containerLogMaxFiles setting controls how many rotated log files are retained per container, with a valid range of 1 to 100 and a default of 5; the oldest files are removed automatically when the limit is exceeded during rotation. Together these parameters establish a predictable ceiling on per-node storage consumption from container logs. The Kubernetes API server's audit log backend provides complementary controls: the audit-log-truncate-enabled flag activates event and batch truncation, and the audit-log-truncate-max-event-size parameter (default 10,240 bytes) limits the size of each individual audit event sent to the backend. The --audit-log-batch-max-size flag bounds the number of events per batch. For environments where audit completeness is a compliance requirement, administrators should size the --audit-log-batch-buffer-size appropriately, as events are dropped rather than queued indefinitely if the buffer overflows. The audit policy configured for the API server directly affects overall storage consumption: selecting appropriate verbosity levels (None, Metadata, Request, or RequestResponse) for different resource types controls the rate at which audit records accumulate, and restricting verbose logging to high-value operations such as secret access or privileged resource modifications reduces the overall storage footprint. The Kubernetes apiserver_audit_event_total Prometheus metric tracks the total number of audit events exported, providing a mechanism to monitor audit throughput and detect developing capacity pressure before events begin to drop. A companion metric, apiserver_audit_level_total, provides a per-request counter partitioned by the audit level assigned by the active policy, giving administrators visibility into audit volume by verbosity tier to inform storage sizing and policy tuning decisions.

Namespace-level storage governance for VKS workloads is available through LimitRange and ResourceQuota objects. LimitRange allows cluster administrators to set per-PersistentVolumeClaim storage request ceilings, while ResourceQuota caps total storage consumption per namespace, enabling cluster-wide storage budget planning and preventing any single project from exhausting its allotment. For persistent log-related storage in standard packages, the Harbor container registry job log PVC defaults to 1 GB (configured via persistence.persistentVolumeClaim.jobLog.size) and can be expanded to 5 GB or higher after verifying available disk space; the Harbor main registry storage PVC defaults to 10 GB and can be increased to 20 GB or higher. The Alertmanager standard package PVC defaults to 2 GiB.

For centralized log management, VCF Log Management is the primary log aggregation platform. It collects syslog feeds from ESX hosts and pulls tasks, events, and alerts from vCenter instances. The platform supports a High Availability deployment model with multiple instances behind an internal load balancer, and it provides both scale-up and scale-out capabilities to accommodate growing log volumes. The deployment model supports a configurable retention notification threshold and the ability to add disk nodes, giving administrators direct control over when they are alerted about storage consumption and the ability to expand capacity without service interruption. Administrators can configure log retention policies by log type and source, controlling both log file size and the number of retained log files.

VCF Operations provides configurable data retention settings that allow administrators to tune how long historical data is kept, directly managing the storage space consumed by the system. Support log properties can be edited to limit retained log size and set logging levels for individual log file folders, providing granular control over which logs consume the most storage. The orchestrator scripting log size can also be defined in megabytes through a dedicated configuration parameter. VCF Operations retains recent task data for actions for 14 days, after which the data is purged from the system.

For proactive capacity monitoring, VCF Operations collects disk log metrics including used, total, and available space for node logs. The platform publishes a Capacity Analytics metric for disk space usage that provides the remaining capacity in GB, calculated as the maximum point between usable capacity and projected utilization over the next three days. This forward-looking projection allows administrators to identify and address storage shortfalls before they affect log collection. Capacity metrics can also be calculated for datastore objects, providing visibility into the underlying storage that hosts log data. VCF Operations generates disk capacity alerts when utilization reaches 80% (Critical) or 90% (Immediate) severity levels, providing automated notification when log storage is approaching exhaustion. VCF Operations also includes a Kubernetes Capacity dashboard that displays Kubernetes workload resource usage against configured requests and limits, providing unified visibility into K8s storage consumption for capacity planning.

Syslog forwarding to external targets provides an additional safeguard for log storage capacity. ESX hosts can be configured to forward syslog messages to a remote log server, offloading log storage from local host volumes to dedicated infrastructure with managed capacity. vCenter supports forwarding to up to three remote syslog servers using TCP, UDP, TLS, or RELP protocols. For VKS clusters, the Fluent Bit standard package can forward container and system logs to Syslog, HTTP, Elasticsearch, Kafka, or Splunk targets, offloading log storage from cluster node volumes to dedicated log infrastructure. When Fluent Bit is configured to collect kube-apiserver audit logs from VKS cluster nodes, the audit log stream operates with a 50 MB in-memory buffer limit, bounding the local storage footprint for audit events that are pending forwarding. On the vSphere Supervisor control plane, kube-apiserver audit logs are collected by Fluent Bit from /var/log/vmware/audit/kube-apiserver.log and stored locally at /var/log/vmware/fluentbit/flb_audit_apiserver before any external forwarding is applied; directing these logs to an external target reduces the storage load on the Supervisor control plane nodes. Workloads running in vSphere Namespaces can stream their logs to a remote Syslog server by applying a LogAgentConfiguration Custom Resource to the namespace; log messages are enriched with namespace name, pod UID, pod name, container name, and ESX host name for traceability. By forwarding logs to external SIEM or log management systems, organizations maintain a secondary copy of log data that is independent of local storage constraints, so that even if local storage is exhausted, the forwarded copies remain intact. Administrators should note that ESX hosts deployed with Auto Deploy store logs on a RAM disk, which provides limited local log capacity; for those deployments, configuring remote syslog forwarding is particularly important.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) includes several mechanisms for managing the storage capacity used by its observability and logging components. The most direct is the PAISConfiguration custom resource (CR), which governs PAIS observability. Within the CR, the metrics-retention property sets the data retention period for Prometheus metrics, and the Prometheus metrics database is backed by a Kubernetes Persistent Volume whose size is inferred from this retention setting. Operators configure this alongside a storage class via observability.prometheusRuntime when activating PAIS observability, giving them direct control over how much storage is allocated to the metrics backend and how long data is retained before being pruned. The metrics retention period has a default of 14 days, which establishes a predictable upper bound on the metrics volume an operator must size storage for.

For LLM request tracing, PAIS supports forwarding LLM traces to an OpenTelemetry Collector by uncommenting the spec.observability.llmTraces section of the PAISConfiguration CR. This externalizes trace storage to whichever observability platform the organization operates, so trace volume does not accumulate within the PAIS cluster itself. PAIS Controller metrics are also exposed at a Prometheus endpoint on the pais-controller-manager service for collection by external monitoring systems, which keeps long-term metric history out of the PAIS namespace.

Deep Learning VMs write logs to well-known paths: JupyterLab authentication events and cloud-init provisioning output go to /var/log/dl.log, and vGPU and NVIDIA data center GPU driver installation events go to /var/log/gpu-install.log. The Triton Inference Server on Deep Learning VMs generates inference request logs and performance metrics that can be collected for audit and compliance review. The vLLM inference server on model endpoints supports a VLLM_LOGGING_LEVEL environment variable that controls logging verbosity, allowing operators to reduce log volume by selecting an appropriate level for production workloads. Model endpoints also have a configurable tempoMountSize on the /tmp mount point that defaults to 1Gi, which bounds ephemeral working storage on each endpoint.

Capacity for the supporting Kubernetes storage on VKS worker nodes is sized through PAIS-provided defaults. VKS worker node pools provisioned for PAIS use kubelet-dir and containerd-dir storage volumes with a default capacity of 100Gi each, mounted to vSAN storage policies. Data Indexing and Retrieval worker nodes use a storage policy configured through the spec.worker.storageClassName section of the PAISConfiguration YAML. For environments that host a private Harbor registry for AI container images, PAIS guidance recommends allocating registry storage space to accommodate at least three versions of each container image planned for deployment, so that registry growth is anticipated rather than reactive.

The foundational storage layer, including vSAN storage policies, namespace storage limits, and platform-level capacity monitoring, is managed through VCF rather than PAIS itself.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend (vDefend) provides several log storage capacity management mechanisms for its security event data.

At the Distributed Firewall (DFW) layer, packet logging uses bounded log rotation: the DFWPKTLOGS.log file rotates at a 50 MB per-file limit, and the system retains a maximum of 10 log files at a time. This bounds DFW packet log storage on transport nodes while retaining sufficient history for troubleshooting and forensic analysis.

vDefend IDS/IPS includes an automated protective mechanism that handles high-volume event scenarios. When incoming IDS/IPS events and PCAP data would otherwise exhaust memory, the mechanism selectively drops non-critical events and PCAPs to maintain system stability. Administrators can observe event volumes through the IDS/IPS Security Dashboard and Security Overview Dashboard.

IDS/IPS event data can be forwarded to external syslog consumers by enabling the ids_events_to_syslog setting under IDS/IPS Settings. Exporting events externally offloads local storage consumption so that log accumulation on transport nodes is bounded by the rotation policy rather than event volume. Remote syslog must be configured on the ESX host to receive IDS/IPS events exported directly from ESX hosts. vDefend components write logs to /var/log, with syslog messages conforming to RFC 5424 on vDefend appliances and RFC 3164 on ESX hosts.

Guest Introspection Host Agent (MUX) logs are written to /var/log/syslog.log on ESX hosts, with each log message including a timestamp, ContextMUX identifier, and thread ID.

The Security Services Platform (SSP), which hosts Security Intelligence and Network Detection and Response analytics, has documented storage sizing baselines for capacity planning. Each SSP controller node requires 225 GB of storage and each worker node also requires 225 GB. Storage requirements scale with worker node count, adding approximately 155 GB per additional worker node. For a maximum configuration of 10 worker nodes (14 total nodes), the recommended persistent volume claim is 3,545 GB. These sizing baselines enable administrators to allocate storage capacity appropriately at deployment time.

vDefend logs can be forwarded to VCF Log Management by configuring it as a syslog server. VCF Log Management provides a centralized log management framework and supports querying log data by time range through Interactive Analytics.

These mechanisms address event log storage capacity for vDefend's security event data. Broader log storage governance across the VCF platform, including infrastructure-wide capacity monitoring and centralized retention policies, is handled by VCF Log Management and VCF Operations.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides configurable log storage capacity management through file size limits, file count limits, and per-component logging level controls that help prevent log storage exhaustion.

PNR Server log files are managed through the vmware-dr.xml configuration file. The <maxFileSize> parameter controls the maximum size of individual log files, with a default of 10,485,760 bytes (10 MB). The <maxFileNum> parameter controls the maximum number of log files retained, with a default of 20 files. PNR also raises a low disk space event when available disk space on the server falls below a configurable threshold, controlled by the localSiteStatus.minDiskSpace parameter with a default of 100 MB. This event can trigger alarms that alert administrators before log storage is exhausted.

Configurable logging levels per component allow administrators to reduce log verbosity when log files are growing too quickly, or increase it temporarily for troubleshooting. Named per-component settings configurable through the Reconfigure Protection and Recovery Settings interface include logManager.Storage, logManager.StorageProvider, logManager.Licensing, and logManager.VvolProvider, each with a default value of verbose. This allows log output from storage, storage provider, licensing, and Virtual Volumes subsystems to be tuned independently.

The PNR Appliance supports forwarding log files to a remote syslog server, allowing log volume to be offloaded to centralized log management infrastructure. In environments using the Live Cyber Recovery (LCR) clean room, PNR can forward events from the clean room to VCF Log Management (version 9.0 or above), Microsoft Sentinel, or Splunk Cloud.

vSphere Replication, which is part of PNR, supports event logging and allows definition of alarms for each event. vSphere Replication maintains dedicated audit logs at /opt/vmware/support/logs/hms/hms-audit.log, with broker logs at /opt/vmware/support/logs/hbr on the Appliance and at /var/run/log on ESX hosts.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides configurable log storage settings and two-tier capacity alerting that support proactive management of event log storage.

DSM Provider logging includes configurable log storage settings accessible through the Provider interface under Storage Settings. Administrators configure the Provider Log Repo URL to direct logs to an S3-compatible object storage repository and bucket; the Bucket Name and optional Region settings complete the S3 object store configuration. Gateway audit log files are stored locally on the Provider VM at `/var/log/tdm/provider/audit/` using filenames in the format `gateway-audit-timestamp.log`, and are archived periodically to an S3 subpath named `gateway audit` when a provider log repository is configured. The Provider VM ships with a default storage configuration of three disks totaling 595 GB (80 GB, 15 GB, and 500 GB), which can be sized for the anticipated log and backup volume at deployment time.

DSM includes two-tier capacity alerting to notify administrators before storage is exhausted. The `data-disk-warning-threshold` setting governs a WARNING global alert when data disk usage reaches 70%; the `data-disk-critical-threshold` setting governs a CRITICAL global alert at 90%. When either threshold is breached, administrators can increase the storage allocation using the `storage-amount` setting. These alerts are visible to DSM administrators and prompt timely remediation before log capacity is exceeded.

For SQL Server databases, DSM exposes the `errorLog.numErrorLogs` custom engine setting, which controls how many error log files are retained before recycling. Configuring this setting allows administrators to manage database-engine-level log storage consumption directly.

S3-compatible storage sizing for DSM depends on the volume of data backups and the configured retention policy. Organizations must assess their log generation rates, retention requirements, and backup volumes to right-size the S3 repository. DSM does not automatically provision or expand storage capacity, so organizational processes for periodic capacity review and storage expansion remain necessary to fully satisfy this control.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides configurable log storage capacity at both the Controller and Service Engine layers, giving organizations the mechanisms to allocate and proactively manage event log storage for application delivery traffic.

At the Service Engine layer, disk capacity for logs is calculated dynamically based on total disk size, CPU cores, RAM, and the configurable maximum-storage-not-allocated-for-logs setting, adjustable through SE runtime properties. These values drive two derived pools: client-logs-capacity (total disk minus debug logs capacity, with adjustments based on configured minimum storage allocated for logs and SE RAM) and debug-logs-capacity (calculated as the SE total disk minus the maximum storage not allocated for logs, divided by 100). A minimally sized Service Engine with two vCPUs, 2 GB of RAM, and 10 GB of storage can hold approximately 8 million log entries. When storage is exhausted, the oldest Significant Log entries are phased out on a per-Service Engine basis and their disk space freed automatically, keeping the Service Engine operational without requiring operator intervention.

Increasing disk capacity on a Service Engine directly extends both the volume of logs retained and the total retention duration. Avi documentation recommends provisioning Controllers and Service Engines with larger storage allocations in environments that require extended log retention. Adding CPU, memory, and disk resources to Service Engines and Controllers scales log throughput and retention proportionally.

For deployments using the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) within VCF, the AKO persistentVolumeClaimSpec.storage parameter allocates 2 GiB of storage for the log Persistent Volume Claim by default, providing a configurable log storage allocation for Kubernetes-based workloads.

For log retention beyond what local disk supports, the Avi Controller's Syslog-Audit-Persistence feature streams alerts and audit events to external syslog servers. The Controller also supports sending audit records for trusted channel initiation, termination, and failures to Events, Syslog, and Splunk destinations. Offloading to an external syslog or Splunk infrastructure decouples event log retention from local Service Engine disk allocation and supports longer retention windows managed at the logging tier. Note that Syslog notification actions push alert events to syslog servers but do not directly export virtual service logs; virtual service logs must be pulled from an external logging system through the API or scripted to be pushed from the Controller.

### Control CR 2.10(a)

> Sub-item (a) of CR 2.10 in IEC 62443-4-2. The licensed standard text is not reproduced at this granularity; refer to the CR 2.10 requirement in the standard.

**SCF Controls:** BCD-01

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides a broad set of infrastructure-level capabilities that support business continuity and disaster recovery planning. While VCF does not itself constitute a Business Continuity Management System, it supplies the technical mechanisms that underpin resilient deployments and contingency operations.

vSAN stretched clusters allow organizations to extend a single vSAN datastore across two geographically separated sites with an independent witness host. When configured with site mirroring, a stretched cluster can tolerate the complete loss of one site while maintaining data availability. vSphere HA, when enabled on a stretched cluster, automatically restarts virtual machines on the surviving site during a site failure. To support this, vSphere HA admission control should be configured to reserve 50% of the cluster's aggregate CPU and memory resources for failover, and site-specific isolation response addresses should be defined so that vSphere HA can validate host isolation even when network connectivity between sites is lost. The admission control setting for host failures the cluster tolerates should also be updated after cluster expansion to reflect the correct host count per availability zone. When stretching a workload domain cluster, the default management vSphere cluster must already be stretched. vSphere HA uses network heartbeats to validate the state of VMware ESX hosts, and can also use heartbeat datastores mounted to more than one host (excluding vSAN datastores) for cluster membership monitoring.

vSphere HA also operates at the individual cluster level independent of stretched clusters. The HA configuration remediation mode can be set to quarantine mode, maintenance mode, or mixed mode to handle host failures in a controlled manner, helping maintain workload availability during hardware events. vSphere Fault Tolerance provides a higher tier of availability for individual virtual machines by maintaining a secondary VM in continuous lockstep with the primary, enabling instantaneous failover with no data loss if the primary host fails.

vSAN Data Protection provides snapshot-based protection through the vSAN Snapshot Service. Protection groups support configurable snapshot scheduling with adjustable intervals and retention policies that define retention duration and time units. This allows organizations to define data protection policies aligned with their recovery point objectives. The Snapshot Service API also supports operations such as restoring deleted virtual machines from snapshots and creating linked clones from snapshots.

vSphere Replication provides asynchronous replication of virtual machines with a configurable Recovery Point Objective (RPO), allowing organizations to replicate workloads to a secondary site and define acceptable data loss windows as part of their contingency planning.

VCF requires all management components, including VCF Operations, VMware vCenter, NSX, and VCF Automation, to be configured with a regular backup schedule and retention policy. VCF Operations configures NSX Manager backups during the initial deployment, with a recommended backup frequency of hourly and 7-day retention. Backup jobs for VCF Operations and all vCenter instances should start within the same 5-minute window to capture a consistent state. The VMware Infrastructure Health adapter within VCF Operations monitors backup job retention for management components, including NSX Manager. Changed Block Tracking (CBT) tracks modified disk sectors on virtual machines to support incremental backups, reducing backup windows and storage requirements for VM-level backup operations. vCenter itself also supports a High Availability configuration with Active and Passive nodes whose configuration files are continuously replicated, providing management-plane resilience independent of the backup schedule.

VMware HCX provides workload mobility capabilities for migrating and replicating VMs between sites. HCX supports multiple migration types through its Mobility Agent service, including vMotion, Cold Migration, Bulk Migration, and Replication Assisted vMotion (RAV), each addressing different scenarios from large-scale parallel migrations to zero-downtime live migrations. HCX also includes appliance-level backup and restore capabilities for the HCX Manager, including certificate backup and restore, supporting planned migrations and recovery scenarios as part of broader BC/DR operations.

VCF Operations monitors infrastructure health and surfaces alerts when conditions threaten availability, giving operations teams early warning of potential failures. The Platform Health Alerts page provides a centralized view of alerts across data sources and infrastructure nodes. VCF Automation provides alerts through the associated VCF Operations application for capacity, performance, and availability monitoring. VCF Operations also includes infrastructure planning and what-if analysis capabilities that help organizations forecast capacity requirements and plan for contingency scenarios. Committed scenarios reserve capacity by setting aside resources for new, upcoming, or planned workloads, helping stakeholders understand capacity requirements across the environment. Integration with IT Service Management and Configuration Management Database platforms is supported through VCF Automation, enabling organizations to tie their infrastructure management into broader service continuity workflows.

Lifecycle management tools allow administrators to plan updates and schedule maintenance windows to make changes with minimal disruption to services, supporting the operational continuity aspect of business continuity planning.

While VCF provides these technical capabilities, a complete BCMS requires organizational elements that fall outside the platform: documented continuity of operations plans, recovery time and recovery point objectives defined by business stakeholders, regular testing and exercise of recovery procedures, communication plans, and management oversight of the continuity program. VCF supplies the infrastructure resilience and recovery mechanisms that such plans depend on, but the planning, governance, and procedural components must be established and maintained by the organization.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to business continuity by providing security-layer continuity mechanisms that remain active during disaster recovery events and workload migrations. NSX Federation Security maintains security posture across data centers during disaster recovery and workload migration, preserving distributed firewall and gateway firewall policies so that enforcement continues through failover.

The Security Services Platform, which provides the underlying infrastructure for vDefend's threat detection and inspection services, supports vSphere High Availability configurations. When a VMware ESX host becomes unhealthy, vSphere HA migrates the affected Security Services Platform virtual machines to healthy hosts in the cluster, restoring threat detection capacity without manual intervention. VMware recommends enabling vSphere HA for host-level failure recovery and configuring a storage policy with RAID for datastore-level failure tolerance in Security Services Platform deployments.

Security Services Platform can also be deployed on a vSAN stretched cluster configuration, which replicates storage across two physically distinct sites connected by a high-bandwidth or low-latency inter-site link. This configuration requires a minimum of two sites with their own hosts and a witness host to handle potential conflicts arising from network partitions, supporting site-level failure scenarios.

When vSphere HA restarts workload VMs on a new recovery host after a failover, the vDefend distributed firewall automatically reinspects all traffic from those VMs on the receiving host, because the Flow Table contents cannot be moved between hosts during host recovery. Security policy enforcement resumes on the new host without manual reconfiguration.

The broader BCMS framework, including Continuity of Operations Plan and BC/DR playbooks, impact analyses, and organizational governance processes, is outside vDefend's scope and must be established through dedicated business continuity management programs.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides the technical execution layer for contingency planning in virtualized infrastructure. Recovery plans define the sequence, priority, and configuration for failover operations, acting as executable BC/DR playbooks. Protection groups organize virtual machines into logical units aligned with application tiers or business functions, establishing the scope of each contingency plan.

Recovery plans support non-disruptive testing before use in planned migration or disaster recovery, allowing organizations to validate their contingency procedures without affecting production workloads. Recovery plan configuration differs between emergency scenarios at the protected site and planned migration of services from one site to another, with PNR supporting both modes. A forced recovery function is available during disaster recovery plan execution for scenarios requiring immediate failover.

vSphere Replication provides host-based replication with configurable Recovery Point Objectives (RPO), allowing organizations to set per-workload data loss tolerances. vSphere Replication supports up to 200 point-in-time recovery instances per virtual machine using the Latest Instances option, enabling recovery to specific earlier states. This capability is relevant to contingency scenarios involving data corruption or ransomware events. When the source site is accessible at the time of recovery, vSphere Replication can synchronize recent changes from the source site to the target site before completing the operation, reducing data loss.

In 9.1, PNR extends to multi-tenant disaster recovery through integration with VCF Automation. The product implements role-based access control with Organization Administrator, Project Administrator, and Project Advanced User roles, each with defined authorization levels for disaster recovery operations. Role assignments must be configured on both the primary and secondary PNR sites to maintain consistent access control across the DR environment. A multi-region deployment topology places separate management domains in each region to contain disaster impact to one region, while enabling service recovery in an alternate management domain when VCF Automation and VCF Operations are affected. The VCF Operations Orchestrator Plug-In for PNR provides Configuration, Inventory Mappings, Protection Groups, and Storage predefined workflow categories for workflow-driven recovery operations.

Cyber recovery deployments support a standard VCF topology where the workload domain is isolated from the management domain, providing separation between management and user workloads in clean-room recovery scenarios.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides application-layer resilience mechanisms at both the control plane and the data plane that organizations can incorporate into business continuity plans for application delivery services.

At the control plane, the Avi Controller is deployed as a cluster with continuous configuration replication across all nodes, providing management-plane redundancy so that a single Controller node failure does not interrupt application delivery operations. Avi supports deployment of the Controller cluster across two availability zones, with vSphere HA recommended to support recovery of any node that fails in the secondary zone. For single-node deployments, enabling vSphere HA on the Controller VM is recommended for node recovery following a host failure. The Avi Controller provides configuration backup and restore capability that supports disaster recovery and configuration migration across Controller instances; organizations should capture and retain Controller backups as part of their continuity documentation.

Availability Zone awareness in Avi allows multiple availability zones to be configured, each defining a subset of vCenter instances, hosts, and datastores that share a fault domain. Configuring availability zones during Service Engine Group setup distributes data-plane capacity across isolated failure domains, supporting continuity designs where application delivery capacity remains available if one fault domain becomes unavailable.

At the data plane, Avi's Elastic High Availability mode provides fast recovery for individual virtual services following Service Engine failure. In vSphere DRS-enabled clusters, virtual service recovery occurs before vSphere HA brings up a replacement Service Engine when virtual service slots are available in other Service Engines, giving the application delivery layer its own recovery path distinct from the infrastructure restart cycle. If vSphere HA does not recover the failed Service Engine within a configured timeout period, the Avi Controller resumes recovery independently. Service Engine VMs should be deployed on vSphere clusters configured with both HA and DRS in fully automated mode; when Avi is deployed with VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) on VCF, DRS and HA are required on every vSphere cluster used with Avi.

For Kubernetes environments, the Avi Multi-Cluster Kubernetes Operator (AMKO) provides federation architecture that supports disaster recovery across Kubernetes clusters. AMKO designates a leader cluster and multiple member clusters, coordinating application delivery failover as part of a multi-cluster DR strategy.

Avi ControlScripts, used in conjunction with the alert framework, can perform automated failover actions such as adding or removing routes based on VIP attachment and detachment events, allowing organizations to define automated remediation workflows that respond to system events without manual administrator intervention.

In 9.1 deployments using VCF Automation, Avi's Central Licensing maintains a global capacity pool that removes the requirement for duplicate licenses in DR scenarios, supporting license continuity across failover sites.

Organizations should document these mechanisms, including the Elastic HA configuration, Controller cluster topology and backup schedule, availability zone placement, and AMKO federation design, in their Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery playbooks. The technical recovery capabilities are built into the product; the planning artifacts, testing procedures, and escalation workflows are organizational responsibilities that extend beyond what Avi provides.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control CR 2.10(b)

> Sub-item (b) of CR 2.10 in IEC 62443-4-2. The licensed standard text is not reproduced at this granularity; refer to the CR 2.10 requirement in the standard.

**SCF Controls:** BCD-12.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides multiple layers of real-time and near-real-time failover capability across compute, storage, networking, and management components, directly addressing this control's requirements for maintaining availability of critical technology assets, applications, services, and data.

At the compute layer, vSphere High Availability (HA) monitors host health and automatically restarts virtual machines on surviving hosts when a failure is detected. Automatic failover shifts resources to healthy nodes in the cluster without manual intervention. For workloads requiring zero downtime, vSphere Fault Tolerance maintains a live shadow instance of a protected virtual machine, so that any hardware failure results in no data loss and no service interruption. Proactive HA integrates with hardware partner monitoring through vSAN Proactive Hardware Management (PHM), which detects degrading components via predictive failure events generated by the OEM vendor. When a predictive failure is identified, Proactive HA places the affected host into maintenance mode, evacuating VMs before a failure occurs. A supported Hardware Support Manager (HSM) must be configured and registered to VMware vCenter for this capability.

vSphere HA admission control can be configured to reserve a percentage of the cluster's aggregate CPU and memory resources specifically for failover capacity. Three admission control policy types are available: cluster resource percentage, slot policy, and dedicated failover hosts. The "Host failure cluster tolerates" admission control setting defines how many simultaneous host failures the cluster can absorb while still restarting all affected workloads. Remediation mode can be set to Quarantine mode, Maintenance mode, or Mixed mode to control how host failures are handled. For vSAN stretched clusters, admission control should reserve 50% of the cluster's aggregate CPU and memory resources so that all virtual machines from one site can be restarted on the other. vSphere HA can also use DRS to bring hosts out of standby mode and migrate virtual machines to defragment cluster resources, so that failover capacity remains available when aggregate resources are fragmented across the cluster.

vMotion enables live migration of running virtual machines to a new host without interruption in availability. VCF Operations HCX extends this mobility to remote sites for cross-site workload movement. HCX Network Extension High Availability provides failover protection for extended networks. Network Extension appliances operate in active-standby pairs through role negotiation, and during a failover event the standby pair takes over the Network Extension service from the active pair. After the failed appliance recovers, the pair renegotiates roles and returns to a healthy state, providing self-healing behavior.

For site-level failover, vSAN stretched clusters span two geographically separated sites with a witness host, enabling VCF to tolerate an entire site failure. When one site becomes unavailable, vSphere HA restarts affected virtual machines on the remaining active site. The storage policy "Site disaster tolerance" rule, when configured for site mirroring, allows the stretched cluster to tolerate a witness host failure even when one site is already unavailable. Site-specific isolation response addresses should be configured so that vSphere HA can validate host isolation even during a network failure between sites. Both availability zones must contain an equal number of hosts to provide failover capacity when either zone goes down.

At the storage layer, vSAN provides storage-level resilience through multiple mechanisms. vSAN fault domains enable the environment to tolerate entire rack failures in addition to single host, storage disk, or network failures, by organizing hosts into separate fault domains so that vSAN distributes object replicas across different physical racks. Configuring fault domains of smaller sizes reduces the impact of a potential rack failure. vSAN Express Storage Architecture (ESA) supports disaggregated storage clusters that pool storage devices across dedicated hosts, separating storage resources from compute hosts. Storage multipathing provides path selection policies with automatic failover to maintain storage access during path failures, and NFS 4.1 datastores support multipathing for redundant storage connectivity. Path policies must be consistent within a cluster. vSAN network implementations should typically use four physical uplinks for full redundancy, with additional failover NICs provisioned for the vSAN network. The teaming and failover policy configured on the backing virtual switch provides network redundancy for vSAN traffic.

At the networking layer, multi-NIC teaming, Link Aggregation Control Protocol (LACP), and failover policies handle network-level failover. Advanced NIC teaming can implement an air gap so that a failure on one network path does not affect the other. NSX tier-0 gateways support active-standby high availability mode with configurable failover behavior (preemptive or nonpreemptive). NSX Edge nodes perform graceful failover to a peer Edge when a failure or maintenance event occurs. For multi-site NSX deployments, the NSX Global Manager can be deployed in a highly available cluster model with a standby cluster ready for manual failover. In unplanned switchover scenarios where a primary site becomes unavailable, a forced failover can be initiated to restore networking services at the surviving site.

VCF also provides failover capabilities for its management and operations components. vCenter supports native high availability through vCenter High Availability, which deploys Active, Passive, and Witness nodes; when the Active node becomes unavailable, the Passive node automatically assumes the active role, preserving management plane operations without manual intervention. The Continuous Availability VCF Operations Model deploys a highly available cluster with data redundancy across different availability zones, maintaining service during a single availability zone failure. VCF Operations failover server sets establish server connections in a defined order and can link separate server sets, providing high availability for monitoring infrastructure in complex environments. Application monitoring high availability with activated collector groups in failover mode can continue monitoring applications without losing data or metrics even when a cloud proxy is down or its components fail. Cloud proxy itself supports high availability. The High Availability VCF Automation Model supports use of an external load balancer for management service resilience.

The VCF platform's Kubernetes consumption layer, provided by vSphere Supervisor and VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), extends these failover mechanisms to containerized workloads. Supervisor requires vSphere HA and fully-automated DRS on the underlying vSphere cluster, so VKS node virtual machines benefit from the same automatic restart and workload balancing that protects traditional VMs. In a zoned Supervisor deployment, Supervisor stretches across three vSphere clusters, with each cluster assigned to a vSphere Zone that represents a distinct failure domain; a failure in one zone does not automatically affect workloads running in the other zones. Starting with VCF and VKS 3.6, Node Auto Placement allows the VKS placement engine to automatically manage resource distribution across failure domains, spreading node pools across zones using failure domain specifications in the cluster topology. Before any zone is taken out of service, control plane nodes must be relocated to a different zone to maintain cluster availability, supporting planned maintenance without workload interruption. Kubernetes provides self-healing behavior at the workload layer: the platform automatically replaces failed containers, reschedules workloads when nodes become unavailable, and continuously reconciles the system toward its desired state.
