This permission model ties record lifecycle actions directly to the identity of the user who performed them, providing an accountability chain for operational records.

NSX Manager generates audit logs for all API calls, CLI commands, and UI access, with audit logging enabled by default and not configurable to be disabled. Each audit log entry includes metadata fields such as audit flag, component name, entity ID, request ID, log level, subcomponent, update flag, and username, which together link every network management action to the user who performed it. NSX also records user operations including UserName, ModuleName, Operation, and Operation status with timestamps and request identifiers. Role binding changes for remote user assignments are logged with timestamp, component identifier, audit flag, user identity, module name, operation name, operation status, and role binding details. NSX audit logs can be accessed programmatically through the /api/v1/administration/audit-logs API endpoint, and an audit log associated with an API call includes a request ID parameter (req-id) to identify a specific API call.

VCF Automation creates audit logs for reportable actions such as deploying cloud templates or catalog items, performing Day 2 actions on resources and deployments, and creating, deleting, or updating property groups and policies (including Day 2 actions, lease, content sharing, approval, deployment limit, and resource quota policies). Each log entry includes a timestamp, message, user name, project name, and trace ID value. Organization Administrator and Organization Auditor roles can view these audit logs.

VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor extend non-repudiation to the workload consumption layer. ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding resources can be configured with a validationActions value of Audit, which causes the Kubernetes API server to record an audit event for every policy evaluation attempt, capturing the requesting principal's identity, the resource targeted, and the policy outcome. When the Deny action is combined with Audit, both the enforcement decision and the corresponding audit record are generated for the same request, creating a linked account of what was attempted, by whom, and what the result was. Operators configure these bindings to control which resource operations and policy evaluations generate audit records, giving platform administrators precise control over the scope of the audit trail. VKS Cluster Management Security Policies extend this pattern to managed clusters: the default Policy enforcement action is Deny, which blocks admission requests with violations; a Dry run option generates policy evaluation records without blocking, supporting audit-only observation of policy compliance. Pod Security Admission (PSA) in Supervisor provides an additional audit channel for workload operations. PSA supports enforce, audit, and warn modes per namespace; in audit mode, security policy violations are recorded as audit annotations without blocking the workload, generating a record of every pod that violated the applicable security profile and the identity of the principal that submitted it. The default VKS cluster configuration sets the PodSecurityStandard audit level to privileged and the enforce level to restricted, so security profile violations are always captured in the audit record even when the pod is admitted. For high-assurance deployments, Kubernetes guidance recommends applying cryptographic protections to audit logs to make them tamper-proof and confidential; organizations requiring non-repudiation as a defensible claim should forward VKS and Supervisor audit logs to an external, append-only system.

The platform's PKI infrastructure strengthens non-repudiation through certificate-based authentication. VCF supports Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) responders that verify a user's private key has not been compromised or revoked, and maintains Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs) to identify certificates that should no longer be trusted. VCF can detect when a certificate has been revoked by the issuing certificate authority. These mechanisms help confirm that a presented identity was valid at the time an action occurred, making it harder for an individual to claim their credentials were invalid or compromised after the fact.

Audit logs from VCF Operations can be sent to an external syslog server to meet security auditing requirements, with configurable syslog server IP address or hostname and port. VCF Operations for Networks integrates its audit log with syslog, and the syslog collector can be configured to collect all audit logs. Each syslog and audit log message contains a timestamp generated by an NTP server when configured. vCenter stores events in its internal database for a configurable retention period, after which they are deleted; auditors should confirm that external log forwarding is configured before this retention window closes to preserve the complete audit trail. This forwarding capability supports long-term retention and tamper-evident storage on external log management platforms, further strengthening non-repudiation by preserving an independent record of actions outside the platform itself.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) contributes to non-repudiation through identity binding, audit-relevant observability, and integrity-anchored model storage that together create records associating AI workload operations with the identities that performed them.

PAIS access is bound to authenticated user identities through OIDC group-based authorization. The authorizedGroups field in the PAISConfiguration custom resource restricts service access to named OIDC groups, so AI service operations are attributable to authenticated users rather than anonymous service callers. HTTPS-based communication with the OIDC provider is required, and PAIS uses a configurable CA trust bundle for secure communication with external services and databases, helping protect the authentication exchange against tampering or impersonation.

For AI operation tracing and metrics, PAIS supports sending traces to an OpenTelemetry collector over HTTP/protobuf or gRPC, configured through the observability parameters in the PAISConfiguration custom resource. The observability.prometheusRuntime setting activates a Prometheus runtime with a configurable storage class and a metrics retention period that defaults to 14 days. These records associate inference and LLM operations with the identities that initiated them and provide an audit-relevant trail at the AI application layer.

For models themselves, the PAIS Model Gallery anchors non-repudiation of ML model provenance. Model revisions are stored as immutable OCI artifacts identified by content digest, with deduplication so that identical content pushed multiple times resolves to a single revision. Model Gallery assigns a unique digest to each revision when the same model is pushed multiple times, and PAIS Deep Learning VMs perform model validation through hash code checking, malware scanning, serialization-attack scanning, and inference testing with Triton Inference Server. These mechanisms produce a tamper-evident record of what model content was uploaded and validated, supporting the ability to attribute model changes to a specific revision.

These PAIS-specific mechanisms supplement the broader audit logging, log forwarding, identity, and platform audit records provided by VCF, VCF SSO, and DSM. The organization must enable observability components and integrate the resulting traces and metrics with a centralized log management system to address the full intent of the control.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to non-repudiation by generating audit records for network security enforcement actions and by enabling controls that attribute those actions to authenticated identities.

The VMware vDefend distributed firewall (DFW) supports per-rule logging in multiple modes, including Application Connectivity Strategy configurations where logging is enabled for all matched traffic. When the DFW operates with a Deny List strategy and logging enabled, traffic matching the policy produces a log entry recording the enforcement action. The DFW also supports a REJECT action, producing a distinct enforcement record for blocked connections. When Identity Firewall is enabled, DFW rules can be scoped to specific user groups and identities. Identity Firewall rules support a Reject action targeting specified user groups, services, and destinations, meaning access-denial events can be attributed to authenticated user identities rather than source IP addresses alone, which strengthens attribution at the network layer.

The VMware vDefend gateway firewall similarly supports per-rule logging of matched rules, creating a parallel record of north-south enforcement actions on NSX edge nodes.

Security Intelligence, a capability within the Security Services Platform (SSP), enables administrators to review analyzed traffic flows and transition security posture from observation to enforcement by modifying allow policies to deny after confirming no additional unprotected flows exist. These policy enforcement decisions create a record of when and by whom the enforcement posture changed. SSP alarms can be acknowledged or suppressed by users; those actions are attributed to the acting user, creating an operational decision audit trail.

vDefend's Malware Prevention capability and Network Detection and Response (NDR) generate security event records and can integrate with Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems, providing additional channels for exporting security events into centralized logging infrastructure.

The broader VCF platform handles administrative non-repudiation for management plane operations, such as who created or modified a firewall rule. vDefend provides the corresponding data plane records: what the firewall enforced, what threats were detected, and how analysts responded. Coverage is Contributes rather than Meets because the control's full scope spans organizational processes and management plane accountability that depend on VCF infrastructure beyond vDefend's data plane enforcement.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) contributes to non-repudiation through audit logging and access controls that tie recovery administrative actions to authenticated identities. All PNR operations require authentication through VCF SSO. PNR records operational information in log files that exclude sensitive data such as private keys and passwords, and generates events covering protection group operations, recovery plan execution, and licensing status changes.

PNR includes a defined set of roles with specific privileges that control what each user can perform. This role-based access model links actions to identity, supporting the attribution chain that non-repudiation requires. For high-impact operations, PNR requires explicit user confirmation before proceeding: the reprotect operation requires the user to confirm the action is irreversible before PNR proceeds, creating a documented acknowledgment tied to the authenticated user identity.

These audit events flow through the VMware vCenter event system and can be forwarded via syslog to external log management platforms for long-term retention, supporting the non-repudiation chain by preserving evidence of who performed which recovery actions and when. Organizations must configure syslog forwarding and manage log retention outside PNR to complete the chain.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

Non-repudiation requires technical mechanisms that create records linking specific actions to specific individuals, making it difficult for a user to credibly deny having performed a given operation. VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) contributes to this capability through audit logging for managed databases and role-based access controls that tie actions to authenticated accounts.

PostgreSQL databases managed by DSM include the pgaudit extension, which provides detailed audit logging for database-level operations. The pgaudit extension records SQL statements, the roles involved, and object-level actions, creating a log trail that associates database activity with the authenticated session that initiated it. The extension is available across all supported PostgreSQL versions. Auditors should verify that pgaudit is enabled and that log output is captured and retained in a centralized log store.

The role-based access model in DSM supports the attribution side of non-repudiation for administrative actions. The DSM console restricts the views and actions available to each user based on their assigned role, so actions performed through the DSM interface can be associated with specific authenticated accounts. Administrative operations such as database provisioning, policy changes, and backup configuration are performed by users authenticated to DSM and are tied to those sessions.

Strong authentication reinforces the link between actions and identities. DSM supports configurable pg_hba.conf entries for PostgreSQL database connections, including methods such as SCRAM-SHA-256, which help establish that the account performing a given action is the legitimate account holder.

DSM does not delete audit logs stored in the S3 repository, retaining the audit trail in a form that is not subject to in-platform deletion. This supports chain-of-custody requirements by keeping historical records accessible for review and dispute resolution.

For SQL Server databases, the dsm_db_user role denies the IMPERSONATE privilege on the database owner account, which restricts users from assuming the identity and full permissions of the Database Owner and keeps actions attributed to the account that executed them.

Non-repudiation as an organizational capability requires more than technical logging within a single platform. Organizations must integrate DSM's audit logs with a centralized log management or SIEM system to provide tamper-evident storage, long-term retention, and chain-of-custody controls. DSM supports log forwarding to syslog or VCF Log Management to facilitate this integration. Policies governing log access, retention periods, and dispute resolution procedures are organizational responsibilities that lie outside the platform itself.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides mechanisms that support non-repudiation of administrative and application-layer actions.

At the administrative plane, Avi's role-based access architecture ties actions to specific authenticated identities. Configuring purpose-scoped roles, such as a dedicated SSL administration role with write access limited to SSL/TLS certificate management and a corresponding dedicated admin account, reduces the permissible action surface for each identity and strengthens audit log attribution. This practice is documented in Avi's 9.1 security guidance for App Transport Security.

At the application traffic layer, Avi's DataScript capability extends the audit record beyond administrative actions. DataScript can enforce blocklist-based access control against a named IP group, terminating connections from blocked clients and logging each violation with client IP attribution. This creates application-layer records that establish which client addresses attempted or completed access and what outcome was applied.

Avi supports syslog forwarding to connect its audit streams to external log management or SIEM platforms. Non-repudiation as a complete organizational capability requires that Avi's logs be forwarded to and preserved in a centralized, tamper-evident log management system operated by the organization. The organization is responsible for configuring the forwarding destination, setting retention periods, and protecting the integrity of stored records.

One important caveat for 9.1 deployments: Avi Pulse does not log audit records for trusted channel initiation and termination, trusted channel function failures, unsuccessful certificate validation attempts, client authentication failures, or SSH session establishment. Organizations relying on Avi Pulse for audit data should supplement it with direct Controller log collection via syslog to capture these security-relevant events and maintain a complete non-repudiation record.

### Control CR 3.1

> Communication integrity: Components shall provide the capability to protect the integrity of data in transit.  Communication integrity enhancement: Use of cryptographic mechanisms to protect integrity.  Support for updates: Provide a mechanism to verify and apply security updates.  Physical tamper resistance/detection: Capability to detect or resist physical tampering.  Provisioning Product: Ensure secure provisioning of the product during manufacturing.  Integrity of the boot process: Verify the integrity of the boot process (Secure Boot).  Integrity of data at rest: Protect the integrity of sensitive data stored on the component.

**SCF Controls:** CRY-04

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides cryptographic mechanisms across its component stack to protect the integrity of data in transit. These mechanisms operate across storage cluster traffic, management plane communications, and network overlay tunnels, and rely on validated cryptographic modules throughout.

All VCF management communications use TLS, which provides integrity verification through message authentication codes (MACs) on every transmitted record. NSX Manager has modern TLS protocol versions enabled by default and older versions disabled. The Telegraf monitoring agent is configured with a minimum TLS version for its communication channels. VCF Operations supports a FIPS Security Mode that, when enabled, imposes additional algorithm and cipher prerequisites on management plane communications.

NSX, which provides the networking infrastructure for VCF, is configured to use FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules by default. These modules are validated through the NIST Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). NSX Manager uses FIPS 140 approved algorithms for authentication to its cryptographic module. NSX Manager also supports activation or deactivation of cipher suites through API configuration to align with specific security requirements. For tunnel-based connectivity, the NSX L2 VPN FIPS compliance suite uses FIPS-validated authenticated encryption with Perfect Forward Secrecy enabled, protecting both confidentiality and integrity of tunneled traffic. NSX supports the Wrapped Encapsulating Security Payload (WESP) protocol, which builds on Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) and provides integrity and authentication services for network traffic while allowing intermediate devices to determine whether data confidentiality is in use.

VCF Operations for Networks uses FIPS-validated cryptographic modules for both internal and external connections. The component uses BC-FJA (Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API) version 2.0.0 as its validated cryptographic module. FIPS mode can be enabled specifically for external connections, restricting all external communication to FIPS-validated modules. VCF Operations for Networks supports several encryption algorithms and ciphers for its data sources, allowing administrators to align cryptographic settings with organizational requirements. VCF Automation also uses the BouncyCastle FIPS 2.0.0 cryptographic module, which is compliant with FIPS 140-3.

The VMware vCenter appliance uses VMware's Boring Crypto Module version 6.0, which provides FIPS-validated AES, ECDSA, HMAC, RSA, and SHA2 algorithms, and the OpenSSL FIPS Object Module (versions 2.0.20-vmw and 3.0), which extends coverage to additional algorithms including SHA-3 and PBKDF. Starting in vSphere 9.0, all TLS profiles across the vSphere platform are FIPS compliant.

vSAN data-in-transit (DIT) encryption is a cluster-wide setting that, when enabled, encrypts all data and metadata traffic as it transits across hosts in the cluster. This includes fault tolerance traffic between cluster nodes. The setting is configurable during encryption configuration and applies uniformly to all inter-host vSAN communication. vSAN Data Services include both data-at-rest encryption and data-in-transit encryption as integrated capabilities. The Reliable Data Transport (RDT) protocol handles communication between hosts over the vSAN VMkernel ports, and enabling DIT encryption adds cryptographic integrity protection to that transport layer.

vSAN uses end-to-end checksums to verify the integrity of stored and transmitted data, confirming that each copy of a file matches the source. The system checks data validity during read and write operations, and if a checksum mismatch is detected, vSAN automatically repairs the data by overwriting the incorrect copy with correct data.

Encrypted vSphere vMotion protects the integrity and confidentiality of data transferred during VM migrations. vSphere vMotion always uses encryption when migrating encrypted virtual machines. For virtual machines that are not encrypted, the encrypted vMotion setting can be configured as Disabled, Opportunistic (use encrypted vMotion if supported, which is the default), or Required (do not migrate if encryption is unsupported). Fault Tolerance log traffic, which replicates running VM state to a secondary host, can be encrypted separately to protect that synchronization channel. Auditors should note that storage vMotion operations performed entirely within a single host, where only the datastore changes without a host transfer, are not covered by the vMotion encryption setting.

For storage protocols, vSAN File Service supports NFS4 Kerberos authentication with security options krb5, krb5i, and krb5p. The krb5i level provides authentication plus integrity checking on all NFS operations without the overhead of full data encryption. The krb5p level adds encryption on top of integrity checking. Both Kerberos-authenticated and system-authenticated NFS4 mounts are supported on the same share, giving administrators flexibility to apply integrity verification where needed. For iSCSI storage connections, VMware ESX supports configurable header and data digests that verify data integrity at the iSCSI layer, beyond the checks that TCP and Ethernet already provide.

VCF Operations monitors transmission integrity through metrics that track frames transmitted or received with bad checksums (CRC values), which indicate data corrupted during transmission. Frames with bad checksums are dropped and not propagated to other ports, which limits the spread of corrupted data on the network.

VCF Operations orchestrator signs packages and encrypts them for data protection, providing integrity assurance for software distribution across the environment. Package signing certificates provide digital identification for encrypted communication and sign packages. Packages exported from a VCF Operations orchestrator server are digitally signed, and the recommended practice is to use certificates signed by a Certificate Authority rather than self-signed certificates.

ESX hosts generate high-quality entropy that supports security-related operations such as generating encryption keys for secure communication over the network. This entropy source underpins the strength of cryptographic keys used across the platform for transmission protection.

The VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters managed by Supervisor extend these protections to the workload consumption layer. Kubernetes enforces TLS across all control plane communications as a structural requirement of its architecture: connections between the API server and etcd, between the API server and kubelets, and between the API server and cluster clients are TLS-protected by default. Cipher suite selection and minimum protocol version are configurable at multiple points in the control plane. The kube-apiserver accepts a `--tls-cipher-suites` parameter that restricts the server to a specified list of cipher suites, and a `--tls-min-version` parameter that sets the minimum acceptable protocol version. The kubelet exposes equivalent settings through `tlsCipherSuites`, which controls which cipher suites are available for HTTPS connections, and `tlsMinVersion`, which sets the minimum acceptable version. For the kube-scheduler, the `--tls-cipher-suites` parameter restricts cipher negotiation to approved suites. Antrea, the CNI plugin in Supervisor environments, also supports configurable TLS cipher suites for its inter-component communications. The vSphere Cloud Provider Interface (CPI), which handles the integration between VKS clusters and the underlying vSphere infrastructure, supports a configurable set of TLS cipher suites for its API communications with vSphere. VKS on Supervisor ships with managed TLS certificates that underpin the cryptographic infrastructure for these connections.

At the workload ingress layer, the Contour ingress controller package exposes `tls.minimum-protocol-version` and `tls.maximum-protocol-version` parameters in its configuration file, giving administrators control over the protocol floor for inbound traffic to applications running on VKS clusters. For service-to-service communication within a cluster, the Istio service mesh standard package operates in mutual TLS (mTLS) mode, requiring both parties to authenticate and encrypting inter-service traffic. The minimum protocol version for Istio mTLS is configurable via `meshConfig.meshMTLS.minProtocolVersion` and defaults to TLS 1.2, with TLS 1.3 also available as an option. Environments with elevated cryptographic requirements can activate FIPS mode at the cluster level through the `ClusterClass` variable `fips.enabled`, which restricts the cryptographic algorithms available to those meeting Federal Information Processing Standards; the Istio standard package also provides FIPS compliance as part of its standard configuration. Harbor, the container image registry available as a Supervisor service, secures registry traffic through TLS certificate configuration via the `tlsCertificate.tls.crt` and `tlsCertificate.tls.key` fields. The Supervisor secret injection agent automatically configures mutual TLS for communication with the vault-agent-injector service using auto-generated certificates.

Defining the integrity-protection policy for customer workload traffic, validating that customer application transmissions use authenticated encryption, and setting the integrity policy for workload-to-workload traffic running in VKS pods are organizational data-policy decisions.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) applies TLS to its AI service communication paths through CA trust bundle management and client TLS configuration in the PAISConfiguration custom resource. The `spec.clientTls.caBundleRefs` setting references ConfigMap resources that hold CA trust bundles for outbound TLS to the OIDC provider and Harbor container registry, with separate bundles recommended per application and periodic updates required before certificate expiration. PAIS endpoints use self-signed certificate authentication; the CA certificate is stored in the `pais-ingress-default` Kubernetes Secret in the Private AI Services namespace, and administrators retrieve it with `kubectl get secrets/pais-ingress-default -o yaml` to distribute to client services.

PAIS requires HTTPS trust bundles for every external integration point: OIDC providers, Harbor registries, content management systems used as Knowledge Base data sources, MCP servers that supply tools to models, and the pgvector database connection through Data Services Manager (DSM). CA trust bundles are imported as certificate files or ConfigMap references, and dedicated settings exist for each integration class, including `ca-trust-bundle-for-dsm` for the pgvector database path. When connecting an MCP server, the issuer certificate must be added as a CA trust bundle before the HTTPS connection can be established. The PAIS Supervisor endpoint communicates over HTTPS on port 6443 with certificate-based authentication, and the Prometheus metrics endpoint uses TLS with the same self-signed certificate model retrieved from the `pais-ingress-default` Secret.

GPU-accelerated VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters provisioned through PAIS run a FIPS-enabled VKR build (v1.33.1-vmware.1-fips-vkr.2 on Ubuntu 24.04), which provides FIPS-compliant cryptographic modules for control plane and worker node communication. JWT-based authentication for VCF Automation integration uses the `TM_CERT_BASE64` credential captured by running `openssl s_client` against the VCF Automation FQDN on port 443 and encoding the certificate in base64. The VCF Consumption CLI supports optional CA certificate validation through the `--ca-certificate` option when establishing context connections to VCF Automation endpoints. The Model Gallery assigns a unique digest to each model revision when the same model is pushed multiple times, which helps administrators detect tampering or unintended overwrites during artifact retrieval from Harbor.

Administrators should note that TLS client authentication and certificate validation on the Prometheus endpoint are turned off by default and must be explicitly enabled through the `tls-client-authentication` setting in PAISConfiguration to activate mutual certificate validation.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to transmission integrity monitoring through several complementary mechanism clusters. The IDS/IPS engine, available in both Distributed and Gateway profiles, includes signature rules that match on TCP protocol fields, including flags, acknowledgement numbers, sequence numbers, and window sizes, to detect protocol anomalies and tampering indicators in network traffic. Custom IDS/IPS signature rules also support TLS-specific matching and can store TLS/SSL certificate artifacts to disk for forensic analysis and threat investigation. The IDS/IPS engine detects obsolete or integrity-deficient protocols by combining port verification with application identification, flagging traffic that uses older SSL and TLS versions that predate current security standards, early SMB versions that lack message signing, and cleartext protocols such as telnet.

The Security Services Platform's Network Detection and Response capability supports SSL verification for event logs sent over HTTPS, protecting the integrity of telemetry data in transit between NDR sensors and the platform. The Security Services Platform's security segmentation analysis identifies risky application protocols that transport data without adequate integrity protection and provides guidance on restricting or blocking them through vDefend policy.

The Gateway Firewall's TLS Inspection feature operates as a TLS proxy to transparently intercept and inspect encrypted traffic for threats while retaining end-to-end encryption for compliant sessions. Administrators can configure Crypto Enforcement on TLS Inspection profiles to require that client and server connections meet defined minimum cipher suite requirements, providing a policy-enforcement mechanism over the cryptographic strength of inspected flows.

The Security Services Platform establishes external communications using TLS handshake negotiation and rejects connections that use deprecated cryptographic algorithms, weak key lengths, or older protocol versions. The SSP Installer and NDR Sensor components use FIPS 140-2 and FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules for their own internal communications, including the VMware BouncyCastle Module and the VMware OpenSSL FIPS provider Object Module.

vDefend does not itself provide cryptographic integrity mechanisms such as message authentication codes or digital signatures for data in transit. Those protections are the responsibility of the applications and transport protocols in use and fall outside vDefend's scope.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) applies cryptographic mechanisms to protect the integrity of data in transit across management communications and replication data flows.

All management and API communications to the PNR server use TLS, which provides integrity verification through message authentication on each transmitted record. PNR requires an SSL/TLS endpoint certificate for every TLS connection to the PNR server; certificates must be server certificates with x509v3 Extended Key Usage indicating TLS Web Server Authentication. PNR uses TLS certificates and private keys to protect network communication and establish authentication with connected services. Administrators can configure the minimum TLS protocol version through envoy proxy settings by editing the tls_minimum_protocol_version parameter in the envoy-proxy-config.json file, allowing organizations to enforce a higher minimum where policy requires. When the minimum version is changed, PNR requires the setting to be updated consistently across all system components.

Replication traffic between sites supports optional network encryption. vSphere Replication supports enabling data encryption for replication traffic on a per-replication basis. When replicating encrypted virtual machines using array-based or vSphere Replication protection groups, PNR requires that the recovery and protected sites share a common Key Management Server (KMS) or that KMS clusters at both sites use common encryption keys, so that replicated data maintains its encryption across sites.

VCF adds platform-layer integrity protections, including vSAN data-in-transit encryption for inter-host storage traffic.

Selecting which data flows receive network encryption and managing the certificate and key management infrastructure are organizational data-policy decisions.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) applies cryptographic protection to data in transit across its major communication paths, using TLS to protect both the confidentiality and integrity of transmitted data.

DSM enforces FIPS 140-3 compliant cryptographic standards for certificate handling. The platform validates certificate algorithms at upload time, rejecting keys or signatures that do not conform to FIPS 140-3 requirements. These requirements apply to trusted root certificates and to custom certificates used during database version upgrades.

Database connections from clients to DSM-managed PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQL Server clusters use TLS. PostgreSQL instances enforce a minimum TLS protocol version through the `ssl_min_protocol_version` setting. PostgreSQL supports TLS-encrypted connections with certificate-based authentication and CA certificate verification. MySQL enforces TLS on client connections, and FIPS mode on the MySQL client side can be activated through the `ssl_fips_mode` setting. DSM also manages SQL Server clusters using TLS for client connections; the `network.tlsProtocols` setting defines the allowed protocol versions for those connections. DSM validates SSL trust during MySQL replication operations.

DSM's certificate management supports custom certificates for database clusters. Administrators can configure database cluster certificates through the DSM UI or via Kubernetes cert-manager, which outputs certificates as Kubernetes TLS Secrets. DSM validates certificate algorithms at upload time and during version upgrades to confirm FIPS 140-3 compliance. The DSM API provides endpoints for retrieving the certificate authority needed to establish secure connections to individual database clusters.

Communication between DSM components is also secured with TLS. The Consumption Operator requires a TLS certificate for communication with the DSM provider. DSM log forwarding supports Auto TLS configuration to secure connections to the SMTP server.

For PostgreSQL data migration, DSM replication connections support SSL/TLS with a configurable SSL mode parameter to secure data in transit between source and target databases.

To fully satisfy this control, organizations should verify that clients connecting to DSM databases are configured to use the TLS connection path and that CA certificates are properly deployed to client trust stores. DSM's internal and external communication paths rely on this client-side configuration for the full TLS chain to be effective.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides TLS termination and re-encryption as a core function of its Service Engine data plane, giving administrators direct control over the cryptographic mechanisms applied to application traffic in transit. The SSL/TLS Profile, configured under Templates > Security > SSL/TLS Profile, defines the accepted protocol versions and the prioritized list of cipher suites for each virtual service. Built-in profile templates provide starting configurations, and administrators can create custom profiles that restrict accepted cipher suites to those using authenticated encryption. The Service Engine completes the TLS handshake for both client-to-Avi and Avi-to-backend sessions, so traffic passes through a cryptographically protected channel on each leg. The SSL Profile also supports post-quantum cryptography cipher suites, providing an option for deployments that need to adopt algorithms addressing emerging cryptographic requirements.

For deployments requiring validated cryptographic modules, Avi supports a FIPS mode in which only FIPS-compliant algorithms are used for all cryptographic operations. When FIPS mode is enabled on a Controller cluster, the Controller and Service Engines use the OpenSSL FIPS Provider, which has been validated at FIPS 140-3 Security Level 1 and awarded CMVP Certificate #4985, for both data-plane TLS sessions and management-plane communications between Avi components.

Cryptographic key material backing TLS certificates can be stored in Thales Luna Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) integrated with both the Avi Controller and Service Engines. Service Engine HSM groups, defined under Templates > Security > HSM Groups, allow Service Engines to authenticate with HSMs for secure storage of SSL certificate private keys. TLS session persistence data shared between Service Engines in a cluster is transmitted in encrypted form, maintaining the integrity of session routing state across the data plane. Configuration backup archives transferred to remote destinations use Secure Copy Protocol (SCP) or Secure File Transfer Protocol (SFTP). Intel QuickAssist Technology (QAT) acceleration is available on Service Engines for bulk cryptographic operations following TLS handshake completion.

For Kubernetes workloads, the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) supports PKIProfile custom resources, which enable certificate-based authentication and encryption for backend service communication. The System-Standard SSL Profile applied through AKO provides TLS re-encryption for traffic to backend servers.

Defining which application traffic requires integrity protection and configuring the virtual service profiles accordingly are organizational data-policy decisions.

### Control CR 3.1(1)

> Communication integrity: Components shall provide the capability to protect the integrity of data in transit.  Communication integrity enhancement: Use of cryptographic mechanisms to protect integrity.  Support for updates: Provide a mechanism to verify and apply security updates.  Physical tamper resistance/detection: Capability to detect or resist physical tampering.  Provisioning Product: Ensure secure provisioning of the product during manufacturing.  Integrity of the boot process: Verify the integrity of the boot process (Secure Boot).  Integrity of data at rest: Protect the integrity of sensitive data stored on the component.

**SCF Controls:** CRY-04

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides cryptographic mechanisms across its component stack to protect the integrity of data in transit. These mechanisms operate across storage cluster traffic, management plane communications, and network overlay tunnels, and rely on validated cryptographic modules throughout.

All VCF management communications use TLS, which provides integrity verification through message authentication codes (MACs) on every transmitted record. NSX Manager has modern TLS protocol versions enabled by default and older versions disabled. The Telegraf monitoring agent is configured with a minimum TLS version for its communication channels. VCF Operations supports a FIPS Security Mode that, when enabled, imposes additional algorithm and cipher prerequisites on management plane communications.

NSX, which provides the networking infrastructure for VCF, is configured to use FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules by default. These modules are validated through the NIST Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). NSX Manager uses FIPS 140 approved algorithms for authentication to its cryptographic module. NSX Manager also supports activation or deactivation of cipher suites through API configuration to align with specific security requirements. For tunnel-based connectivity, the NSX L2 VPN FIPS compliance suite uses FIPS-validated authenticated encryption with Perfect Forward Secrecy enabled, protecting both confidentiality and integrity of tunneled traffic. NSX supports the Wrapped Encapsulating Security Payload (WESP) protocol, which builds on Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) and provides integrity and authentication services for network traffic while allowing intermediate devices to determine whether data confidentiality is in use.

VCF Operations for Networks uses FIPS-validated cryptographic modules for both internal and external connections. The component uses BC-FJA (Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API) version 2.0.0 as its validated cryptographic module. FIPS mode can be enabled specifically for external connections, restricting all external communication to FIPS-validated modules. VCF Operations for Networks supports several encryption algorithms and ciphers for its data sources, allowing administrators to align cryptographic settings with organizational requirements. VCF Automation also uses the BouncyCastle FIPS 2.0.0 cryptographic module, which is compliant with FIPS 140-3.

The VMware vCenter appliance uses VMware's Boring Crypto Module version 6.0, which provides FIPS-validated AES, ECDSA, HMAC, RSA, and SHA2 algorithms, and the OpenSSL FIPS Object Module (versions 2.0.20-vmw and 3.0), which extends coverage to additional algorithms including SHA-3 and PBKDF. Starting in vSphere 9.0, all TLS profiles across the vSphere platform are FIPS compliant.

vSAN data-in-transit (DIT) encryption is a cluster-wide setting that, when enabled, encrypts all data and metadata traffic as it transits across hosts in the cluster. This includes fault tolerance traffic between cluster nodes. The setting is configurable during encryption configuration and applies uniformly to all inter-host vSAN communication. vSAN Data Services include both data-at-rest encryption and data-in-transit encryption as integrated capabilities. The Reliable Data Transport (RDT) protocol handles communication between hosts over the vSAN VMkernel ports, and enabling DIT encryption adds cryptographic integrity protection to that transport layer.

vSAN uses end-to-end checksums to verify the integrity of stored and transmitted data, confirming that each copy of a file matches the source. The system checks data validity during read and write operations, and if a checksum mismatch is detected, vSAN automatically repairs the data by overwriting the incorrect copy with correct data.

Encrypted vSphere vMotion protects the integrity and confidentiality of data transferred during VM migrations. vSphere vMotion always uses encryption when migrating encrypted virtual machines. For virtual machines that are not encrypted, the encrypted vMotion setting can be configured as Disabled, Opportunistic (use encrypted vMotion if supported, which is the default), or Required (do not migrate if encryption is unsupported). Fault Tolerance log traffic, which replicates running VM state to a secondary host, can be encrypted separately to protect that synchronization channel. Auditors should note that storage vMotion operations performed entirely within a single host, where only the datastore changes without a host transfer, are not covered by the vMotion encryption setting.

For storage protocols, vSAN File Service supports NFS4 Kerberos authentication with security options krb5, krb5i, and krb5p. The krb5i level provides authentication plus integrity checking on all NFS operations without the overhead of full data encryption. The krb5p level adds encryption on top of integrity checking. Both Kerberos-authenticated and system-authenticated NFS4 mounts are supported on the same share, giving administrators flexibility to apply integrity verification where needed. For iSCSI storage connections, VMware ESX supports configurable header and data digests that verify data integrity at the iSCSI layer, beyond the checks that TCP and Ethernet already provide.

VCF Operations monitors transmission integrity through metrics that track frames transmitted or received with bad checksums (CRC values), which indicate data corrupted during transmission. Frames with bad checksums are dropped and not propagated to other ports, which limits the spread of corrupted data on the network.

VCF Operations orchestrator signs packages and encrypts them for data protection, providing integrity assurance for software distribution across the environment. Package signing certificates provide digital identification for encrypted communication and sign packages. Packages exported from a VCF Operations orchestrator server are digitally signed, and the recommended practice is to use certificates signed by a Certificate Authority rather than self-signed certificates.

ESX hosts generate high-quality entropy that supports security-related operations such as generating encryption keys for secure communication over the network. This entropy source underpins the strength of cryptographic keys used across the platform for transmission protection.

The VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters managed by Supervisor extend these protections to the workload consumption layer. Kubernetes enforces TLS across all control plane communications as a structural requirement of its architecture: connections between the API server and etcd, between the API server and kubelets, and between the API server and cluster clients are TLS-protected by default. Cipher suite selection and minimum protocol version are configurable at multiple points in the control plane. The kube-apiserver accepts a `--tls-cipher-suites` parameter that restricts the server to a specified list of cipher suites, and a `--tls-min-version` parameter that sets the minimum acceptable protocol version. The kubelet exposes equivalent settings through `tlsCipherSuites`, which controls which cipher suites are available for HTTPS connections, and `tlsMinVersion`, which sets the minimum acceptable version. For the kube-scheduler, the `--tls-cipher-suites` parameter restricts cipher negotiation to approved suites. Antrea, the CNI plugin in Supervisor environments, also supports configurable TLS cipher suites for its inter-component communications. The vSphere Cloud Provider Interface (CPI), which handles the integration between VKS clusters and the underlying vSphere infrastructure, supports a configurable set of TLS cipher suites for its API communications with vSphere. VKS on Supervisor ships with managed TLS certificates that underpin the cryptographic infrastructure for these connections.

At the workload ingress layer, the Contour ingress controller package exposes `tls.minimum-protocol-version` and `tls.maximum-protocol-version` parameters in its configuration file, giving administrators control over the protocol floor for inbound traffic to applications running on VKS clusters. For service-to-service communication within a cluster, the Istio service mesh standard package operates in mutual TLS (mTLS) mode, requiring both parties to authenticate and encrypting inter-service traffic. The minimum protocol version for Istio mTLS is configurable via `meshConfig.meshMTLS.minProtocolVersion` and defaults to TLS 1.2, with TLS 1.3 also available as an option. Environments with elevated cryptographic requirements can activate FIPS mode at the cluster level through the `ClusterClass` variable `fips.enabled`, which restricts the cryptographic algorithms available to those meeting Federal Information Processing Standards; the Istio standard package also provides FIPS compliance as part of its standard configuration. Harbor, the container image registry available as a Supervisor service, secures registry traffic through TLS certificate configuration via the `tlsCertificate.tls.crt` and `tlsCertificate.tls.key` fields. The Supervisor secret injection agent automatically configures mutual TLS for communication with the vault-agent-injector service using auto-generated certificates.

Defining the integrity-protection policy for customer workload traffic, validating that customer application transmissions use authenticated encryption, and setting the integrity policy for workload-to-workload traffic running in VKS pods are organizational data-policy decisions.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) applies TLS to its AI service communication paths through CA trust bundle management and client TLS configuration in the PAISConfiguration custom resource. The `spec.clientTls.caBundleRefs` setting references ConfigMap resources that hold CA trust bundles for outbound TLS to the OIDC provider and Harbor container registry, with separate bundles recommended per application and periodic updates required before certificate expiration. PAIS endpoints use self-signed certificate authentication; the CA certificate is stored in the `pais-ingress-default` Kubernetes Secret in the Private AI Services namespace, and administrators retrieve it with `kubectl get secrets/pais-ingress-default -o yaml` to distribute to client services.

PAIS requires HTTPS trust bundles for every external integration point: OIDC providers, Harbor registries, content management systems used as Knowledge Base data sources, MCP servers that supply tools to models, and the pgvector database connection through Data Services Manager (DSM). CA trust bundles are imported as certificate files or ConfigMap references, and dedicated settings exist for each integration class, including `ca-trust-bundle-for-dsm` for the pgvector database path. When connecting an MCP server, the issuer certificate must be added as a CA trust bundle before the HTTPS connection can be established. The PAIS Supervisor endpoint communicates over HTTPS on port 6443 with certificate-based authentication, and the Prometheus metrics endpoint uses TLS with the same self-signed certificate model retrieved from the `pais-ingress-default` Secret.

GPU-accelerated VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters provisioned through PAIS run a FIPS-enabled VKR build (v1.33.1-vmware.1-fips-vkr.2 on Ubuntu 24.04), which provides FIPS-compliant cryptographic modules for control plane and worker node communication. JWT-based authentication for VCF Automation integration uses the `TM_CERT_BASE64` credential captured by running `openssl s_client` against the VCF Automation FQDN on port 443 and encoding the certificate in base64. The VCF Consumption CLI supports optional CA certificate validation through the `--ca-certificate` option when establishing context connections to VCF Automation endpoints. The Model Gallery assigns a unique digest to each model revision when the same model is pushed multiple times, which helps administrators detect tampering or unintended overwrites during artifact retrieval from Harbor.

Administrators should note that TLS client authentication and certificate validation on the Prometheus endpoint are turned off by default and must be explicitly enabled through the `tls-client-authentication` setting in PAISConfiguration to activate mutual certificate validation.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to transmission integrity monitoring through several complementary mechanism clusters. The IDS/IPS engine, available in both Distributed and Gateway profiles, includes signature rules that match on TCP protocol fields, including flags, acknowledgement numbers, sequence numbers, and window sizes, to detect protocol anomalies and tampering indicators in network traffic. Custom IDS/IPS signature rules also support TLS-specific matching and can store TLS/SSL certificate artifacts to disk for forensic analysis and threat investigation. The IDS/IPS engine detects obsolete or integrity-deficient protocols by combining port verification with application identification, flagging traffic that uses older SSL and TLS versions that predate current security standards, early SMB versions that lack message signing, and cleartext protocols such as telnet.

The Security Services Platform's Network Detection and Response capability supports SSL verification for event logs sent over HTTPS, protecting the integrity of telemetry data in transit between NDR sensors and the platform. The Security Services Platform's security segmentation analysis identifies risky application protocols that transport data without adequate integrity protection and provides guidance on restricting or blocking them through vDefend policy.

The Gateway Firewall's TLS Inspection feature operates as a TLS proxy to transparently intercept and inspect encrypted traffic for threats while retaining end-to-end encryption for compliant sessions. Administrators can configure Crypto Enforcement on TLS Inspection profiles to require that client and server connections meet defined minimum cipher suite requirements, providing a policy-enforcement mechanism over the cryptographic strength of inspected flows.

The Security Services Platform establishes external communications using TLS handshake negotiation and rejects connections that use deprecated cryptographic algorithms, weak key lengths, or older protocol versions. The SSP Installer and NDR Sensor components use FIPS 140-2 and FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules for their own internal communications, including the VMware BouncyCastle Module and the VMware OpenSSL FIPS provider Object Module.

vDefend does not itself provide cryptographic integrity mechanisms such as message authentication codes or digital signatures for data in transit. Those protections are the responsibility of the applications and transport protocols in use and fall outside vDefend's scope.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) applies cryptographic mechanisms to protect the integrity of data in transit across management communications and replication data flows.

All management and API communications to the PNR server use TLS, which provides integrity verification through message authentication on each transmitted record. PNR requires an SSL/TLS endpoint certificate for every TLS connection to the PNR server; certificates must be server certificates with x509v3 Extended Key Usage indicating TLS Web Server Authentication. PNR uses TLS certificates and private keys to protect network communication and establish authentication with connected services. Administrators can configure the minimum TLS protocol version through envoy proxy settings by editing the tls_minimum_protocol_version parameter in the envoy-proxy-config.json file, allowing organizations to enforce a higher minimum where policy requires. When the minimum version is changed, PNR requires the setting to be updated consistently across all system components.

Replication traffic between sites supports optional network encryption. vSphere Replication supports enabling data encryption for replication traffic on a per-replication basis. When replicating encrypted virtual machines using array-based or vSphere Replication protection groups, PNR requires that the recovery and protected sites share a common Key Management Server (KMS) or that KMS clusters at both sites use common encryption keys, so that replicated data maintains its encryption across sites.

VCF adds platform-layer integrity protections, including vSAN data-in-transit encryption for inter-host storage traffic.

Selecting which data flows receive network encryption and managing the certificate and key management infrastructure are organizational data-policy decisions.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) applies cryptographic protection to data in transit across its major communication paths, using TLS to protect both the confidentiality and integrity of transmitted data.

DSM enforces FIPS 140-3 compliant cryptographic standards for certificate handling. The platform validates certificate algorithms at upload time, rejecting keys or signatures that do not conform to FIPS 140-3 requirements. These requirements apply to trusted root certificates and to custom certificates used during database version upgrades.

Database connections from clients to DSM-managed PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQL Server clusters use TLS. PostgreSQL instances enforce a minimum TLS protocol version through the `ssl_min_protocol_version` setting. PostgreSQL supports TLS-encrypted connections with certificate-based authentication and CA certificate verification. MySQL enforces TLS on client connections, and FIPS mode on the MySQL client side can be activated through the `ssl_fips_mode` setting. DSM also manages SQL Server clusters using TLS for client connections; the `network.tlsProtocols` setting defines the allowed protocol versions for those connections. DSM validates SSL trust during MySQL replication operations.

DSM's certificate management supports custom certificates for database clusters. Administrators can configure database cluster certificates through the DSM UI or via Kubernetes cert-manager, which outputs certificates as Kubernetes TLS Secrets. DSM validates certificate algorithms at upload time and during version upgrades to confirm FIPS 140-3 compliance. The DSM API provides endpoints for retrieving the certificate authority needed to establish secure connections to individual database clusters.

Communication between DSM components is also secured with TLS. The Consumption Operator requires a TLS certificate for communication with the DSM provider. DSM log forwarding supports Auto TLS configuration to secure connections to the SMTP server.

For PostgreSQL data migration, DSM replication connections support SSL/TLS with a configurable SSL mode parameter to secure data in transit between source and target databases.

To fully satisfy this control, organizations should verify that clients connecting to DSM databases are configured to use the TLS connection path and that CA certificates are properly deployed to client trust stores. DSM's internal and external communication paths rely on this client-side configuration for the full TLS chain to be effective.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides TLS termination and re-encryption as a core function of its Service Engine data plane, giving administrators direct control over the cryptographic mechanisms applied to application traffic in transit. The SSL/TLS Profile, configured under Templates > Security > SSL/TLS Profile, defines the accepted protocol versions and the prioritized list of cipher suites for each virtual service. Built-in profile templates provide starting configurations, and administrators can create custom profiles that restrict accepted cipher suites to those using authenticated encryption. The Service Engine completes the TLS handshake for both client-to-Avi and Avi-to-backend sessions, so traffic passes through a cryptographically protected channel on each leg. The SSL Profile also supports post-quantum cryptography cipher suites, providing an option for deployments that need to adopt algorithms addressing emerging cryptographic requirements.

For deployments requiring validated cryptographic modules, Avi supports a FIPS mode in which only FIPS-compliant algorithms are used for all cryptographic operations. When FIPS mode is enabled on a Controller cluster, the Controller and Service Engines use the OpenSSL FIPS Provider, which has been validated at FIPS 140-3 Security Level 1 and awarded CMVP Certificate #4985, for both data-plane TLS sessions and management-plane communications between Avi components.

Cryptographic key material backing TLS certificates can be stored in Thales Luna Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) integrated with both the Avi Controller and Service Engines. Service Engine HSM groups, defined under Templates > Security > HSM Groups, allow Service Engines to authenticate with HSMs for secure storage of SSL certificate private keys. TLS session persistence data shared between Service Engines in a cluster is transmitted in encrypted form, maintaining the integrity of session routing state across the data plane. Configuration backup archives transferred to remote destinations use Secure Copy Protocol (SCP) or Secure File Transfer Protocol (SFTP). Intel QuickAssist Technology (QAT) acceleration is available on Service Engines for bulk cryptographic operations following TLS handshake completion.

For Kubernetes workloads, the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) supports PKIProfile custom resources, which enable certificate-based authentication and encryption for backend service communication. The System-Standard SSL Profile applied through AKO provides TLS re-encryption for traffic to backend servers.

Defining which application traffic requires integrity protection and configuring the virtual service profiles accordingly are organizational data-policy decisions.

### Control CR 3.3

> Security functionality verification: Provide the capability to verify the correct operation of security functions.  Verification during boot: Verify security functions during the boot process.

**SCF Controls:** CHG-06

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several technical mechanisms that support verification of cybersecurity and data protection control functionality following changes, though the organizational process of systematically defining when verification is required and documenting results remains an additional responsibility.

VCF Operations includes a Configuration Drifts capability that monitors vCenter instances and vSphere clusters for deviations from assigned configuration templates. When a change is made, scheduled drift detection compares current configuration values against the desired state defined in those templates. Any deviation is flagged, and administrators can view and download drift status reports from the Configuration Drifts dashboard. Configuration templates can be integrated with a source control repository for versioning and change control, supporting automated testing and validation of configuration updates after changes are applied.

At the host and cluster level, vSphere Configuration Profiles manage the configuration of all hosts in a cluster collectively, ensuring consistency and detecting when individual host configurations deviate from the cluster-level desired state. vSphere Lifecycle Manager extends this to software specifications, offering compliance check operations that return the compliance status of each host in a cluster against the committed desired state. Together, these mechanisms allow administrators to confirm that host-level security controls remain intact after patching, upgrades, or configuration changes.

VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure objects against configured compliance benchmarks and identifies non-compliant objects along with their specific violations. When non-compliance is detected, VCF Operations provides actionable remediation recommendations. Automated remediation actions can also be configured to bring objects back into compliance based on severity. Separately, VCF includes automated compliance assessment capabilities for vSphere security controls based on the VMware vSphere Security Configuration Guide. These assessments can be run after changes to confirm that host and virtual machine configurations remain aligned with the established security baseline, where drift from the baseline after a change would indicate that a control is no longer functioning as intended.

NSX Manager maintains detailed audit logs that capture both old and new values for modified security policies. When a security policy is changed, the audit log records the user who performed the operation, the security policy identifier, rule configurations, action types, and sequence numbers. This level of detail allows administrators to review exactly what changed in a security policy and compare the before-and-after state, which is a foundational step in verifying that security controls continue to operate as designed after modifications.

Lifecycle management operations include built-in precheck validation. VCF Operations requires a precheck validation step before management component upgrades can proceed, and NSX Manager's upgrade coordinator includes pre-checks to validate upgrade readiness. These prechecks help confirm that the environment is in a known-good state before changes are applied.

VCF Automation provides a Content and Policies interface that identifies custom forms containing actions that cannot be validated. The Form Actions tab in the custom form designer lists all actions used in a form, allowing administrators to review non-validatable actions and verify their input validation code. This provides a mechanism for verifying that automation-driven controls continue to enforce expected behavior after form or policy modifications. VCF Automation day 2 action policies control which users can run selected actions on deployed resources, with only users matching the policy criteria permitted to execute those actions. After changes to these policies, administrators can review the policy configuration to confirm that the intended access restrictions remain in place.

At the Kubernetes consumption layer, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor provide additional mechanisms for verifying security control behavior after changes. Pod Security Admission supports three enforcement modes that can be set independently per namespace: enforce, audit, and warn. The enforce mode blocks pods that do not comply with the configured Pod Security Standard; the audit mode logs non-compliant pod creation attempts without blocking; and the warn mode returns a warning to the API client without blocking. VKS cluster management provides out-of-the-box security policy templates, including baseline and restricted profiles that mirror Pod Security Standard definitions, and custom policies can be created in Dry run mode to observe their impact before enabling full enforcement. After a change to Pod Security Admission configuration, operators can apply the restricted profile in audit or warn mode to observe which workloads would be affected, with the audit log capturing the results for review.

Kubernetes ValidatingAdmissionPolicy bindings support validationActions of Deny, Warn, and Audit to control how validation failures are handled during admission. When set to Audit, policy violations are recorded in the Kubernetes audit log without blocking traffic, allowing operators to observe policy match behavior after a policy change without disrupting production workloads. The FailurePolicy field governs behavior when a policy expression fails to evaluate, and bindings are scoped to namespaces so that namespace-level labels determine enforcement scope. After deploying or modifying a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy, operators can submit representative test resources to verify that validation rules fire as intended.

Kubernetes RBAC provides a SelfSubjectAccessReview capability that allows operators to query whether a given subject holds a specific permission on a given resource, enabling direct confirmation that role bindings are applying as intended after changes to Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, or ClusterRoleBindings. Pod security contexts can also be reviewed after policy changes to verify that individual workloads are operating within the intended privilege boundaries.

At the Supervisor infrastructure level, core cluster component pods such as etcd and kube-apiserver record restart events and uptime duration. After configuration changes that affect the cluster control plane, operators can examine these health indicators to confirm that cluster infrastructure components remain continuously operational and that changes have not disrupted core control functionality.

For resilience control verification, VKS cluster management supports backup and restore of entire clusters, selected namespaces, or specific resources identified by a label, managed through the VCF Automation Tenant Portal under Manage & Govern > VCF Services > Kubernetes Management. The Velero Plugin for vSphere supports both stateless and stateful applications and allows restore operations to be initiated from the Data Protection tab, including restoring a backup from a source cluster to a different target cluster. VKS cluster node VMs are backed up and restored through Supervisor state backup using the vCenter backup feature. After changes to backup or storage configuration, performing a test restore from a recent backup confirms that the resilience control is functioning correctly.

Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) for Supervisor and VMware Kubernetes Service release nodes (VKrs) are available to guide hardening verification for the Kubernetes platform layer, providing a reference baseline against which post-change configuration compliance can be assessed.

These capabilities provide the technical building blocks for post-change control verification across the full VCF platform: configuration drift detection, compliance benchmarking, audit logging of security policy changes, upgrade prechecks, automation policy review, Pod Security Admission audit modes, admission policy verification, Kubernetes RBAC inspection, and VKS backup restore testing. However, they do not by themselves constitute a complete verification program. Organizations must establish procedures that define when functional verification is required, what controls to test after specific types of changes, how to perform functional testing of whether security controls are actively blocking, detecting, or enforcing as intended, and how to document verification results.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend and the Security Services Platform (SSP) provide several mechanisms that help organizations verify that network security controls continue to operate as intended after changes to firewall policies, segmentation rules, and threat protection configurations.

The Security Services Platform's Security Intelligence feature includes a Monitoring capability that checks for changes in the scope of input entities every hour when enabled. When a change is detected, affected policy recommendations are flagged with a "change-detected" status indicator. Administrators can review the flagged recommendations through the Policy Recommendations interface, accessible via Monitor & Plan, and rerun the analysis to confirm that segmentation policies and firewall rules remain consistent with the intended security posture after an infrastructure or policy change.

The Rule Analysis feature on the Security Services Platform provides a structured mechanism for verifying the efficiency and correctness of Distributed Firewall rules. When activated, Rule Analysis evaluates rule configurations and generates reports identifying redundant, shadowed, or misconfigured rules. After changes are made to firewall policies, running Rule Analysis helps confirm that the updated rule set operates as intended and that no unintended effects have been introduced. The feature's operational status is visible in the Security Services Platform UI at System > Platform & Features by checking the Rule Analysis feature card Status field.

The Security Services Platform extracts all IDS policies, rules, and security profiles to support multiple remediations, clean up stale policies, and suggest policies that do not conflict with one another. This supports a verification workflow where policy consistency across IDS and threat prevention configurations can be reviewed following changes. Before republishing policies, administrators can also review infrastructure and environment rules through the Review & Add Rules interface for the SSP-Infrastructure policy, confirming that rules are free of error conditions before propagating changes to the enforcement layer.

The Security Services Platform System > Overview page displays the current platform status, allowing administrators to assess stability before and after configuration changes. Feature-specific status indicators are available at System > Platform & Features, where the Rule Analysis and Malware Prevention feature cards each display a current operational status that administrators can check following updates or changes. The platform's deployment wizard also conducts validation checks before implementing configuration changes, reducing the risk that a change introduces a misconfiguration that could silently degrade security control effectiveness.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides several mechanisms that help verify data protection controls remain functional after changes to the environment.

Test recovery operations are the primary verification mechanism. Administrators can run test recoveries at any time to confirm that recovery plans execute correctly without affecting production workloads. Each test exercises the full recovery workflow, including virtual machine startup, network reconfiguration, and any custom steps defined in the plan. Recovery plans transition through defined states during testing, moving to a Test Complete state when the test finishes or a Test Interrupted state when a failure occurs during execution. This state tracking provides immediate visibility into whether data protection controls are operating as designed following any change.

Recovery plan history reports capture the result and timestamps for each plan execution and for individual steps within the plan. These records can be exported for documentation and compliance purposes, giving change management teams evidence that data protection controls were exercised and verified after changes. PNR explicitly requires that protection groups and recovery plans be verified following upgrades to the Protection and Recovery Appliance, and that connections between protected and recovery sites be validated for correctness after any upgrade.

Protection group state monitoring surfaces configuration problems that result from environmental changes. Protection and Recovery protection groups monitor the status of virtual machines and determine which operations are allowed in each protection state. Groups transition through well-defined states including Configuring Protection, Removing Protection, Restoring Placeholders, and Operations in Progress. When protection is disrupted by a change, the affected group transitions to a state that restricts test recovery and planned migration operations, surfacing the problem before a recovery attempt is made.

Alarms provide proactive notification when changes affect data protection controls. Alarms are generated when connectivity problems occur or when protection groups and recovery plans enter an error state, allowing administrators to detect and remediate issues promptly.

PNR introduces an Advanced Cyber Compliance (ACC) subscription that extends verification capabilities. With ACC enabled, Cyber Recovery can install security sensors on virtual machines during the validation phase to perform vulnerability analysis, behavioral analysis, and malware signature scanning. This adds an active security verification layer alongside the existing snapshot integrity and replication status checks. The ACC subscription also enables use of the Latest instances option in recovery plans, providing additional recovery point selection capabilities for verification testing.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

Avi Load Balancer provides several mechanisms that support verification of security control configurations following changes. The Avi Controller CLI includes the `show networksecuritypolicy` command, which allows administrators to inspect the current state of Network Security Policies and confirm that changes have taken effect as intended. At the virtual service level, Avi WAF Policies attach a Positive Security group that applies learned application data and defines hit and miss actions, giving administrators a direct view of the rules actively enforced after a WAF policy modification. The `learned-data-promotion-location` setting controls which learned application data is promoted to Positive Security rules, allowing administrators to validate that the correct data has been incorporated following a change.

For correlating configuration changes with observed effects, the Service Engine Analytics Config Events overlay lets administrators identify whether a shift in traffic behavior was caused by a specific configuration modification, such as an update to virtual service security settings. This provides a post-change verification path that links configuration actions to traffic outcomes in a single view. Individual Layer 4 Policy Set rules provide a mechanism to control the behavior of Layer 4 virtual services by evaluating Layer 3 and Layer 4 attributes, allowing targeted policy modifications to be assessed in isolation.

ControlScripts are Python-based scripts that execute on Avi Load Balancer Controllers and are initiated by Alert Actions triggered by system events. They can alter Avi Load Balancer configuration or communicate with remote systems such as email, syslog, and SNMP servers, enabling automated post-change health checks and notification workflows. Alert Actions can also trigger ControlScripts to automatically add offending client IP addresses to a blocklist attached to a Network Security Policy, providing a feedback loop between detection and enforced policy state. The Application Security Report generates a downloadable PDF documenting security findings and pre-check results, supporting structured post-change documentation.

For deployments integrated with VCF, the Avi Load Balancer for VCF documentation specifies that Avi Management Plane alarms, alerts, and dashboards must be operational as part of post-upgrade validation, establishing explicit criteria that administrators can check following software changes.

The control's question asks whether the organization verifies control functionality as a deliberate process. Avi's observability features, CLI inspection tools, Config Events overlay, and ControlScript automation support that activity by making control state visible and queryable after a change. The organization must define acceptance criteria, expected baselines, and approval workflows. Avi does not automate the organizational verification decision itself.
