The Configuration Drift dashboard allows administrators to view and compare drifts and identify settings that have changed, while the Detect Drift function detects drift across all configuration templates and displays results in the Drifts table. This change-detection capability supports identification of unauthorized modifications to managed infrastructure.

VCF Operations provides a layered alerting framework. Log-based alerts can be configured through the Symptom Definitions card in Infrastructure Operations, and alert definitions can be created to monitor infrastructure events and conditions. The system correlates a subset of events published by monitored systems and delivers them as faults, while fault symptoms can be defined based on those events and added to alert definitions. This allows administrators to build detection logic around events that may indicate compromise.

VCF Operations collects security properties for virtual machines, including the connection state of CD-ROM, USB controller, parallel port, and serial port devices, which supports detection of unauthorized device connections to virtual infrastructure. Administrators can filter virtual machines by encryption status, vCenter name, host name, and VM name to assess compliance posture across the environment.

Audit event reporting in VCF Operations records configuration changes, user actions, and authentication events across the managed infrastructure. These audit events can be filtered by authentication status, user, vCenter instance, object type, and event description, giving investigators a detailed view of activity across the environment for conducting security forensics.

VCF Operations includes a Forensics widget that allows administrators to compare data across two time periods, search for and filter objects within the environment, and display widget data based on filtered object sets. This capability supports forensic comparison and analysis when investigating potential indicators of compromise. Guided Network Troubleshooting highlights anomalous entities and metrics within the network path, aiding root-cause analysis during incident investigation.

OS and Application Monitoring Properties are collected for operating systems, application services, remote checks, Linux processes, and Windows services. These monitoring data points can be used to create reports, views, and dashboards for ongoing system inspection, providing visibility into the operational state of managed components and flagging deviations from expected behavior.

VMware ESX hosts generate detailed audit logs, and multiple VCF components support syslog forwarding to external log aggregation and SIEM systems. VMware vCenter provides both API and UI methods to configure syslog forwarding. vSphere Supervisor can forward control plane and Kubernetes API server audit logs to external syslog servers via Fluent Bit in RFC 5424 format. These log forwarding capabilities allow VCF telemetry to feed into external IoC detection and correlation tools. ESX also enforces integrity checks on installed VIBs through the Host Image Profile Acceptance Level, governing what software is permitted to run on the host and supporting detection of unauthorized additions to the hypervisor. vSphere provides preconfigured alarms that monitor the status of host hardware system boards, allowing notification when hardware-level anomalies are detected.

The NSX networking layer includes monitoring dashboards showing details about system health, networking, security, and compliance reporting. NSX supports logging and auditing capabilities that can be integrated with external logging and monitoring solutions to monitor for security events and anomalies. The System Health Agent on NSX components monitors component statistics, analyzes them, alerts on abnormalities, and can trigger remediation actions when specific conditions are met. VCF Operations for Networks monitors network flows and can detect outlier VMs based on flow metrics including bytes, packets, sessions, and traffic rate, supporting detection of anomalous traffic patterns within the environment. At the host layer, ESX measures boot components against a Trusted Platform Module (TPM) 2.0 hardware root of trust, and VMware vCenter exposes the resulting boot measurements and TPM event logs through its trusted-infrastructure API, so administrators can confirm a host booted in a known-good state and detect firmware or boot-level tampering. Secure Boot independently validates the cryptographic signatures of the ESX kernel and modules at startup, so unsigned or altered components are surfaced as an integrity failure rather than loaded silently.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer contributes to this control through its application-layer threat detection and event logging capabilities, which provide the data security teams need during periodic Indicators of Compromise (IoC) inspections.

Avi's Web Application Firewall (WAF) analyzes all incoming and outgoing application traffic using signature-based detection, a positive security model, client reputation scoring, machine learning, and outlier analysis. WAF signatures include detection for OWASP Top 10 attack vectors and other threat categories. The WAF operates in either Detection mode or Enforcement mode; Detection mode allows new rules to be evaluated without blocking legitimate traffic, making it suitable for baseline establishment and traffic anomaly review during periodic inspections. Partial detection allows individual rules to be placed in Enforcement mode while the policy as a whole remains in Detection mode, supporting incremental adoption.

The Avi Controller generates structured security events that auditors and security teams can review as part of periodic IoC inspections. The Controller tracks denial-of-service attack events with attack count metrics and generates alerts at configurable severity levels. The scan detection system dynamically classifies client IPs into Good, Bad, and Unknown groups based on observed traffic patterns, adjusting automatically as patterns change. The Controller also logs the initiator and target of failed trusted channel establishment attempts, including cases where an SSL certificate is absent, providing visibility into authentication anomalies.

Avi events serve as an audit trail for user logins, configuration changes, and runtime state changes, and can be forwarded to Splunk for centralized audit trail analysis. Client request logs can be collected automatically based on categories of significance for application-level review. System events provide a historical record of faults within the system. DataScripts support custom identification of suspicious traffic and execution of appropriate mitigation actions, extending detection coverage beyond built-in signatures.

Avi provides several tools suited for periodic review workflows. The application security report can be generated on demand through the Controller UI or CLI, with an optional duration parameter to define the report period, producing a point-in-time record of detected threats suitable for scheduled IoC review cycles. The Config Audit Trail, accessible at Operations > Events > Config Audit Trail, presents a filtered view of user activity and configuration change events over a selected period, supporting review of whether configuration changes preceded observed anomalies. Alert events can be streamed to external syslog servers using Syslog Audit Persistence, enabling integration with SIEM platforms that support automated IoC correlation. The Controller also generates system reports for upgrade operations containing pre-check results and operational status, which support verification of system state before and after changes.

The organization is responsible for defining the inspection cadence, determining what constitutes a compromise indicator in context, and performing the host-level and physical inspection that Avi does not address as an application delivery controller. Platform-level system integrity inspection relies on the underlying VCF platform.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control HDR 3.11(1)

> Host integrity verification: Verify host hardware/software integrity.  Host hardware root of trust: Use hardware to anchor host integrity.

**SCF Controls:** CFG-06.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides automated mechanisms to detect unauthorized deviations from approved baselines and to remediate them, operating across three complementary layers: configuration compliance monitoring, host software state management, and preventive policy enforcement. The VCF consumption layer extends these mechanisms to Kubernetes workloads through VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), adding declarative self-healing, admission-based baseline enforcement, and workload-level audit logging.

VCF Operations includes a compliance management engine that continuously monitors infrastructure objects against defined baselines. The scope of monitoring covers VMware vCenter instances, vSAN, NSX, and cloud environments, with monitored objects including hosts, virtual machines, distributed port groups, and distributed switches. VCF Operations supports both built-in and custom compliance benchmarks, including VMware SDDC, VCF, regulatory, and custom benchmarks. Administrators can create custom benchmarks tailored to organizational requirements and edit alert definitions and policies at any time after creation. When VCF Operations detects that an object deviates from its compliance policy, it generates alerts and notifications. Depending on the severity of the non-compliance, administrators can configure automated remediation actions to bring the object back into compliance. For violations that require manual review, the compliance engine highlights risk areas and provides actionable remediation recommendations.

VCF Operations also includes a Configuration Drifts feature that proactively monitors configuration changes across vCenter instances and vSphere clusters. Administrators define desired values through configuration templates, and VCF Operations compares the current configuration of each managed object against those templates on a scheduled basis. When a deviation is detected, the system generates a drift notification identifying the specific settings that changed. The Configuration Drifts Overview page lets administrators view and compare drifts, and drift status reports can be downloaded for review and record-keeping. Configuration templates can be integrated with a source control repository for versioning, auditing, and change management, enabling accurate configuration updates and supporting change control with reviews.

For host software state, vSphere Lifecycle Manager takes a desired-state approach. Administrators define a desired software specification, including base image, components, and add-ons, for each cluster or standalone host through Image Manager. The system checks host compliance against this specification and, when the current state differs from the desired state, cluster remediation applies the desired specification to bring all hosts into alignment. Pre-check validation runs before remediation to confirm hosts are in a good state to be updated. Global and per-cluster remediation policies govern how the remediation process behaves. At the individual host level, VMware ESX also enforces software integrity through the Host Image Profile Acceptance Level, which governs the integrity validation requirements for VIBs (VMware Installation Bundles) before they can be installed on a host, providing a preventive control that complements Lifecycle Manager's desired-state remediation.

VCF Automation extends baseline enforcement to provisioned infrastructure through Policy as Code, which lets organization administrators enforce instance-centric policies on Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) resources from a central location. IaaS Resource Policies support validation expressions that enforce constraints on resource creation and updates, such as requiring specific parameter values during provisioning. This preventive enforcement complements VCF Operations' detective and corrective controls by blocking non-compliant resource configurations before they are deployed.

At the Kubernetes workload layer, VKS clusters operate on a declarative state model where desired workload configurations are persisted as Kubernetes objects. The Kubernetes control plane continuously reconciles actual cluster state against the declared desired state: when a running pod crashes, is deleted, or is evicted from a node, the relevant controller automatically reschedules it to restore the declared replica count. Deployments maintain revision history and support rollback when a new rollout fails readiness or liveness checks. Kubernetes liveness probes provide a further container-level detection mechanism: when a container's health check fails, Kubernetes automatically restarts that container to restore correct application state. At the node level, VKS MachineHealthCheck objects specify the conditions under which a cluster node is considered unhealthy, including condition types such as MemoryPressure and DiskPressure with configurable timeout durations. When a node meets those conditions, the Cluster API triggers automated machine replacement to restore the declared cluster state. MachineHealthCheck is configurable for both control plane and worker node pools and can be updated at runtime. This continuous reconciliation provides automated resiliency at the workload layer, complementing the infrastructure-level remediation provided by VCF Operations and vSphere Lifecycle Manager.

VKS clusters support Kubernetes ValidatingAdmissionPolicy objects, which define CEL-based (Common Expression Language) rules evaluated against each API request before it is persisted. The validationActions field on a ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding specifies how violations are handled: Audit records the violation in the Kubernetes audit log, Warn returns a warning to the requesting client, and Deny rejects the request outright. Policy failures are recorded in audit events with a validation.policy.admission.k8s.io/validation_failure annotation, enabling automated monitoring pipelines to detect unauthorized changes. The Pod Security Admission controller provides enforce, audit, and warn modes for pod workload baselines, configurable per namespace or VKS cluster through the ClusterClass podSecurityStandard field. In enforce mode, pod submissions that violate the configured profile are rejected at admission; in audit mode, violations are recorded without blocking the pod. VCF Automation mutation policies with the pod-security template extend this further by automatically modifying pods to meet security standards before admission, providing active remediation rather than only rejection. The VKS Policy Insights page surfaces incompatibility detections, including pre-existing Gatekeeper installations not deployed via cluster management, flagging configuration deviations for administrative review.

For audit trail purposes, VCF Operations tracks each configuration change to the authenticated user who initiated it or scheduled the job that performed the change, providing accountability for changes that may cause compliance deviations. NSX Manager audit logs capture both old and new values for modified security policies, recording rule changes including operation status, timestamps, and user information. VCF Operations also provides the isolation.device.edit.disable setting to restrict unauthorized modification of device configurations on managed objects. At the Kubernetes layer, VKS audit logging records all API server activity with authorization.k8s.io/decision and authorization.k8s.io/reason annotations that capture authorization outcomes and their justification, providing a chronological record of all actions across the VKS cluster that can feed automated monitoring pipelines for drift detection.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend (vDefend) contributes to integrity assurance at the network policy and security detection layers but does not perform host-level configuration baseline deviation detection or automated remediation of endpoint settings.

Security Intelligence, part of the Security Services Platform (SSP), continuously monitors network traffic flows and alerts administrators to new or unexpected flows between environments, enabling detection of deviations from established microsegmentation baselines. Security Intelligence evaluates whether security rules have been implemented and generates a security score to track policy enforcement progress, helping administrators identify gaps between the desired baseline and current policy state.

Within the IDS/IPS subsystem, vDefend tracks user modifications to system signatures, allowing administrators to filter and view only those signatures that have been customized or altered from their default state. Global Signature Management records signatures that have been changed or excluded by users. When deviations from the signature baseline need to be corrected, the bulk edit feature supports a Reset action that restores signatures to their default configuration, providing a resiliency action within the security detection layer.

For malware-related events, Intelligent Assist provides automated resiliency. Targeted remediation enables all IDS signatures for the affected workload; Comprehensive remediation automatically creates DFW policies to restrict access for affected systems. Applied remediation policies can be reviewed under Security > IDS/IPS & Malware Prevention > Emergency Threat Rules. Administrators can also implement lockdown protection rules to restrict traffic leakage between environment pairs as an additional resiliency action.

Automated remediation of unauthorized changes to system configurations or OS-level settings is outside vDefend's scope.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control HDR 3.12

> Host provisioning: Securely provision host unique identifiers.

**SCF Controls:** AST-18

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF contributes to roots of trust protection through cryptographic key management, certificate trust chain infrastructure, hardware-based integrity verification, and software package signing across its component stack.

VCF manages cryptographic keys through integration with external Key Management Servers (KMS) that support the Key Management Interoperability Protocol (KMIP) 1.1 standard, or through the built-in vSphere Native Key Provider. VMware vCenter communicates with external KMS instances using the KMIP protocol, and the KMS must be capable of acting as a symmetric key foundry and server. The vSphere Native Key Provider can coexist with an existing key server infrastructure, giving organizations flexibility in how they provision and manage encryption keys. Trust between vCenter and a KMS can be established through multiple methods: uploading a KMS certificate directly, using the vCenter Root CA certificate, providing an uploaded certificate and private key, or generating a Certificate Signing Request for the KMS to sign. The vSphere Web Services API exposes an establishTrust() method that enables mutual trust between vCenter and a KMS server using self-signed certificates, signed certificate signing requests, or certificate and key pair exchange. For stronger security, administrators should upload a leaf certificate or intermediate CA certificate rather than a root CA certificate, since uploading a root CA causes vCenter to trust all certificates signed by that authority.

vCenter maintains a trusted root certificate store where administrators can add root certificates or certificate chains from enterprise or third-party certificate authorities. Adding a root certificate to this store establishes trust with that CA for all subsequent certificate validation across the vCenter environment. The TrustedRootChains API provides programmatic access to manage and retrieve root certificate chains by identifier, supporting automation of trust chain verification. The underlying certificate storage uses the VMware Endpoint Certificate Store (VECS), which maintains named stores including TRUSTED_ROOTS for all trusted root certificates and TRUSTED_ROOTS_CRLS for certificate revocation list data; administrators can manage these stores with the vecs-cli command-line tool. The TRUSTED_ROOTS_CRLS store must not be deleted or modified, as doing so can damage the certificate infrastructure. Control plane VMs in vSphere Supervisor environments rely on a Trusted Roots bundle that must be kept current with the latest vCenter root certificates.

At the hardware level, VCF supports Secure Boot and Trusted Platform Module (TPM) to help verify that hosts boot in known-good configurations. The vSphere Security Configuration Guide recommends enabling Secure Boot and TPM as part of VMware ESX host hardening. vCenter provides API interfaces for retrieving TPM event logs for configuring firmware trust with an attestation service or validating boot-time TPM measurements, and for listing configured TPM endorsement keys on a host. These capabilities support establishing a hardware-rooted chain of trust from firmware through the hypervisor.

Beyond core key and certificate management, several VCF components maintain their own trust infrastructure. VCF Operations orchestrator supports the import, export, and generation of package signing certificates, providing a mechanism for verifying the integrity and authenticity of software packages deployed within the environment. VCF Operations also maintains an SSL truststore with a password-protected keystore for managing certificates used in secure communications. VCF Automation can establish trust relationships with external servers by verifying and accepting certificate information for SSL communication, and it exchanges SAML service provider metadata with external identity providers to establish federated trust relationships. NSX components establish trust through certificate exchange between Local Manager and Global Manager instances during federation setup. VCF PowerCLI maintains its own Trusted Certificate Store where trusted certificates are saved and used when connecting to server systems, extending the trust chain to administrative automation tooling.

VCF provides the cryptographic infrastructure for establishing, storing, and verifying trust relationships through certificates and keys across all its major components. However, the organizational processes for provisioning supplier keys, verifying supply chain integrity of cryptographic material, defining key protection policies, and physically protecting key storage remain the responsibility of the deploying organization.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control HDR 3.13(a)

> Host boot integrity: Verify integrity of the host OS bootloader.  Host boot authenticity: Verify authenticity of the host OS bootloader.

**SCF Controls:** AST-18

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF contributes to roots of trust protection through cryptographic key management, certificate trust chain infrastructure, hardware-based integrity verification, and software package signing across its component stack.

VCF manages cryptographic keys through integration with external Key Management Servers (KMS) that support the Key Management Interoperability Protocol (KMIP) 1.1 standard, or through the built-in vSphere Native Key Provider. VMware vCenter communicates with external KMS instances using the KMIP protocol, and the KMS must be capable of acting as a symmetric key foundry and server. The vSphere Native Key Provider can coexist with an existing key server infrastructure, giving organizations flexibility in how they provision and manage encryption keys. Trust between vCenter and a KMS can be established through multiple methods: uploading a KMS certificate directly, using the vCenter Root CA certificate, providing an uploaded certificate and private key, or generating a Certificate Signing Request for the KMS to sign. The vSphere Web Services API exposes an establishTrust() method that enables mutual trust between vCenter and a KMS server using self-signed certificates, signed certificate signing requests, or certificate and key pair exchange. For stronger security, administrators should upload a leaf certificate or intermediate CA certificate rather than a root CA certificate, since uploading a root CA causes vCenter to trust all certificates signed by that authority.

vCenter maintains a trusted root certificate store where administrators can add root certificates or certificate chains from enterprise or third-party certificate authorities. Adding a root certificate to this store establishes trust with that CA for all subsequent certificate validation across the vCenter environment. The TrustedRootChains API provides programmatic access to manage and retrieve root certificate chains by identifier, supporting automation of trust chain verification. The underlying certificate storage uses the VMware Endpoint Certificate Store (VECS), which maintains named stores including TRUSTED_ROOTS for all trusted root certificates and TRUSTED_ROOTS_CRLS for certificate revocation list data; administrators can manage these stores with the vecs-cli command-line tool. The TRUSTED_ROOTS_CRLS store must not be deleted or modified, as doing so can damage the certificate infrastructure. Control plane VMs in vSphere Supervisor environments rely on a Trusted Roots bundle that must be kept current with the latest vCenter root certificates.

At the hardware level, VCF supports Secure Boot and Trusted Platform Module (TPM) to help verify that hosts boot in known-good configurations. The vSphere Security Configuration Guide recommends enabling Secure Boot and TPM as part of VMware ESX host hardening. vCenter provides API interfaces for retrieving TPM event logs for configuring firmware trust with an attestation service or validating boot-time TPM measurements, and for listing configured TPM endorsement keys on a host. These capabilities support establishing a hardware-rooted chain of trust from firmware through the hypervisor.

Beyond core key and certificate management, several VCF components maintain their own trust infrastructure. VCF Operations orchestrator supports the import, export, and generation of package signing certificates, providing a mechanism for verifying the integrity and authenticity of software packages deployed within the environment. VCF Operations also maintains an SSL truststore with a password-protected keystore for managing certificates used in secure communications. VCF Automation can establish trust relationships with external servers by verifying and accepting certificate information for SSL communication, and it exchanges SAML service provider metadata with external identity providers to establish federated trust relationships. NSX components establish trust through certificate exchange between Local Manager and Global Manager instances during federation setup. VCF PowerCLI maintains its own Trusted Certificate Store where trusted certificates are saved and used when connecting to server systems, extending the trust chain to administrative automation tooling.

VCF provides the cryptographic infrastructure for establishing, storing, and verifying trust relationships through certificates and keys across all its major components. However, the organizational processes for provisioning supplier keys, verifying supply chain integrity of cryptographic material, defining key protection policies, and physically protecting key storage remain the responsibility of the deploying organization.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control HDR 3.13(b)

> Host boot integrity: Verify integrity of the host OS bootloader.  Host boot authenticity: Verify authenticity of the host OS bootloader.

**SCF Controls:** CFG-07

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several automated provisioning and configuration capabilities that contribute to Zero-Touch Provisioning (ZTP) objectives, though full ZTP for arbitrary network devices requires additional organizational processes and tooling beyond VCF itself.

VCF Operations automates the commissioning of VMware ESX hosts into the VCF environment. When new hosts are added, administrators specify host details and select a network pool; the platform validates each host and commissions it into the free pool for use with VCF domains and clusters. Network pools automatically assign static IP addresses to vSAN, NFS, iSCSI, and vMotion VMkernel ports when hosts are added to a domain or cluster, removing the need for manual network configuration of each host interface. This commissioning workflow applies a consistent, repeatable configuration to each host as it joins the environment. SDK samples are available for programmatic host commissioning, cluster creation, and domain creation, enabling further automation of the onboarding process.

Once hosts are commissioned, vSphere Lifecycle Manager enforces a desired-state software model. Administrators define an approved software image specification (base image, components, vendor add-ons) for each cluster, and Lifecycle Manager applies that specification to all hosts collectively. When a new host joins a cluster, it is checked against the desired state and remediated to match if it differs. This approach means newly added hosts automatically receive the correct software configuration without individual manual setup.

vSphere also supports Auto Deploy, a PXE-based provisioning mechanism that allows ESX hosts to boot from the network and receive their complete software image automatically from a central deployment server without manual intervention. Because the deployment server delivers the boot image over the network, Auto Deploy requires a secure separation between the production network and the management or deployment network; custom certificates are supported to help secure the provisioning process.

VCF Automation extends automated provisioning through cloud templates and network profiles. Administrators can define cloud-specific network profiles that govern the behavior of network provisioning for deployments, specifying how networks are assigned and configured when new workloads or infrastructure components are instantiated. On-demand network provisioning is supported through external IPAM integrations, allowing new networks to be created automatically in the external IPAM system as part of the deployment process. Cloud template properties allow administrators to define storage provisioning types and other infrastructure settings declaratively, so that deployed resources receive their configuration from a template rather than through manual steps. Administrators can test template syntax and placement before release, then publish versioned templates to the self-service catalog for repeatable, policy-governed provisioning.

Policy as Code lets organization administrators enforce constraints on provisioned IaaS resources centrally, so deployments automatically comply with organizational standards without manual review of each provisioning request.

VCF Automation also supports onboarding existing unmanaged virtual machines into managed deployments through its API, bringing them under the same lifecycle and policy governance as newly provisioned resources. Day 2 customizations for VM Apps support network configuration changes similar to initial deployment, extending the automated configuration model beyond initial provisioning into the operational lifecycle.

At the Kubernetes consumption layer, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) provides declarative, automated cluster provisioning that follows a similar zero-touch model. When a platform administrator creates a VKS cluster using the ClusterClass API, the cluster is automatically provisioned with pre-defined configurations covering the Kubernetes version, node count, resource limits, CNI plugin, storage classes, and security settings without requiring manual node-by-node configuration. The TkgServiceConfiguration object allows administrators to define cluster-wide defaults for the container network interface, global proxy server settings, and trusted TLS certificates; these settings apply automatically to all VKS clusters created within the Supervisor namespace, so newly provisioned clusters receive a consistent, pre-approved configuration from the moment they are created.

When an external identity provider is registered with a Supervisor, all VKS clusters subsequently created on that Supervisor are automatically configured with that identity provider through the Pinnipid authentication service. VKS runs the Pinnipid OIDC client on both the Supervisor and VKS cluster nodes, so configuring an external OIDC provider for the Supervisor automatically configures the Pinnipid components across all clusters without per-cluster administrator action. Node-level configuration is handled through ClusterClass variables and the osConfiguration parameter; the osConfiguration trust setting applies system-wide certificate trust automatically to all nodes in a cluster, supporting secure configuration of nodes as they join. Pod Security Admission, with Baseline and Restricted profiles, automatically applies security policies to workloads as they are created, so newly deployed containers receive consistent security constraints without per-deployment administrator intervention. Validating and mutating webhooks can extend this enforcement to apply organization-specific configuration policies automatically at admission time.

VCF Operations provides network auto-discovery capabilities that support SSH, SNMP v2, SNMPv3, and API-based access credential types for discovering devices on the network. This automated discovery helps identify and catalog new devices as they appear, which is a foundational step in any ZTP workflow.

These capabilities collectively provide an automated, template-driven provisioning model for infrastructure and workload components within the VCF environment. For physical network equipment such as switches, routers, and access points, organizations will need vendor-specific ZTP frameworks or external orchestration tools that operate outside VCF's scope. Organizations should pair VCF's provisioning automation with network device management platforms and configuration management tools to achieve a full ZTP strategy across the infrastructure stack.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to zero-touch provisioning by automating the application of network security policies to devices as they are onboarded to the infrastructure, though it does not provide a standalone ZTP solution covering initial device bootstrapping.

For physical workloads, vDefend Bare Metal Security provides an automated onboarding workflow. Administrators generate a registration token through the Security Services Platform, install the BMS installer service, and deploy Bare Metal Agents on target servers. Once deployed, the Bare Metal Agents receive security policy configurations from the Bare Metal Orchestrator (BMO) and Bare Metal Controller (BMC), translate those policies, and program the data path. Onboarded bare metal servers and their interfaces are integrated with NSX Manager for centralized management and visibility. When bare metal servers are onboarded, the Security Services Platform automatically creates a default Bare Metal server group and default DFW rules, providing an immediate security policy baseline without per-server manual configuration.

For virtualized workloads, vDefend policy groups enable automated security orchestration by moving away from static, manual IP management. Security policies update automatically as workloads are provisioned, moved, or modified, so newly placed virtual machines receive their intended security posture without requiring per-workload firewall rule configuration.

The Security Services Platform is deployed as an OVA appliance, and the SSP Installer supports adding control plane and worker nodes to scale the security management infrastructure as environments grow.

In 9.1, vDefend Distributed IDS/IPS supports automatic signature downloads as a configurable option, so intrusion detection and prevention profiles can be maintained without manual intervention as new workloads join protected segments. The Network Detection and Response (NDR) Sensor follows the same token-based onboarding model used by Bare Metal Security, registering to the Security Services Platform through a two-step process: generating a registration token in the Security Services Platform UI and completing registration via CLI.

The broader aspects of ZTP, such as initial operating system installation, base network configuration, and hardware initialization, fall outside vDefend's scope and are handled by other VCF components or vendor-specific provisioning frameworks.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) contributes to Zero-Touch Provisioning through automatic Service Engine lifecycle management and cloud integration automation. The Avi Controller automatically provisions and integrates Service Engine virtual machines as load balancing capacity is required, deploying them into the network without per-instance manual configuration steps. Service Engines can be rapidly provisioned on virtual infrastructure and de-provisioned when no longer needed, with the Controller managing placement, network attachment, and policy application.

For NSX-integrated deployments, the NSX Cloud Quick Start Wizard provides a guided configuration walkthrough for establishing a basic NSX Cloud environment using NSX Overlay networking, reducing the manual steps required to integrate Avi with the underlying NSX networking fabric. This wizard is accessible from the Avi Controller interface and completes configuration with a minimal set of inputs.

When an NSX cloud connector is configured, the Avi Controller can automatically generate vDefend objects using the `automate_dfw_objects` setting. As virtual services are provisioned through the Controller, their corresponding network security objects can be created automatically without requiring separate manual firewall configuration steps in vDefend.

Avi also supports automated certificate lifecycle management through integration with Venafi Trust Protection Platform. The Avi Controller Onboard Discovery feature automates the import of certificates from network devices into Venafi for monitoring, validation, and provisioning. Once discovered, the Controller can generate corresponding certificate and application objects in Venafi, enabling certificate provisioning to virtual services immediately after discovery.

Provisioning of infrastructure hosts, network switches, and other platform components outside the application delivery tier is managed by VCF platform capabilities. Organizations deploying Avi within VCF can combine Avi's Service Engine and virtual service automation with VCF platform provisioning workflows to address the full scope of this control.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control HDR 3.14

> Host data at rest integrity: Protect host file system integrity.  Host storage encryption: Use cryptography for host data at rest.

**SCF Controls:** END-06.5

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides automated mechanisms to verify the integrity of the boot process for both physical hosts and virtual machines, spanning hardware-rooted trust, software specification validation, and post-boot health verification.

ESX hosts support UEFI Secure Boot, which uses cryptographic signature verification to validate each component loaded during the boot sequence. When Secure Boot is enabled, the host firmware checks the digital signature of the bootloader, and each subsequent stage verifies the next, creating a chain of trust from firmware through the hypervisor kernel. The vSphere Security Configuration Guide recommends enabling Secure Boot on all ESX hosts as part of standard hardening. ESX also supports an execInstalledOnly enforcement mode, built on top of UEFI Secure Boot, which restricts execution to software installed through the managed package process and prevents unauthorized binaries from running on the host.

VCF also supports Trusted Platform Module (TPM) integration on ESX hosts. TPM devices perform measured boot, recording cryptographic hashes of each boot component into platform configuration registers. VMware vCenter provides programmatic access to TPM device information through the trusted infrastructure API, including retrieval of TPM event logs that record boot-time measurements. These event logs can be used with an attestation service to validate that hosts booted with known-good firmware and software configurations. The Tpm interface from the trusted infrastructure package allows administrators to list TPM devices on a host and retrieve detailed information including manufacturer, model, and firmware version. vCenter also supports unsealing secrets bound to an endorsement key to verify reported measurements from the TPM event log, providing a second verification path for boot integrity. ESX host TPM modules must run the latest 2.0 firmware when in use, and must be disabled in the BIOS if not in use, keeping the hardware trust baseline consistent and verifiable.

Remote attestation capabilities allow vCenter to verify host boot integrity by comparing TPM measurements against expected values, providing an automated mechanism to detect tampering or unexpected changes in the boot process of managed hosts.

For virtual machines, VCF supports UEFI Secure Boot at the VM level, allowing guest operating systems to verify boot component integrity through the same cryptographic chain-of-trust mechanism used on physical hardware. VM boot firmware can be configured to EFI type using the vSphere Automation SDK Boot.UpdateSpec() method, and boot options including EFI firmware type and boot behavior settings are configurable through both the vSphere Client and the vSphere Automation API. VM-level Secure Boot is supported starting with Hardware Version 13. For Windows virtual machines, vSphere also supports Configurable Code Integrity under Virtualization-Based Security (VBS), which enforces that only trusted, signed code runs from the boot loader onwards within the guest.

At the software specification level, VCF's lifecycle management includes automated validation before any software is applied to hosts. The Drafts service offers a validate_Task method that checks whether a draft software specification is complete and identifies any conflicts between draft components or unresolved dependencies. Before saving a draft and turning it into the desired state for a cluster or standalone host, the specification should be validated to confirm it is complete and valid. This automated pre-deployment validation helps ensure that hosts receive verified, conflict-free software configurations, reducing the risk of boot failures or integrity issues caused by incompatible components. VCF also supports the creation of exported ISO images from desired software specifications for use in clean installs and bootstrapping, allowing administrators to provision systems from a known, validated software baseline.

For post-boot verification, VCF provides automated checking mechanisms across its management components. The Supportability and Serviceability tool (SoS) on VCF Operations includes a health check function that verifies system state after operations such as a vCenter instance restore, confirming that all components have started correctly and are operating as expected. VCF Operations Cloud Proxy implements automated first boot verification by checking for the presence of a "Succeeded" file in /var/log/firstboot, providing a programmatic way to confirm that the boot process completed without error.

Auditors evaluating a VCF deployment for this control should verify that UEFI Secure Boot is enabled on all ESX hosts, that TPM 2.0 modules are active and running current firmware, that lifecycle management workflows use the software specification validation step before applying updates, that SoS health checks are run after restore or maintenance operations, and that boot verification logs are monitored for component deployments.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control HDR 3.14(1)

> Host data at rest integrity: Protect host file system integrity.  Host storage encryption: Use cryptography for host data at rest.

**SCF Controls:** END-06.6

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides multiple automated mechanisms that protect the integrity of boot firmware across both physical VMware ESX hosts and virtual machines.

ESX hosts support UEFI Secure Boot, which validates the cryptographic signature of each component in the boot chain before allowing it to execute. When Secure Boot is enabled, the host firmware restricts the boot process to only authorized, signed code, protecting the hypervisor from unauthorized modification. The vSphere Security Configuration Guide recommends enabling Secure Boot on all ESX hosts as a standard hardening measure, alongside Trusted Platform Module (TPM) enablement. Secure Boot enforcement can be activated or deactivated through ESXCLI, and ESX also supports execInstalledOnly enforcement, a control built on top of UEFI Secure Boot that restricts execution to only installed binaries.

TPM 2.0 integration on ESX hosts provides measured boot capabilities. The TPM records cryptographic hashes of each boot stage into hardware-protected platform configuration registers, creating a tamper-evident chain of trust from firmware through the hypervisor. If any boot component is altered, the TPM measurements will differ from expected values. The TPM event log can be retrieved for configuring firmware trust with an attestation service or for validating boot time measurements. VMware vCenter provides programmatic access to TPM device information, including manufacturer and firmware version details, through the trusted infrastructure API. TPM endorsement keys used to establish hardware identity and trust are also managed through this API. An attestation service can compare measurements against known-good values to confirm that boot firmware and subsequent components have not been modified.

For virtual machines, VCF supports the Extensible Firmware Interface (EFI) as a virtual hardware feature, enabling UEFI Secure Boot for guest operating systems. Virtual machine firmware type can be configured to EFI through the vSphere Client or programmatically through the vSphere Automation SDK using the Boot.UpdateSpec API or the BootTypes.UpdateSpec.Builder setType() method. UEFI Secure Boot at the VM level protects guest operating system boot firmware using the same signature verification approach as physical hosts. For Windows virtual machines, vSphere supports Virtualization-based Security (VBS) with Configurable Code Integrity, which restricts execution to trusted code from the boot loader onward within the guest operating system. VCF also supports hardware memory encryption for confidential workloads through technologies like SEV-ES, which protects VM state from inspection or modification at the hardware level.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager provides automated firmware lifecycle management for ESX hosts. Firmware add-ons can be added, replaced, removed, upgraded, or downgraded through vSphere Lifecycle Manager images, allowing administrators to maintain firmware at vendor-validated versions across the host fleet. vSphere Lifecycle Manager images consist of a base image, vendor add-ons, components, and firmware add-ons that can be independently retrieved and applied to hosts. This automated approach helps keep host firmware aligned with tested baselines and reduces the window during which outdated firmware is in use.

VCF Operations provides monitoring for firmware integrity through management packs. The Management Pack for Lenovo Compute generates alerts for firmware unrecoverable boot device failure events detected by UEFI, as well as firmware update failed alerts when a firmware update does not complete normally and the target image is partially written. These automated alerts allow operations teams to detect and respond to firmware integrity issues.

VMware publishes security advisories for known firmware and software vulnerabilities affecting VCF components, with remediation guidance for each advisory.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control NDR 1.6

> Network wireless access: Authenticate all users on network wireless ports.  Wireless intrusion detection: Detect unauthorized wireless access points.

**SCF Controls:** NET-15

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control NDR 1.6(1)

> Network wireless access: Authenticate all users on network wireless ports.  Wireless intrusion detection: Detect unauthorized wireless access points.

**SCF Controls:** NET-15.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control NDR 1.13

> Untrusted network access: Control network traffic from untrusted zones.  Network access control (NAC): Implement NAC for untrusted devices.

**SCF Controls:** NET-01.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several foundational capabilities that support a Zero Trust Architecture approach, though a complete ZTA implementation requires organizational policies, identity governance processes, continuous verification mechanisms, and additional tooling beyond what VCF delivers on its own.

VCF enforces authentication as a prerequisite to all access. The platform's authentication framework determines whether users are who they claim to be through credentials including smart cards, usernames, and passwords. VCF SSO integrates with the VCF Identity Broker to provide centralized authentication for VCF components, with support for identity provider federation to enterprise directories such as Active Directory Federation Services. Federating vCenter to an enterprise identity provider provides flexible options such as multi-factor authentication. OAuth 2.0 support allows information exchange among web services without exposing sensitive data such as usernames and passwords, reducing credential exposure during authentication flows. NSX network devices require authentication prior to establishing any management connection for administrative access, and all administrative accounts must be password protected. VMware ESX and vCenter systems restrict connections to authenticated users and they can access only those objects for which they have the required privileges and are authorized to use or view. Direct access to the ESX host console can be restricted to prevent administrators from overriding policy enforcement, and ESX supports configuration of users who can override lockdown mode and access the DCUI. For Windows virtual machines, vSphere Virtualization-based Security supports Credential Guard, which isolates and hardens key system and user secrets in the guest operating system against compromise. At the workload consumption layer, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters support multiple authentication mechanisms for the Kubernetes API server, including OIDC federation with VCF SSO, X.509 client certificates, service account tokens, and webhook token authentication. Namespace-level access in VKS follows the same VCF SSO identity model as the broader vSphere platform. VKS implements a zero-trust token validation posture for external integrations such as AWS IRSA, trusting tokens only when issuer, signature, audience, and subject conditions are verified. Kubernetes guidance recommends enabling as few authentication mechanisms as necessary to simplify user management and prevent retained access through unused pathways, and it recommends setting short certificate lifetimes with automated rotation to limit the window of opportunity for credential misuse.

Once authenticated, VCF applies authorization controls that restrict what each principal can do. The vCenter AuthorizationManager controls access to specific server objects based on the permissions associated with each object. Authenticated users can view objects or invoke operations on the server only when the permissions associated with their account allow it. Administrators can create, modify, and manage roles and permissions through the AuthorizationManager, enabling granular role-based access control across the vSphere environment. The CloudAdmin role restricts access to platform-level operations, and the No Cryptography Administrator system role supports all Administrator privileges except for Cryptographic Operations privileges, allowing organizations to separate encryption key management from general administration. You can clone the No Cryptography Administrator role and create custom roles with only some of the Cryptographic Operations privileges to further limit what users can do. NSX provides granular role separation within the VPC scope, where you can assign VPC Admin, Network Admin, Security Admin, Network Operator, and Security Operator roles. A VPC Admin has full access to all networking and security objects inside an NSX VPC, while other roles provide narrower access. This "authenticate first, then authorize" model aligns with core ZTA principles of never granting implicit trust. For containerized workloads, Kubernetes RBAC provides Role, ClusterRole, RoleBinding, and ClusterRoleBinding objects that define what each authenticated identity may perform at the namespace or cluster level. Least-privileged access applies even to administrators; elevated access is reserved for break-glass scenarios while standard operations use narrower roles. Authorization webhooks support fine-grained, attribute-based authorization decisions by passing field and label selector information to the webhook, enabling per-resource access control based on object attributes such as node name and labels. Dynamic Resource Allocation APIs carry elevated privilege implications and should be controlled using RBAC to restrict access based on user persona. Pod Security Admission enforces workload security profiles at the namespace level, with Baseline and Restricted profiles limiting privilege escalation, host namespace access, and container capabilities. Security contexts allow administrators to require that containers run as non-root users, drop Linux capabilities, apply seccomp profiles, or use AppArmor to restrict system call access at runtime. Cluster administrators can create custom pod security policies beyond the platform defaults and bind them to specific users, applying different trust levels to different teams or namespaces. Argo CD, available as a standard package for VKS clusters, supports local account authentication backed by global RBAC policies that define access control through policy rules and group mappings, extending authenticated and authorized access controls to the continuous delivery pipeline. Harbor, also deployable as a VKS standard package, enforces role-based access control for artifact management, requiring authentication and authorization before users can pull or push container images and charts.

Solution-to-solution communication within VCF uses certificate-based trust rather than passwords. VCF Automation establishes trust relationships with servers by verifying and trusting certificate information for SSL communication. When a client receives an element from VCF Operations, it verifies the server identity and decides whether to trust the signature before proceeding. Mutual TLS authentication based on self-signed or CA certificates is required between hosts and the NSX Central Control Plane. The VCF Identity Broker provides a centralized authentication source for VCF components, and vCenter and NSX components connect to it for SSO-based access. This certificate-based trust model means that VCF's own internal communications follow a verify-then-trust pattern rather than assuming trust based on network location. At the consumption layer, the Secret Store Supervisor Service enforces RBAC for administrators, DevOps engineers, and application tenants managing and distributing sensitive data, providing controlled access to credentials without broad exposure. Kubernetes guidance recommends using short-lived secrets to limit the impact of unintentional credential exposure, and audit rules can alert on anomalous access patterns such as concurrent reading of multiple secrets by a single user. Scoping IAM role trust policies to specific service accounts and namespaces enforces least-privilege at the credential level within VKS deployments.

VCF's networking layer provides segmentation capabilities that support ZTA network isolation. NSX projects enable multi-tenancy by isolating security and networking objects across tenants within a single NSX deployment, so that each tenant's resources are separated from others. NSX VPC enables tenant-level isolation of both networking and security objects, with each VPC operating as a self-contained boundary. Security groups can further define isolation settings for on-demand private or outbound networks. Kubernetes network policies can be imported to NSX and converted to vDefend policies, extending consistent security boundaries to containerized workloads alongside traditional VMs. The vSphere Distributed Services Engine supports zero-trust security by offloading network security processing to a dedicated data processing unit, separating security enforcement from the host operating system. These segmentation capabilities allow organizations to build network architectures where lateral movement between zones requires explicit authorization. VMware vDefend extends this foundation with microsegmentation and identity-based firewall rules that integrate with Active Directory for user-aware access enforcement at the workload level. At the Kubernetes networking layer, Cilium, a supported CNI option, enforces network policies at layers three through seven using an identity-based security model that is decoupled from network addressing, allowing policy to follow workload identity rather than IP addresses. Istio's ambient data plane mode, available as a standard package for VKS clusters, uses a per-node ztunnel component to provide mutual authentication and encrypted transport for east-west traffic within the mesh, extending verify-before-trust semantics to workload-to-workload communication. Service meshes operating at layer seven can enforce policies based on workload identity and frequently provide mutual TLS to maintain data confidentiality even in the presence of a compromised node.

VCF Operations provides visibility into platform activities through audit event collection. VCF Operations reports on authentication and security-related audit events, and each audit event has a detailed view containing information that helps analyze events in the virtual infrastructure for suspicious user actions or system issues. This supports the continuous monitoring aspect of zero trust by correlating authentication events, configuration changes, and related activity across the environment.

To realize a full Zero Trust Architecture, organizations need to supplement VCF's technical controls with an overarching ZTA policy framework that defines trust boundaries, continuous verification requirements, and least-privilege access principles across all systems. Device posture assessment, continuous session monitoring, and adaptive access policies based on risk signals typically require integration with identity governance platforms and security analytics tools beyond what VCF provides natively.

Zero Trust Architecture requires that every user and device be authenticated and authorized before accessing data or resources, with no implicit trust based on network location or prior access. VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) implements authentication and access control mechanisms across its AI services layer that support this model.

PAIS enforces access through OIDC-based group membership. The PAISConfiguration custom resource requires an issuer URL, a client ID, and scopes including openid, groups, and offline_access, with a groups claim carried in the access token. Access is restricted to user accounts that belong to authorized groups configured via the authorizedGroups setting, and users outside those groups are denied access regardless of network position. Communication with the OIDC provider is required over HTTPS, and an issuer certificate must be present for that provider. PAIS supports a single OIDC provider per configuration.

Certificate-based mutual trust is enforced across PAIS component connections. CA trust bundles are required for the OIDC provider, the Harbor registry hosting the model gallery, content management systems used as data sources in Data Indexing and Retrieval, MCP servers providing tools to models, and the pgvector database server storing vector embeddings for Knowledge Bases. Trust bundles are configured through VCF Automation by uploading a certificate file or referencing a ConfigMap in the namespace, and the worker.clientTls.caBundleRefs setting controls the CA bundle references used for client TLS connections. Separate trust bundles per application are recommended, with refreshes required before certificate expiration.

At the model and data plane, the Model Gallery uses Harbor project access controls to restrict access to sensitive model training and tuning data, and model endpoints authenticate to private registries through a harbor-pull-secret-readonly secret. Remote MCP servers connected to PAIS can use static authentication tokens, and Knowledge Base data sources such as Confluence and SharePoint require a user name and password or a personal access token configured per administrator settings. Grafana dashboards visualizing PAIS metrics also authenticate through the OIDC provider, extending OIDC-mediated access into the observability path.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend (vDefend) is a foundational technology for implementing Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) within VCF. While a full ZTA implementation also requires identity governance, organizational policy, and continuous verification processes beyond any single product, vDefend provides the network security enforcement layer that treats all east-west traffic as untrusted by default and enforces explicit authorization at the workload level.

vDefend enforces security policies at the vNIC level, meaning every virtual machine's network interface is individually firewalled regardless of its network location. Network location does not confer trust, and every communication must be explicitly permitted. The default-deny posture of Distributed Firewall rules means workloads cannot communicate unless a policy explicitly allows that traffic, reducing the risk of lateral movement by treating all internal traffic as untrusted. The Distributed Firewall Connectivity Strategy feature simplifies micro-segmentation rule creation for applications, lowering the operational barrier to deploying a Zero Trust model.

Security Services Platform provides a guided security segmentation journey that walks administrators through a staged implementation of zero trust using vDefend. This workflow moves from broad infrastructure segmentation through environment-level isolation to fine-grained application ringfencing, where policies are defined down to the individual application tier. Security Intelligence complements this journey by providing the visibility and analytics needed to understand application security policy requirements, supporting identification of gaps in the zero trust posture. Security Services Platform generates Security Segmentation Reports and Blast Radius Reports that break down at-risk workloads, communication chains, and network flows to support Zero Trust journey planning. Security Services Platform also enforces role-based access control by assigning platform roles to remote users and user groups, subjecting access to the security management plane to the same authorization principles that govern workload-level policies.

vDefend Identity Firewall extends the zero trust model beyond IP-based rules by integrating with Active Directory to create firewall rules based on user identity and group membership. Administrators can define policies that grant or restrict access based on authenticated user identity rather than IP address. Identity Firewall rules can specify source users or groups, destination resources, services, and context profiles to allow or deny traffic based on user identity. Identity Firewall also supports simultaneous logins by multiple users on the same virtual machine, with each user's application access controlled based on their role requirements. Administrators can use Identity Firewall with Remote Desktop Session Host to create Active Directory security groups that allow or deny users access to application servers based on their assigned role. Identity Firewall supports two login detection methods: Event Log Scraper (ELS), which reads Windows security event logs from domain controllers, and Guest Introspection, which captures authentication events from within the guest operating system. The choice between these methods depends on the workload type and deployment environment.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer contributes to Zero Trust Architecture by enforcing authentication and authorization at the application delivery layer before client requests reach protected workloads. Avi's SSO Policy, configured per virtual service, supports Authentication Rules and Authorization Rules that validate JSON Web Tokens, OAuth 2.0 access tokens, and OIDC claims on every incoming request. Clients that present invalid or absent credentials are denied access before any request is forwarded to the backend resource. This per-virtual-service enforcement model means each application can independently require successful authentication as a condition of access.

Auth Mapping Profiles extend this authentication model into the Avi Controller's administrative plane. These profiles map authenticated user identities, including identities sourced from LDAP, SAML, or other external directory services, to specific tenants and roles with varying permission levels. Auth Mapping rules can be configured to deny all privileges to specific users, blocking them from obtaining any role or tenant assignment on login. When remote authentication is configured, administrators can independently enable or disable local user login, supporting stricter authentication policy enforcement. A user without a matching rule in the Auth Mapping Profile receives no privileges on login. Auth Mapping Profiles should be configured to enforce tenant isolation except for designated super users who require cross-tenant access.

At the management plane, administrators can restrict the source IP address ranges permitted to reach the Avi Controller and enforce strong password requirements. User Account Lockout is implemented on the Controller as a mechanism that blocks access after repeated failed authentication attempts. User accounts can be scoped to specific tenants and granted different roles within each tenant, limiting the blast radius of a compromised credential. HTTP security policies attached to virtual services are configurable to meet the security requirements applicable to each deployed application.

In VCF-integrated deployments, the Avi PKI Profile contains the list of Trusted Authorities allowed to sign client certificates and can be used to implement Zero Trust security between the server and the client. This certificate-based mechanism supports mutual authentication at the application delivery tier, complementing token-based authentication for workloads that rely on client certificate validation rather than OAuth or OIDC flows.

Full Zero Trust Architecture across a VCF environment requires capabilities beyond what Avi provides at the application delivery tier. Network-layer microsegmentation between workloads is provided by VMware vDefend, which is a separate product. OAuth, OIDC, and SAML federation require an external identity provider. The organizational policies that govern how authentication is enforced for all users and all devices must be defined and maintained by the organization; Avi provides the enforcement point for application traffic but does not itself constitute a complete ZTA implementation.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control NDR 1.13(1)

> Untrusted network access: Control network traffic from untrusted zones.  Network access control (NAC): Implement NAC for untrusted devices.

**SCF Controls:** NET-04, NET-04.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides multiple layers of access control list (ACL) mechanisms and network traffic restriction capabilities that contribute to data flow enforcement, though full ACL governance across physical and virtual infrastructure requires organizational processes and may benefit from VMware vDefend for advanced stateful firewall rule sets.

At the network infrastructure layer, VCF uses vSphere Distributed Switches to centrally manage VLAN assignments, port group policies, and traffic shaping rules across all hosts in a workload domain. Distributed switches support traffic shaping policies that limit the bandwidth of incoming traffic, helping restrict which traffic types and volumes can traverse each network segment. Distributed port groups and uplink port groups also support a Traffic Filtering and Marking Policy, where per-rule actions can be set to Allow, Drop, or Tag traffic based on defined match criteria, providing rule-based traffic enforcement at the virtual switch level. Traffic segmentation is built into VCF's architecture: management, vMotion, vSAN, and NFS traffic each run on dedicated VLANs and VMkernel ports, and the vMotion network must be distinct from the vSAN network. This separation restricts which traffic types can traverse each segment by design. Private VLANs (PVLANs) provide additional Layer 2 isolation within a single VLAN through virtual network segmentation on distributed virtual switches, limiting communication between VMs even on the same segment. Distributed switches also support VLAN-based SPAN (VSPAN) for virtual distributed port mirroring, which aids in monitoring and validating traffic flows. At the ESX host level, a built-in firewall operates on a deny-by-default basis, closing all ports except those an administrator explicitly designates as authorized, giving each host an independent network access control point.

NSX extends these capabilities with software-defined networking constructs including segments, Tier-0 and Tier-1 gateways, and Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs). NSX Tier-0 gateways can be configured with linked Tier-1 gateways to create multi-tier topologies, enabling route-based traffic restrictions between network tiers. NSX edge gateways handle north-south routing. With vDefend deployed, North-South Firewall Rules on these gateways provide traffic protection, and an NSX project's default gateway firewall and distributed firewall rules (a vDefend capability) govern the default behavior of north-south and east-west traffic for workloads in that project. VPCs are self-contained virtual network environments that provide an abstraction layer for setting up isolated virtual private cloud networks with their own topology, IP address management, and permission models, allocatable to discrete projects in a self-service consumption model. NSX Manager enforces approved authorizations for controlling the flow of management information within network devices based on information flow control policies.

NSX also provides data flow enforcement at the load balancer tier through its Access List Control (ALC) feature. The NSX load balancer compares all traffic flowing through it with ACL statements, which either drop or allow the traffic based on configured rules. This gives administrators another enforcement point for restricting network traffic to only what is authorized.

At the Consumption layer, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and the Supervisor extend ACL enforcement to containerized workloads through Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resources, which function as Layer 3/4 ACLs for pod-level traffic. NetworkPolicy objects specify allowed ingress and egress traffic by pod selector, namespace selector, IP block, port, and protocol; pods not selected by any NetworkPolicy receive no restriction by default, making explicit policy deployment necessary to enforce a default-deny posture across a cluster. VKS clusters run Antrea as the default CNI plugin, which implements NetworkPolicy enforcement through the AntreaPolicy feature gate and provides per-policy and per-rule packet and byte count statistics through the NetworkPolicyStats capability, giving administrators visibility into which policies are actively restricting traffic. For VKS clusters using NSX VPC networking, NSX-backed network policies apply distributed ACLs at the hypervisor level, extending enforcement beyond the CNI layer. The ovn-kubernetes Transparent network mode similarly supports distributed ACLs applied via Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resources. For Layer 7 traffic control within VKS clusters, the Istio service mesh standard package provides a pluggable policy layer and configuration API supporting access controls, rate limits, and quotas based on workload identity; when deployed in ambient mode with strict mutual TLS, all service-to-service traffic within the mesh is subject to authentication before authorization checks apply, restricting lateral movement to explicitly permitted paths.

For file-level and storage data flow isolation, the Supervisor uses ACLs to isolate file share access within supervisor namespaces, restricting data access between workloads running in different namespaces. vSAN file services support managing ACLs on SMB shares using the MMC tool, giving administrators granular control over which users and systems can access shared storage resources. Administrators should be aware that ACL-based file share isolation in the Supervisor carries a risk of IP spoofing, which should be addressed through complementary network controls when deploying file shares in multi-tenant environments. Argo CD deployments on the Supervisor implement network segmentation and firewall rules to control communication between Argo CD components, following the principle of restricting inter-component traffic to only what each component requires. The Harbor container registry standard package enforces role-based access control over artifact access, complementing network-layer ACLs with image-level access restrictions.

For object-level access control, VCF Automation maintains an ACL table that defines which users can access specific defined entities. System administrators with the Manage General ACL right can create, modify, and remove ACL entries through the accessControls API. Those with the View General ACL right can audit ACL assignments on specific entities. Access to runtime defined entities is controlled through rights bundles and ACL entries. This ACL mechanism works alongside role-based access controls so that rights determine what actions a user can perform while ACLs determine which objects those actions apply to.

VCF Operations for Networks supports ACL visibility through its network assurance and verification capabilities, which allow administrators to validate that ACL configurations are applied as intended across the managed environment. This includes the ability to verify ACL configurations on supported physical network equipment such as Cisco ASR routers, helping validate that physical and virtual network access controls are consistent.

For workload-level traffic enforcement with stateful firewall rules, microsegmentation, and deep packet inspection, VMware vDefend and Gateway Firewall extend VCF's networking foundation with fine-grained Layer 2-7 policy enforcement. Organizations that require ACL governance beyond what VCF provides natively should establish processes for ACL review cycles, change management for firewall rules, and regular validation of both physical and virtual network access controls.

#### VMware vDefend (Meets)

VMware vDefend provides the primary data flow enforcement mechanism within VCF through its Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, and Security Intelligence capabilities. These capabilities function as software-defined access control lists governing traffic between workloads, segments, and external networks.

The vDefend distributed firewall enforces Layer 2-7 access policies at the vNIC level on every workload. Administrators define firewall rules that explicitly permit or deny traffic based on source and destination groups, ports, protocols, application identity, user identity via Active Directory integration through Identity Firewall, and fully qualified domain names (FQDN filtering). Because enforcement happens at the workload's virtual network interface, traffic is filtered before it reaches the physical network, and policies follow the workload as it moves between hosts.
